title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for LLM inference rather than training. The companies say the design targets higher performance and efficiency at scale, extending OpenAI's push to control its own compute stack and reduce dependence on Nvidia GPUs for serving its models.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant identified a threat actor exploiting a zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager to escalate a compromised admin account to root via a malicious CSV upload. The attacker first gained access through rogue peering connections, then ran extensive anti-forensic cleanup, deleting files and reverting configuration changes to purge indicators of the intrusion.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake instructions about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware code comments to derail AI-based security analysis. The text sits in a JavaScript comment that runtimes skip, but it can trigger refusals, prompt confusion, or premature classification in LLM-first triage systems that feed raw file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Researchers at Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration that lets any unauthenticated user with a free account forge approvals, push code, or steal credentials by exploiting workflows that grant untrusted pull requests excessive permissions. A scan of roughly 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 fully exploitable, including projects at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare, where the flaw enables code execution, token theft, and supply-chain compromise.

title: Jalapeño: OpenAI's new Chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, OpenAI's first custom inference chip, built specifically for LLM workloads and designed from scratch to production in nine months. Early testing shows substantially better performance per watt than current state-of-the-art accelerators, and OpenAI plans to deploy the chip at gigawatt scale with data center partners across multiple generations, extending its stack from models and products into silicon.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 for root access months before disclosure, Mandiant found.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter obtained by The Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against Claude, attempting to copy the model's outputs to train rival systems. The accusation escalates tensions over model distillation, a technique where one company uses a competitor's outputs to cheaply replicate its capabilities.

title: Binance tells EU customers that it will stop providing services for them from July 1, after Greece rejected its application for a bloc-wide license last week (Financial Times)
blurb: Binance told EU customers it will stop serving them starting July 1, after Greece rejected its application for a bloc-wide license under the MiCA crypto regulation last week. The decision cuts off the world's largest crypto exchange from the EU market, forcing affected users to move funds or close positions and signaling that MiCA approval has become a hard gate for operating across the bloc.

title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7 angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm manufacturing technology. The node uses new nanostack transistors that bond two wafers and stack n-type and p-type devices vertically, delivering up to 50% higher performance and 70% better energy efficiency than IBM's 2nm node, plus roughly double the transistor density.

title: Binance tells EU users it will no longer provide services after failing to secure MiCA license
blurb: Binance told users across France, Italy, Poland, and Spain that it will suspend services because it will not hold a MiCA license by the July 1 deadline, when crypto firms need authorization from at least one EU member state to operate across the bloc. The exchange has halted new EU registrations and withdrew its Greek application, but says customer assets stay accessible and it plans to seek a license in France.

title: Protocol Prying: Systematic Vulnerability Research in the Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share Proximity Transfer Protocols
blurb: Researchers performed the first cross-platform reverse engineering and protocol-aware fuzzing study of Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share, the proximity file-transfer stacks running on over five billion devices. They found six vulnerabilities, including three pre-authentication AirDrop flaws in macOS/iOS, two protocol-layer issues in Samsung Quick Share, and a bounty-earning heap use-after-free in Google Quick Share for Windows, all disclosed and acknowledged by the vendors.

title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models
blurb: This paper shows that multi-model systems like routing, voting, and mixture-of-agents have a hard accuracy ceiling set by beta, the rate at which every model fails the same query, which the usual pairwise error-correlation metric cannot detect. Across 67 models from 21 providers, the all-wrong tail is roughly 2.5 times larger than standard models predict, and on checkable tasks combining models rarely beats the single best one without a strong query-level routing signal.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Researchers at Zafran Security disclosed DifyTap, a set of four vulnerabilities in the open source LLMOps platform Dify that let attackers read other tenants' private chats, files, and documents in multi-tenant deployments. Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, patched all four flaws in version 1.14.2, and the two most severe carry CVSS scores of 9.1 and 9.4.

title: Unlocking the Cloudflare app ecosystem with OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all developers, letting them register their own OAuth clients for delegated, scoped access to the Cloudflare API. The change replaces hard-to-manage API tokens for third-party integrations and ships alongside upgrades to consent, revocation, and app-ownership visibility aimed at SaaS tools, internal platforms, and agentic apps.

title: IBM details major chip breakthrough with new sub-1nm ‘nanostack’ 3D architecture
blurb: IBM detailed a sub-1nm "nanostack" 3D chip architecture at the 0.7nm node, the first known three-dimensional nanosheet-based design, built by vertically stacking and staggering transistors with different materials in each layer. The chips pack roughly 100 billion transistors at nearly double the density of IBM's 2nm process, delivering up to 50% more performance or 70% greater energy efficiency for AI and cloud workloads.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers trained reinforcement learning and diffusion models to design radio-frequency integrated circuits from scratch, producing record-performance layouts that abandon the human-readable symmetry RFIC engineers rely on. The approach compresses a design process that normally takes expert engineers months into far less time, and could accelerate the RF chips underpinning 5G, satellite links, and autonomous vehicles.

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
blurb: A peer-reviewed critique in Nature argues that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana quantum claims rest on flawed tune-up software and coding errors that produced incorrect statements to reviewers. Author Henry Legg of the University of St Andrews says omitted raw data points to device disorder incompatible with a topological gap, and estimates Microsoft is centuries, not years, from a working topological quantum computer.

title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
blurb: Citizen Lab reported that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it had stopped selling to Russia. The case, backed by both on-device traces and Russia's own forensic report, shows how export cutoffs can lag actual field use and how seized-device forensics drive political prosecutions.

title: The HTTP QUERY Method
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, defining the HTTP QUERY method for sending a request body that the server processes safely and idempotently before returning a result. QUERY fills the gap between GET, which cannot carry a body for complex queries, and POST, which is unsafe to retry, letting clients send large search payloads that can be automatically repeated or cached.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea 2 is a series of foundation image-generation models built for creative exploration across styles rather than a single default aesthetic.

title: Framework's 10G Ethernet module exposes USB-C's complexity
blurb: Jeff Geerling tested WisdPi's new 10G Ethernet Expansion Card for Framework laptops and found USB-C's tangled bandwidth tiers cap real-world speeds well below the rated 10 Gbps. The Realtek RTL8159 controller needs USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 to hit 9.4 Gbps, but many Framework ports negotiate slower modes, Linux driver support is broken on newer kernels, and the module ran near 70°C under sustained load.

title: Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable
blurb: This paper argues that hallucination in generative world models concentrates in low-coverage regions of the state-action space, making it predictable from lightweight data signals. The authors release MMBench2, a 427-hour benchmark, identify three distinct failure modes, and use their hallucination predictors as curiosity rewards to adapt a 350M-parameter model to unseen environments with as few as 50 real trajectories.

title: The White House is asking OpenAI to slow roll the release of its new model over safety concerns
blurb: OpenAI will limit the release of GPT-5.6 to a small group of vetted partners after the Trump administration asked it to hold back over cybersecurity concerns, according to The Information. Sam Altman reportedly told staff that the government will approve access customer by customer during a preview period, mirroring the restricted rollout Anthropic adopted for its frontier cyber model Claude Mythos.

title: OpenAI will delay GPT-5.6 after Trump administration request
blurb: OpenAI will release GPT-5.6 in limited preview to a small set of enterprise customers after the Trump administration, citing security concerns, asked it to stagger the launch and approve customer access case by case. The terms are softer than those given to Anthropic, which this month faced an export-control directive suspending access to its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models for non-US nationals.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are now exploiting Cisco Unified CM's critical SSRF flaw (CVE-2026-20230), weeks after Cisco's June 3 patch warned it could grant root access.

title: Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks
blurb: Google attributed a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY to Russian state-sponsored group Turla, citing its use against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to Italian foreign policy. The multi-component implant communicates over secure WebSocket and shares significant code with Kazuar, a Turla staple since 2017, with suspected development tracing back to December 2022.

title: The US government asks OpenAI to slow its next model’s release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of GPT-5.6, first limiting it to a short list of trusted partners with access approved customer by customer during a preview period. The request, framed as a cybersecurity concern by the Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy, follows a similar directive against Anthropic two weeks earlier and marks the first time Washington has preemptively restricted a US AI launch.

title: LuaJIT 3.0 proposed syntax extensions
blurb: LuaJIT's maintainer opened an umbrella issue proposing optional syntax extensions for the 3.0 release, with backwards compatibility as a hard constraint.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI inference startup behind the MAX platform and the Mojo programming language founded by former Apple and Google engineer Chris Lattner. The deal gives Qualcomm a unified software layer for running models across hardware, strengthening its position against Nvidia's CUDA as it pushes deeper into data-center and edge AI compute.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers found a macOS technique that lets standard users disable EDR and MDM tools without admin credentials or kernel exploits.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis models can be manipulated into revealing whose data trained them, exposing patients through membership inference attacks. The finding highlights a privacy gap in healthcare AI, where models built on sensitive records can leak whether a specific individual was part of the training set.

title: Microsoft gives Windows 10 users an unexpected extra year of free security updates
blurb: Microsoft extended free Windows 10 security updates by an additional year, pushing its consumer Extended Security Updates program to October 12, 2027. The change appeared quietly in updated documentation rather than a formal announcement, giving users more time to migrate to Windows 11 through Windows Backup sync, 1,000 Microsoft Rewards points, or a $30 license covering up to 10 devices.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba must be punished for largest Claude cloning attack
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba and its Qwen AI lab of running the largest campaign yet to clone Claude, generating 28.8 million exchanges through roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5 to extract agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon capabilities. In a letter to senators, Anthropic said the attacks used proxy networks and obfuscation and continued after Trump's April crackdown on Chinese AI distillation, calling for Alibaba to be punished.

title: Anthropic's Mythos Model Found Vulnerabilities in Classified US Government Systems, Official Says
blurb: A U.S. official told the AP that Anthropic's Mythos model identified vulnerabilities in highly sensitive, classified government systems within hours during a joint testing exercise with U.S. intelligence agencies. The tests ran under Anthropic's Project Glasswing initiative, and the disclosure comes as the administration restricts foreign access to Mythos and its limited Fable variant, citing cybersecurity concerns.

title: Mostly Automatic Translation of Language Interpreters from C to Safe Rust
blurb: Reboot is a mostly-automatic system that translates real-world C language interpreters into safe Rust, converting six interpreters of 6k to 23k lines with only 1 to 11 manual interventions each. Translations pass 100% of provided test suites and 62% to 92% of held-out validation tests, and a mujs case study confirms that heap buffer overflows and use-after-free bugs are eliminated in the safe Rust output.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: At Build 2026, Microsoft introduced Coreutils for Windows, a fork of the Rust-based uutils package that brings standard Unix commands like ls and rm natively to the platform. The single-binary preview lets developers run the same syntax across WSL, Linux VMs, and native Windows shells, and makes Unix shell scripts portable to Windows, though it still conflicts with some existing cmd and PowerShell commands.

title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
blurb: Microsoft extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program for consumer devices by another year, pushing the deadline to October 12, 2027. The change arrived without a formal announcement, surfacing only in updated ESU documentation and an editor's note, and gives users still on Windows 10 more time before migrating to Windows 11 or paying $30 for continued coverage.

title: Washington’s Foreign Ban on Anthropic’s Top Models May Backfire
blurb: The White House restricted foreign access to Anthropic's top models, citing national security, but the policy may push overseas buyers toward Chinese alternatives. Analysts argue the ban could boost demand for China's open-source AI, handing rivals like DeepSeek and Alibaba a tailwind in markets the US is trying to keep ahead in.

title: Italy is investigating Microsoft 365's price hike, saying Microsoft failed to inform users that AI tools like Copilot were being integrated into the service (Giulia Segreti/Reuters)
blurb: Italy's antitrust authority opened an investigation into Microsoft 365's price increase, alleging Microsoft failed to tell users that AI tools like Copilot were being bundled into the service. The probe centers on whether the integration justified the higher cost or amounted to an unfair commercial practice, adding to the regulatory scrutiny Microsoft faces over how it packages AI across its products.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe told Wall Street on its Q2 FY2026 call that it is deferring roughly $500M in planned Creative Cloud price increases, breaking a decade-long pattern of clockwork annual hikes. The move came alongside record revenue of $6.62B, up 13% year over year, but the stock is down about 38% year to date as investors read the deferral as the first crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing set for July 10th, with all proceeds funding new memory fabs, advanced packaging, and EUV tool orders. The money targets its Y1 fab in Yongin and a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, neither of which reaches volume output until 2027, meaning the raise does nothing to ease the DRAM and HBM shortage still pushing prices up.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics, the Oregon maker of the two-legged Digit warehouse robot, is going public through a SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI that values it at $2.5B and provides more than $620M in cash. The deal makes Agility the first US public company focused solely on humanoid robots, though early filings withhold revenue and show it burned roughly $100M in 2025 while remaining unprofitable.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal worth up to $6.3B with SpaceX, paying $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The contract is the open-weight lab's first and trails SpaceX's larger deals with Anthropic and Google, underscoring how the rocket maker has turned xAI's stalled chip stockpile into a rental business for frontier labs.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown managed batch system with Kueue, a cloud-native job queueing system for Kubernetes batch workloads. The post covers why the team migrated millions of batch jobs to Kueue as part of moving its Titus container platform to be more Kubernetes-native, and how the switch let it drop bespoke per-tenant capacity and priority code in favor of an ecosystem component.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
blurb: Résumé prompt injection reliably boosts rankings only when manipulation is rare and candidate quality is similar, collapsing when many candidates inject.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: Founding a UG & Co. KG in Germany cost this engineer €9,600 and 152 days, yet five months in he still can't send a single invoice because his VAT ID hasn't arrived. The author details a timeline where a law firm, notary, two courts, a tax firm, and software vendors all billed him on schedule, while the one function the state exists to enable, clean invoicing, remains blocked.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm shares jumped 14% after-hours after the company laid out an AI roadmap that includes acquiring inference-software startup Modular for $3.92 billion in stock, previewing two data center chips, and raising its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue target to $40 billion. Modular's platform automates porting AI models across chips, which Qualcomm could use to make switching from Nvidia accelerators easier and boost adoption of its new Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator.

title: Where Do Models Find Happiness? Emotion Vectors in Open-Source LLMs
blurb: Emotion vectors found in Claude Sonnet 4.5 replicate in two open-weight LLMs, with valence geometry emerging at different layer depths.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a cursor-tracking Magic Pointer in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside early signs of cross-device linking. The features would let Gemini act on whatever a user is doing across apps rather than inside a single window, pushing the assistant toward the always-on desktop agent role Microsoft and OpenAI are also chasing.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators linked to Alibaba and its Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fake accounts to harvest outputs for adversarial distillation, calling it the largest such attack it has tracked. The April-to-June campaign targeted Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning and exceeded Anthropic's February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI and MiniMax combined.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful AI model, according to a person familiar with the matter. The request comes nearly two weeks after rival Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings from the market under regulatory pressure, signaling that Washington is now shaping the pace at which frontier models reach customers.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together account for 74% of its wafer revenue. The reported 5% to 10% increases would raise wafer costs for Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek, with some already rolling out and others folded into future purchase orders.

title: OpenAI staggers GPT-5.6 rollout for government vetting, eyes 2027 IPO
blurb: OpenAI will roll out GPT-5.6 to a small set of partners under government vetting rather than releasing it publicly, after the Trump administration asked for a customer-by-customer review of frontier models. The same approach followed Anthropic's Mythos, which Washington forced offline this month, while OpenAI separately leans toward a 2027 IPO rather than filing this year.

title: Apple Shares Fall After Prices Increase for Macs, iPads
blurb: Apple raised prices across all Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro to offset a memory and storage chip shortage, sending its shares down. The increases took effect globally and are largely unprecedented, with no equivalent sweeping hike across this much of the product line in the company's modern history.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across an entire channel. The tool monitors channel activity, sends preset alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drops comments into conversations, and can be directed to fix code issues, pushing Claude further into persistent workplace-agent territory.

title: SpaceX launches secretive Starfall reentry demo mission
blurb: SpaceX launched the first test flight of its Starfall reentry capsule on June 23, a vehicle the company describes as a microgravity lab for in-space manufacturing and point-to-point cargo delivery. SpaceX ran the flight like a classified mission, withholding upper-stage video and confirming only that Starfall deployed, without saying whether the 2,100-kilogram spacecraft survived its Pacific reentry and splashdown.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom debut custom Jalapeño chip for AI inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom debuted Jalapeño, a custom inference-only chip that OpenAI says delivers significantly higher performance per watt than current state-of-the-art accelerators by reducing data movement between logic and memory. The chip extends Broadcom's custom-silicon record from Google's TPU line and will run in custom server racks alongside Broadcom Tomahawk 6 networking, signaling OpenAI's bid to cut its dependence on Nvidia for inference.

title: How a Niche Technology Became a Choke Point for A.I.
blurb: Advanced chip packaging, the technology that stacks and connects silicon to boost AI compute, has concentrated in Taiwan and deepened US dependence on TSMC. The bottleneck has shifted from chip fabrication to packaging capacity, giving Taiwan fresh leverage even as American firms race to build domestic alternatives.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees, offering free hosting for up to 500 domains with no query limits or per-request billing.

title: Augmentation with Dilution: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Human Contributor Ecosystems After AI Coding Agent Adoption
blurb: A study of 11,097 GitHub repositories from 2023 to 2026 finds that adopting AI coding agents leaves the absolute number of human contributors unchanged but reduces their relative density, with newcomers' share of participation dropping 3.7 percentage points immediately after adoption. The authors call this "augmentation with dilution": agents shift effort from writing code to reviewing it, deepening review by 5.3% while quietly reshaping who participates in open-source projects.

title: [AINews] OpenAI reports median internal Codex output tokens grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal since November 2025.
blurb: OpenAI's economic research team reported that median internal Codex output tokens grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal between November 2025 and June 2026. The data shows AI usage deepening fastest outside coding, a shift OpenAI frames as evidence that even employees with unlimited access had badly underused the tools through late 2025.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm acquired AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push deeper into the data center inference market. The deal, expected to close in the second half of 2026, gives Qualcomm a developer-friendly platform for running AI across diverse compute environments as enterprises hunt for ways to cut soaring token costs.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, displacing U.S. machines from the top of the list. The result marks China's return to the public ranking after years of withholding submissions, signaling intensifying competition over high-performance computing capacity that underpins AI and scientific research.

title: Anthropic’s Claude is winning over paid consumers, a market owned by ChatGPT
blurb: Anthropic's Claude is gaining ground on ChatGPT among paying consumers, with credit-card transaction data from Indagari showing Claude's consumer revenue up roughly 75% since January 2026. The trend suggests Anthropic has a broader customer base than its enterprise-and-developer reputation implies, though ChatGPT still holds a commanding lead in paying users and overall consumer reach.

title: Sources: SpaceX COO Gwynne Shotwell told investors during an IPO roadshow SpaceX may launch a Starlink mobile product and build its own terrestrial US network (Financial Times)
blurb: SpaceX COO Gwynne Shotwell told investors on an IPO roadshow that SpaceX may launch a Starlink mobile product and build its own terrestrial US network, according to the Financial Times. The move would put Starlink in direct competition with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile, extending its satellite service into a full consumer wireless offering ahead of a planned public listing.

title: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until 2027; expectations of a windfall from OpenAI's debut helped support Softbank's stock (Aya Wagatsuma/Bloomberg)
blurb: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until 2027. The stock had been buoyed by expectations of a windfall from OpenAI's public debut, leaving SoftBank exposed as its largest AI bet slips further out on the timeline.

title: SpaceX wants to sell Starlink phone service directly to US consumers
blurb: SpaceX told investors it plans to launch a Starlink mobile service sold directly to US consumers, putting it in direct competition with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile, the Financial Times reported. The move would replace its current wholesale arrangement with T-Mobile and lean on the $17B of EchoStar spectrum licenses it acquired last year, letting SpaceX capture more revenue per user from a market measured in hundreds of millions of subscribers.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, with advisers urging Sam Altman to slow down after watching SpaceX's stock turn volatile. The caution comes as the company works through financial challenges, suggesting public markets remain wary of richly valued AI startups despite OpenAI's growth.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of high-capability "covered models" to report major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days of discovery. Reportable events would include models evading human oversight, resisting shutdown, enabling cyberattacks, or accelerating chemical and biological weapons development, with the Secretary of Commerce tasked to set the capability thresholds defining which systems qualify.

title: OpenAI will initially only release ChatGPT 5.6 to government-approved customers
blurb: OpenAI will stagger the release of ChatGPT 5.6, initially limiting access to customers approved individually by federal leaders during a preview period before a wider rollout a couple of weeks later. The move follows a Trump executive order asking AI labs to submit powerful models for voluntary federal review, though Altman told staff this gated approach is not OpenAI's preferred long-term model.

title: Show HN: OpenKnowledge – open source AI-first alternative to Obsidian/Notion
blurb: OpenKnowledge is an open source, local-first markdown editor and LLM wiki that positions itself as an AI-first alternative to Obsidian and Notion. It offers full WYSIWYG editing, collaborative AI editing with Claude, Codex, and Cursor via MCP or CLI, and git-backed team sharing, available as a macOS app or as a local web app on Linux, Windows, and Intel Mac.

title: Patronus AI lands $50M to build ‘digital worlds’ that stress-test AI agents
blurb: Patronus AI raised $50M in a Series B led by Greenfield Partners to build simulated "digital worlds" that stress-test AI agents before deployment. The startup, founded by former Meta researchers, creates replicas of websites and internal systems where agents are tested via reinforcement learning, with revenue up 15-fold over the past year and most frontier labs now customers.

title: a16z-backed Base Power is offering cheaper electricity to the power grid that needs it most
blurb: Base Power began selling its 25kWh-plus home batteries in Illinois, its first move into PJM Interconnection territory, where surging data center demand has nearly doubled wholesale electricity prices over the past year. The startup undercuts utility ComEd's rates by 25% and sidesteps PJM's clogged interconnection queue by installing behind the meter, building a residential virtual power plant after raising $1B led by Addition in October.

title: Microsoft adds another year to Windows 10 extended update program
blurb: Microsoft extended its Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program by another year, pushing the end date from October 12, 2026 to October 12, 2027. The quiet policy change, confirmed via a blog post editor's note, reflects how slow Windows 11 adoption has been, echoing Microsoft's repeated support extensions for Windows XP throughout the 2010s.

title: Notion Mail shuts down amid agent takeover
blurb: Notion is shutting down its email client, Notion Mail, on September 22, redirecting users to its AI agents to manage their inboxes instead. The company said more than half of Notion Mail users already handle email without ever opening their inbox, and agents that run email workflows will keep working after the shutdown. Notion built the product on its 2024 acquisition of Skiff.

title: Musk Starts Expanding X Payments to More Users After Delay
blurb: Musk began rolling out X Money, the platform's digital payments service, to a subset of premium X users after repeated delays. The launch advances Musk's plan to turn X into an "everything app" that bundles social media, messaging, and financial services, putting it in competition with PayPal and Cash App.

title: Memory maker SK hynix files for $29B US IPO amid AI demand
blurb: SK hynix, the world's largest supplier of HBM memory, filed to list on Nasdaq and aims to raise up to $29.4 billion, which would be the second-largest IPO on record after SpaceX. The South Korean company produces more than half the world's HBM, the high-bandwidth RAM that feeds AI chips like Nvidia's Rubin, and posted a 77% net margin on revenue that jumped 198% year-over-year to $38 billion in Q1.

title: ON Semiconductor strikes $7 billion deal for Synaptics in physical AI push
blurb: ON Semiconductor agreed to acquire Synaptics in a nearly $7B all-stock deal, its largest ever, to expand into physical AI and connected compute. ON Semi says the deal lifts its addressable market by $30B to $243B by 2030, though its shares fell about 6% after hours while Synaptics rallied roughly 13%.

title: Italy opens an antitrust probe into Microsoft 365’s AI price rise
blurb: Italy's competition authority opened an investigation into Microsoft over how it raised Microsoft 365 prices, alleging customers were automatically moved to a costlier Copilot-bundled plan unless they opted out. The probe targets the opt-out design rather than the price itself, framing the bundled AI upgrade as a consumer-consent issue ahead of the increase taking effect on 1 July 2026.

title: Liquid AI's smallest model yet LFM2.5-230M beats models 4X its size at data extraction, can run 'anywhere'
blurb: Liquid AI released LFM2.5-230M, a 230-million-parameter model that outperforms Alibaba's Qwen3.5-0.8B and Google's Gemma 3 1B on data extraction benchmarks despite being a fraction of their size. Built on the LFM2 hybrid architecture for on-device agentic workflows, it runs on phones, laptops, and robotics, and stays free for individuals and companies under $10M in annual revenue.

title: Trump Plans to Name Adam Candeub as Antitrust Chief in Justice Dept.
blurb: Trump plans to nominate Adam Candeub, a telecommunications lawyer and vocal tech critic, to lead the Justice Department's antitrust division. The pick signals continued aggressive enforcement against large tech platforms, putting companies already facing federal scrutiny on notice that the administration intends to keep pressure on Big Tech consolidation and market power.

title: Analog chipmaker Onsemi buys Synaptics in $7B all-stock deal to push into physical AI
blurb: Onsemi agreed to acquire Synaptics in an all-stock deal valued at about $7B, paying 1.35 Onsemi shares per Synaptics share to expand into "physical AI" and edge computing. The acquisition pairs Onsemi's analog and silicon carbide power chips with Synaptics' sensing, touch, and human-interface technology, targeting robots, drones, and autonomous vehicles, though Onsemi's stock fell more than 8% on the news.

title: “MS 마요라나 양자 칩 성과, 섣부른 결론”…학계서 비판 제기
blurb: A peer-reviewed Nature paper by St. Andrews physicist Henry Legg argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol, the framework behind its Majorana quantum chip claims, is flawed and that the observed signals could stem from other physical effects or biased data selection. Microsoft stands by its roadmap to a scalable quantum computer by 2029, but Legg notes the company has not fully released raw data for independent verification.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding, backed by Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, HV Capital, Cathay Innovation, and Hiro Capital. The venture, which LeCun launched after leaving Meta following 12 years leading its AI research, will focus on world models and has named former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun as CEO.

title: Researchers cast new doubt on Microsoft’s quantum computing advance
blurb: A peer-reviewed Nature paper by Dr. Henry Legg of the University of St Andrews argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol, the analysis framework underpinning its Majorana qubit claims, is flawed and that the experimental data could be explained by other effects. The critique adds to long-standing skepticism over Microsoft's 2025 Majorana 1 chip and its goal of a scalable quantum computer by 2029.

title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
blurb: Apple released Xcode 26.6, adding Google Gemini as a coding assistant option alongside Anthropic's Claude Agent and OpenAI Codex. The update also enables other compatible agents through the Agent Client Protocol and ships Swift 6.3.3 plus SDKs for the iOS 26.5 family, giving developers their choice of frontier model without leaving the IDE.

title: Meta's first in-house AI glasses designs include a Kylie Jenner collaboration
blurb: Meta released its first in-house-designed AI smart glasses, the Meta Glasses line, spanning three styles including a Meta Glasses model co-designed with Kylie Jenner. Built with EssilorLuxottica and running Meta's Muse Spark model, the frames pack dual 100-degree cameras, open-ear speakers, six microphones, and 20-language translation, but drop the in-lens screen from last year's Ray-Ban Display collaboration.

title: Ending respiratory infections
blurb: Intercept launched as a $500M philanthropic initiative to fund broad-spectrum preventatives and air-cleaning technologies that could make respiratory infections like colds and flu rare. The effort targets a gap that fits neither philanthropic nor commercial funding, betting that new protein-design tools, immunology data, and platform technologies can finally tackle hundreds of mutating viral strains at once.

title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
blurb: The author pointed a pure-Go git server (go-git) at a Tigris object storage bucket by reusing billy, the filesystem abstraction go-git expects, so the bucket could serve git operations without any /usr/bin/git binary. The post doubles as a clear walk through git's storage internals, showing that a repository is just objects, trees, commits, and refs, and that git stores whole files rather than diffs.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Mirendil, a San Francisco startup founded by former Anthropic researchers Behnam Neyshabur and Harsh Mehta, raised $200M at a $1B valuation in one of the largest seed rounds in AI history. Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins co-led the round with NVIDIA participating, betting on the pair's goal of building AI that automates AI research itself, though the company has yet to ship a product.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported Q3 revenue of $41.46B, more than quadrupling from $9.3B a year ago and beating Wall Street's $35.84B target, driven by AI-fueled memory demand. Gross margin hit 84.9%, the highest among major US tech companies, and Micron guided to roughly $50B in current-quarter revenue as memory shortages persist into 2028.

title: CLIR: Liveness-Driven and Structure-Aware Fuzzing for the Cranelift Compiler
blurb: CLIR is a differential fuzzing framework for the Cranelift compiler that found 24 bugs across four architectures in 72 hours, 8x more than cranelift-fuzzgen.

title: Slate EV truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate Auto opened orders for its electric pickup starting at $24,950, undercutting every EV truck on the market and most gas trucks. The Blank Slate ships as a bare two-seat pickup that converts to a five-seat SUV, charges from a standard 120V wall outlet, supports DIY repair with swappable panels and free manuals, and taps Tesla's Supercharger network.

title: Micron is tech's new margin king as memory crisis pushes company past Nvidia and Meta
blurb: Micron posted a record 84.9% gross margin in its fiscal third quarter, the highest among major US tech companies, topping Meta at 81.9% and Nvidia at 75% as AI-driven memory demand sends prices soaring. Revenue hit $41.46 billion and net income reached $28.24 billion, while the stock is up over 700% in the past year, pushing Micron's market cap past $1 trillion.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, entering a market already contested by about a dozen startups building return vehicles for in-orbit manufacturing. Starfall carries roughly 1 metric ton and is about 3x the size of Varda's capsules, but lacks in-orbit maneuverability and precise landing guidance, leading rival founders to read the move as validation of demand rather than a threat.

title: An entire Herculaneum scroll has been read for the first time
blurb: Researchers on the Vesuvius Challenge completely virtually unwrapped and read PHerc. 1667, the first carbonized Herculaneum scroll deciphered end to end without physically opening it. The team scanned the sealed roll with high-resolution X-rays, reconstructed and flattened the wound sheet, and used machine learning to recover the faint ink, revealing a Stoic ethical treatise from the 2nd century BC.

title: Half-Life 2 in a Browser
blurb: A WebAssembly port of Half-Life 2 that runs the full game in the browser with no installation.

title: Minimus container images are now free
blurb: Minimus now offers its hardened, security-configured container images free, deployable via curated Helm charts, with no support or guaranteed patching on the free tier.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix Reportedly Preparing Huge AI Spending Push
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix are preparing to announce hundreds of billions of dollars in new AI-related investments on Monday, according to South Korean media reports. The combined spending push from the world's two largest memory chipmakers would expand capacity for high-bandwidth memory and advanced packaging, the components most constrained by surging demand for AI accelerators.

title: Un-0: Generating Images with Coupled Oscillators
blurb: Unconventional AI released Un-0, an open-source image generator that runs on a simulated system of coupled oscillators rather than standard deep networks on GPUs. The model hits FID 6.74 on ImageNet 64×64, matching early versions of leading conventional generators, as a proof of concept for physical computing substrates the company claims could eventually cut AI energy use by roughly 1,000x.

title: Meta plans to replace 90% of content review staff with AI
blurb: Meta plans to replace up to 90% of its content review staff with AI, having already automated half of that moderation work, per Axios. The shift comes shortly after a recent Instagram breach and will deepen by year's end, raising questions about whether automated systems can handle sensitive safety calls human reviewers currently make.

title: How agents are transforming work
blurb: OpenAI published research on how AI agents are reshaping work, arguing they can now handle longer and more complex multi-step tasks across a range of roles. The paper frames agents as expanding individual productivity rather than simply automating discrete tasks, a positioning that supports OpenAI's push to move enterprise customers from chatbot pilots to deployed agentic systems.

title: Qualcomm claims it's not too late for Dragonfly to land in datacenters
blurb: Qualcomm argued that it is not too late to bring its Dragonfly chips into the datacenter, despite a market already dominated by Nvidia, AMD, and Arm-based incumbents. The company is betting that demand for AI and inference capacity leaves room for a new entrant, positioning Dragonfly as its push back into server silicon after earlier datacenter efforts stalled.

title: Microsoft rivals line up to tell UK watchdog where the software behemoth hurt them
blurb: Microsoft's competitors are submitting complaints to the UK's Competition and Markets Authority, detailing how the company's cloud licensing practices have disadvantaged them in the market. The testimony feeds an ongoing CMA investigation into whether Microsoft uses Windows Server and Office licensing terms to steer customers toward Azure, a probe that could force changes to how the company prices software across rival clouds.

title: The 'papers, please' era of the internet will decimate your privacy
blurb: The author argues that age-verification laws are identity-verification mandates in disguise, forcing users to hand sensitive ID or biometric data to opaque third-party apps that may not protect it from breaches. Citing Australia's under-16 social media ban, which BMJ research found barely reduced teen usage while 70% kept posting, the piece warns the global push trades real privacy for unproven safety gains.

title: Perplexity Computer for Counsel
blurb: Perplexity launched Computer for Counsel, a version of its agentic Computer product that connects legal research databases, document repositories, and contract tools to automate research, document gathering, and contract triage. It runs on 20+ frontier models that the system selects per task and integrates premium legal sources including Midpage case law, Deel compliance data, and LegalZoom contract templates, targeting the 75% of lawyers who cite admin work as a major time drain.

title: Make an Origami Circuit Board
blurb: Researchers at City University of Hong Kong's MEI Lab developed a method to create circuit traces in paper by folding and cutting it, using a nonwoven textile impregnated with gallium-indium liquid metal. The pressure from origami and kirigami techniques, ranging from 2.5 to 100 megapascals, cracks an insulating oxide layer to form conductive paths, letting crafters embed LEDs and motors without adhesive copper tape or shaped molds.

title: Home Broadband Is 5G’s Surprise Killer App
blurb: Fixed wireless access, which delivers home broadband over 5G cellular towers instead of cable or fiber, now serves over 14 million U.S. customers and carries 28% of worldwide wireless traffic. Carriers repurpose surplus 5G capacity and a usage quirk, mobile traffic falls after 8 p.m. as home demand peaks, to undercut fiber deployment costs and reach rural areas more cheaply than satellite services like Starlink.

title: Instagram wants to monopolize your attention
blurb: Instagram launched a TV app for Amazon Fire TV, Google TV, and Samsung sets, adding disappearing Stories, horizontal YouTube-style videos, and casting on top of vertical Reels. The Meta-owned platform plans a push into longform episodic content and "live creator experiences," betting its 3 billion users will watch phone-native content on the couch as it chases YouTube and microdrama apps.

title: Cisco cuts 500 Bay Area jobs in aggressive pivot to AI
blurb: Cisco will cut 471 Bay Area jobs effective July 13, including 154 at its Milpitas campus, as part of a broader plan to eliminate 4,000 roles worldwide. CEO Chuck Robbins framed the reductions as a reshaping of Cisco's operating model around AI, with the company redirecting investment into silicon, optics, and security while pressing remaining staff to build AI into their workflows.

title: RAMageddon just got extremely real
blurb: Apple raised prices across Macs, iPads, HomePods, and the Vision Pro, with some models jumping hundreds of dollars as the global memory shortage drives up component costs. The hikes are notable because Apple's margins and purchasing scale usually let it absorb supply-chain swings, and they follow earlier RAM-driven price increases on game consoles, laptops, and Android phones.

title: OpenAI says 97.9% of its employees are now using Codex, up from ~40% in August 2025; non-developer usage of Codex has risen 137x for individual users (Thomas Claburn/The Register)
blurb: OpenAI said 97.9% of its employees now use Codex, up from roughly 40% in August 2025, with non-developer usage rising 137x for individual users. The figures point to the AI coding tool spreading well beyond engineering teams into roles that have never shipped production code.

title: SK Hynix surges 12% after Micron earnings; blockbuster Nasdaq listing
blurb: SK Hynix shares jumped more than 12% on Thursday after the South Korean memory maker filed to raise up to $29.4B in a Nasdaq ADR listing expected to begin trading July 10. The rally was reinforced by rival Micron's quarterly revenue more than quadrupling on AI memory demand, a read-across that lifted Samsung and TSMC and signaled that high-bandwidth memory supply stays constrained.

title: Pentagon Sees Bigger Role for AI in Setting Military Targets
blurb: The Pentagon quietly revised its targeting doctrine to allow AI a larger role in selecting military targets during combat. The change opens the door for automated systems to inform or make critical wartime decisions, escalating debate over how much lethal judgment should be delegated to algorithms.

title: Microsoft lifts price of Xbox consoles due to soaring component costs
blurb: Microsoft will raise Xbox console prices starting Aug. 1, pushing the 512GB Series S up $100 to about $500 and the entry-level Series X to roughly $750. The company blamed component costs, noting console memory and storage prices have risen more than 2.5x as Micron and SK Hynix prioritize high-bandwidth memory for AI infrastructure over consumer chips.

title: DeepSeek Looks to Double Workforce as Fresh Funding Fuels Growth
blurb: DeepSeek plans to roughly double its workforce, opening 27 technical roles including development engineers and AI product managers after raising fresh funding. The Hangzhou-based lab is making every position open to interns, a signal it is scaling research and product capacity to keep pace with larger US and Chinese AI competitors.

title: Warp, a startup using AI to automate payroll compliance and employee management, raised a $60M Series B led by Battery, bringing its total funding to $85M (Lucinda Shen/Axios)
blurb: Warp raised a $60M Series B led by Battery, bringing total funding to $85M for its AI platform that automates payroll, compliance, and employee management for startups. The round signals continued investor appetite for software that handles the back-office burden of HR and regulatory filings, a category where manual processes still dominate at smaller companies.

title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
blurb: Intel’s chip-manufacturing business is showing early signs of recovery after years of delays, losses, and market-share erosion to TSMC and AMD. The company is central to President Trump’s push to expand US chip production, though executives concede a full turnaround remains distant, with foundry profitability and external customers still unproven.

title: The New York Times Amends Lawsuit Against OpenAI and Microsoft
blurb: The New York Times amended its copyright lawsuit against OpenAI and Microsoft, accusing Microsoft of encouraging OpenAI to train its AI systems on copyrighted articles. The new filing expands the case beyond OpenAI's conduct to implicate its largest backer, sharpening a legal fight that could shape how courts treat training data sourced from paywalled journalism.

title: Nvidia and Samsung to power 6G-ready digital twin for Japan telecom giant
blurb: KDDI launched a joint initiative with Nvidia, Samsung, and Keysight to build a high-fidelity digital twin of its radio access network for the 6G era. The virtual replica lets operators train and validate AI optimization models against simulated radio conditions without touching live networks, with a prototype targeted for March 2028 and broader rollout by 2030.

title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley
blurb: The Economist argues that hundreds of "zombie unicorns" now haunt Silicon Valley, startups once valued above $1B that can no longer raise fresh capital, go public, or find buyers at their inflated marks. Their backers face a choice between writing down paper gains and propping up companies that have stopped growing, freezing capital that would otherwise flow to a new generation of startups.

title: Elastic lays off 7% of employees
blurb: Elastic is cutting roughly 7% of its workforce as CEO Ash Kulkarni restructures the company around AI and automation, flattening engineering into three core areas led by direct reports. Kulkarni framed the cuts as a reorganization rather than a retreat, saying Elastic will keep hiring in sales and expects total headcount to grow year-over-year this fiscal year.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed how its kernel release process left infrastructure unexposed to "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a Linux local privilege escalation bug disclosed on April 29 that abuses the AF_ALG crypto API. Most servers already ran patched 6.12 and 6.18 LTS kernels because the fix landed in stable releases weeks before public disclosure, and existing behavioral detections flagged the exploit pattern within minutes.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away, and urged leaders to act now. The agencies said frontier models will sharply increase the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks by lowering barriers for bad actors, framing cyber risk as a core business and leadership responsibility rather than a purely technical one.

title: Samsung’s $647bn home investment heads for the chip-starved southwest
blurb: Samsung is expected to announce a 1,000 trillion won ($647.5B) investment in South Korea over the next decade, with a reported 300 trillion won earmarked for new chip factories in the country's underdeveloped southwest. The pledge, set for a June 29 presidential event where rival SK Hynix will detail its own plans, spans AI data centers, batteries, and displays as much as semiconductors.

title: Ford execs say they made a mistake when they replaced human engineers with AI
blurb: Ford rehired about 350 veteran "gray beard" engineers after concluding that replacing them with AI was a mistake, with VP Charles Poon calling the move a major error. The returning specialists reprogram diagnostic and AI tools and hunt for defects before production, a shift Ford credits for jumping from 10th to the top mainstream brand in JD Power's latest quality survey.

title: A $2.5 Billion Whodunit: The Hack That Dented the U.K. Economy
blurb: Investigators now attribute last year's ransomware attack on Jaguar Land Rover to Russian operators, after a loose collective of cybercriminals first claimed credit for the breach. The disruption halted production for weeks and is estimated to have cost roughly $2.5 billion, making it one of the most economically damaging cyberattacks ever to hit the U.K.

title: They read the scroll thing! AI helps decipher ancient document charred by Vesuvius
blurb: Researchers used AI to read a Herculaneum papyrus scroll charred and sealed by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD, recovering text without physically unrolling the fragile carbonized roll. Machine learning models detected faint ink traces in high-resolution CT scans, extending the Vercelli Scrolls effort to decipher a library that has resisted reading for nearly two thousand years.

title: Parts of NSA lose Mythos 5 access after White House imposes limits
blurb: Parts of the NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 after the Trump administration imposed export controls on the company this month, citing national-security concerns. The restrictions, which also pulled back Fable 5, cut off the agency's red teams whose authority ran through Project Glasswing, the April program that gave select researchers early access to Mythos Preview for testing software weaknesses.

title: How the US AI build-out is pushing up prices for electricity, software, and more; in a survey, 81% of economists say it will add to inflation over the next year (Justin Lahart/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The US AI build-out is raising prices for electricity, software, and other inputs as data-center demand outpaces supply. In a Wall Street Journal survey, 81% of economists said the spending will add to inflation over the next year, complicating the case for near-term rate cuts.

title: AI Cost Reality Check Hits Asia Tech Stocks as Apple Hikes Price
blurb: Asian technology stocks fell after Apple and Microsoft raised prices on their products, raising concern that climbing component costs will dampen device demand. The selloff hit memory chipmakers hardest, since a slowdown in hardware sales threatens the memory rally that has underpinned much of the AI trade.

title: Anthropic's latest hiring spree reveals where it's building AI data centers next
blurb: Anthropic is racing to build AI data center capacity in the Asia-Pacific region, with eight of 13 open compute-team roles based in Australia or Japan covering deal sourcing, electrical engineering, and multi-hundred-megawatt energy procurement. The push follows a $65B raise at a $965B valuation and a $47B revenue run-rate, as surging consumer and enterprise usage strains infrastructure and pulls the company toward overseas expansion.

title: Notion killing Skiff-influenced email app since most users use AI agents instead
blurb: Notion will shut down Notion Mail across web, desktop, and iOS on September 22, ending the last remnant of its 2024 Skiff acquisition. The company says more than half of Notion Mail users already manage their inboxes through AI agents without opening the client, and existing email history will remain in Gmail.

title: Drone Startup Elroy Air Is Said to Near $800 Million SPAC Deal
blurb: Cargo drone startup Elroy Air is in advanced talks to go public through a SPAC merger that would value the combined company at roughly $1 billion in enterprise value. The deal, reportedly worth about $800 million, would fund Elroy's push to replace delivery trucks with autonomous aircraft as it moves from prototype to commercial operations.

title: York satellite demonstrates two-way UHF communications from low Earth orbit
blurb: York Space Systems' Dragoon satellite demonstrated two-way UHF tactical communications from low Earth orbit, completing five downlink and uplink tests over three months for the Space Force's Space Development Agency. The experiment tests whether UHF services traditionally delivered by geostationary satellites can move to smaller, closer spacecraft, and marks the first in-orbit run of York's larger LX-class platform.

title: Apple’s Macs and iPads and Microsoft’s Xbox consoles are getting more expensive — blame AI
blurb: Apple and Microsoft raised Mac, iPad, and Xbox prices 15-25% as AI server demand quadrupled memory and storage chip costs.

title: Qualcomm Targets Over $15 Billion in Data Center Revenue, Names Meta CPU Customer
blurb: Qualcomm told investors it expects more than $15B in data center revenue by fiscal 2029, and named Meta as the first public customer for its Dragonfly C1000 server CPUs. The multi-year deal puts the 250-core Oryon chips in Meta servers entering production in late 2028, part of a forecast that nearly doubles Qualcomm's non-handset chip target to $40B and sent shares up over 12% after hours.

title: Tumbling AI stocks signal another day of turmoil for tech companies
blurb: Tech stocks sold off Friday on mounting concern over rising AI infrastructure costs, with SoftBank plunging more than 12% and Apple shedding 6% after raising MacBook and iPad prices. The weakness spread across the global chip supply chain, hitting SK Hynix, Samsung, and TSMC, as investors weighed whether climbing semiconductor prices will squeeze margins at the largest tech companies.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier, was hit by a cyberattack in which an attacker claims to have stolen internal documents and trade secrets from both companies. The breach raises supply-chain security concerns for the iPhone assembler as it expands manufacturing in India, though Tata has not confirmed the scope or whether customer data was compromised.

title: Apple just raised the price of its products by hundreds of dollars
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac and iPad lineup, citing the steepest component cost spike it has ever seen, with the best-selling 13-inch M5 MacBook Air jumping from $1,099 to $1,299 and the Mac Studio M3 Ultra climbing $500 to $2,499. The company had absorbed the memory crunch through supplier deals longer than rivals, but the iPhone remains unchanged for now, with an iPhone 18 Pro increase already rumored for the fall.

title: How space weather could bust the AI boom
blurb: A severe solar storm could cripple the strained US electric grid by damaging high-voltage transformers that take months or years to replace, threatening the AI data centers now drawing as much power as small cities. The author argues that big tech, with the most to lose, should fund better space weather forecasting, which gives grid operators advance warning to limit catastrophic damage.

title: California launches tracker for AI-related job losses
blurb: California launched a public portal that tracks AI-related job losses statewide, built with the Employment Development Department and UC's California Policy Lab using unemployment claims data combined with AI exposure measures. Updated monthly and framed by Governor Newsom's office as an "early warning system," the tracker breaks down exposure by age, gender, industry, and region, though researchers caution it cannot confirm AI as the actual cause of any specific layoffs.

title: The Long-Term Threat to the Memory Chip Boom Is Innovation
blurb: Micron's outlook signals the memory-chip shortage will persist, but the WSJ argues the bigger long-term risk to suppliers is customer innovation. As prices climb, large memory buyers are developing workarounds that use chips more efficiently, which could erode demand once the current boom fades.

title: New Alexa+ Agentic Ads let shoppers purchase directly within an ad
blurb: Amazon introduced Alexa+ Agentic Ads, the first ad format that lets shoppers complete a purchase through conversation without leaving the ad. The format launches during Prime Day with Papa Johns for food ordering and artists Beck, Jill Scott, and Omar Courtz for Ticketmaster concert sales, with Amazon planning to add more brands over time.

title: US Govt to individually approve who gets GPT 5.6
blurb: A report that the US government would individually vet who can access GPT 5.6 drew heavy debate on r/LocalLLaMA.

title: TikTok and YouTube cut 4.7 million under-16 accounts in Indonesia
blurb: TikTok and YouTube deactivated roughly 4.7 million accounts belonging to children under 16 in Indonesia, with TikTok accounting for 4.1 million and YouTube 600,000, the country's communications minister said on June 25. The cuts follow a March rule requiring high-risk platforms to enforce a hard age threshold themselves, and figures from X, Instagram, and Roblox are still outstanding, making the total a first instalment rather than a final count.

title: Om Malik has died
blurb: Om Malik, the San Francisco-based writer, investor, and Gigaom founder, died on June 24, 2026, at Stanford Hospital following a long battle with heart disease. He spent two decades chronicling and shaping the early internet and venture worlds before turning to photography and personal essays, and was widely regarded as one of tech's most steadying and substantive voices.

title: Qwen-AgentWorld: Language World Models for General Agents
blurb: Alibaba's Qwen team released Qwen-AgentWorld, a pair of language world models (35B-A3B and 397B-A17B) that simulate agentic environments across 7 domains using long chain-of-thought reasoning, trained on over 10M real-world interaction trajectories. On the new AgentWorldBench, the models outperform frontier baselines, and using them as a decoupled environment simulator for agentic RL beats training in real environments alone.

title: Micron’s Blockbuster Earnings Quiet the AI Doubters
blurb: Micron reported quarterly earnings that beat expectations on strong demand for high-bandwidth memory used in AI accelerators, sending its shares sharply higher. The rally lifted Nasdaq futures and eased concerns that AI spending is cooling, with memory chips emerging as one of the clearest beneficiaries of the data-center buildout.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI-detection startup GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and tools that flag machine-written text, fake citations, and invented statistics, all of which Superhuman will fold into its Go assistant as part of an "authenticity layer" for verifying where content came from.

title: The Wild West era of robotaxis is starting to end
blurb: A UN vehicle standards forum adopted the first international framework for fully autonomous vehicles, setting a common safety baseline across the US, China, the EU, Japan, and Britain ahead of a January 2027 effective date. The rules turn safety into a documentation burden of audited governance, lifecycle testing, and performance records, raising the bar past polished demos as the IEA projects 700,000 to 3 million robotaxis across 40 to 80 cities by 2035.

title: Cordyceps: The Silent Parasite Consuming Your Supply Chain
blurb: Novee disclosed Cordyceps, a class of unauthenticated GitHub Actions CI/CD flaws letting anyone hijack repos at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: IA : qu’est-ce que l’« adversarial distillation » que ANTHROPIC reproche à ALIBABA ?
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running an "adversarial distillation" campaign between April and June 2026, using systematic queries to extract knowledge from its models and train competing systems, according to a letter cited by Bloomberg. The dispute marks a shift in AI's geopolitical fight from chips and data centers to the models themselves, raising the question of how labs protect proprietary capabilities from rivals probing them at scale.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic that covers joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal for Micron's data center HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The partnership ties frontier model demand directly to infrastructure, giving Anthropic guaranteed supply for training and serving Claude while positioning Micron's memory portfolio at the center of AI compute scaling.

title: Android 17’s new foldable gaming mode could make flippy phones more fun
blurb: Google is adding a foldable gaming mode to Android 17 that displays a virtual gamepad with touch controls on one half of the screen. The controller emulates physical button presses at the system level, works with any game supporting physical controllers, and offers a full D-pad, dual sticks, A/B/X/Y, shoulder triggers, and configurable layout, sizing, and haptics, with launch set for the coming months.

title: Apple hints at more price increases coming later
blurb: Apple raised prices on iPad and Mac today and signaled in a statement to Bloomberg and others that more increases are coming, saying it needs to "begin raising prices on a number of products." The iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods were spared this round but are likely targets, with fall increases on the iPhone 18 and new Watch models seen as near certain amid the memory supply crunch.

title: Apple stock gets slammed on bigger Mac, iPad price hikes. Why it can weather the storm
blurb: Apple shares fell more than 6% on Thursday, their worst session in over a year, after the company raised prices 17% to 25% across MacBooks, iPads, and home devices to pass on surging memory costs. Microsoft hiked Xbox prices the same day and dropped 3.5% to a 52-week low, but analysts say Apple's scale and supplier leverage leave it better positioned than rivals to absorb the increases.

title: Elastic announces a ~7% reduction in its workforce, and says "advances in AI and automation are letting us operate with leaner teams"; ESTC closed down 8.70% (Richard Speed/The Register)
blurb: Elastic announced a ~7% workforce reduction, attributing the cuts to AI and automation that let it "operate with leaner teams." Shares closed down 8.70%, adding the search and observability vendor to a growing list of software companies framing layoffs as AI-driven efficiency rather than weak demand.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom chip built specifically for large language model inference. The companies designed it to improve performance, efficiency, and scale across AI systems, giving OpenAI more control over the compute stack that runs its models as it reduces reliance on third-party accelerators.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: Malware authors are embedding fake system instructions and policy-triggering text about nuclear and biological weapons into spyware comment blocks to derail LLM-based code analysis. The header doesn't affect execution but can trigger refusals, context pollution, or premature classification in naive AI-first triage pipelines, though static tools like YARA rules, entropy checks, and AST parsing still detect the underlying payload.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter to The Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against the company to access its Claude model. Distillation extracts a target model's behavior by querying it at scale to train a competing system, and Anthropic characterized the effort as a "brazen" campaign to replicate Claude's capabilities.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively called DifyTap, in the open source LLMOps platform Dify, which powers over 1 million applications across more than 50 industries. The flaws, two rated critical, let attackers in multi-tenant cloud setups read other customers' private chats, trigger cross-tenant internal API calls, and access files uploaded by other tenants. Dify version 1.14.2 patches the issues.

title: Jalapeño: OpenAI's new Chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom unveiled Jalapeño, OpenAI's first inference accelerator, designed in-house around its understanding of LLM inference and taken from design to production in nine months. Early testing shows performance per watt substantially better than current state-of-the-art, with engineering samples already running workloads like GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark and deployment planned at gigawatt scale alongside data center partners.

title: AI Is Designing Radio Chips That Humans Couldn’t Even Imagine
blurb: Princeton researchers applied reinforcement learning, inverse design, and diffusion models to generate radio-frequency integrated circuits (RFICs) from scratch, producing layouts that achieve record performance while cutting design time substantially. RFIC design has long remained an experience-driven craft that bottlenecks wireless progress, and the authors argue further gains will require large shared chip-design datasets and open ecosystems so models can learn universal electromagnetic and circuit behaviors.

title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7 angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm manufacturing technology. The node uses nanostack transistors that vertically bond complementary n-type and p-type tiers across two wafers, delivering up to 50% higher performance, 70% better energy efficiency, and roughly double the transistor density versus IBM's 2nm node.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of foundation image-generation models aimed at creative exploration rather than a single polished default aesthetic. The models use a diffusion transformer trained through a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, midtraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, with architectural additions like grouped-query attention, sigmoid-gated attention, and Qwen3-VL text encoding.

title: GLM 5.2: why I’m replacing Opus in Claude Code with this new model
blurb: GLM 5.2, the open-weight coding model from Z.AI, was run through four real tasks inside a production Next.js codebase, including an architecture audit, a landing page redesign, and a 45-minute autonomous bug-hunting session pulling from Sentry and Vercel logs. The full run cost $3.36 for roughly 6 million tokens, with the author concluding the model is good enough to replace Opus in Claude Code.

title: Run a vLLM Server on HF Jobs in One Command
blurb: Hugging Face's new guide shows how to launch a vLLM server on HF Jobs with a single `hf jobs run` command, using the official vllm/vllm-openai image on a chosen GPU flavor. The server speaks the OpenAI API, exposes a public proxy URL authenticated with an HF token, and is billed per minute, making it suited for tests, evals, and batch generation rather than production.

title: Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces RiVER, a reinforcement learning framework that trains LLMs on score-based coding tasks using deterministic execution feedback instead of ground-truth solutions, addressing scale dominance and frequency dominance through calibrated reward shaping. Trained only on AtCoder Heuristic Contest tasks, it improved Qwen3-8B and GLM-Z1-9B by roughly 9% in ALE rating rank and also transferred to exact-solution benchmarks like LiveCodeBench and USACO.

title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models
blurb: This paper shows that combining language models through routing, voting, or mixture-of-agents cannot exceed one minus beta, the rate at which every model fails the same query, a ceiling that average pairwise error correlation cannot detect. Across 67 models from 21 providers, single-factor models underprice this all-wrong tail by about 2.5x on open-ended math, and on checkable tasks ensembling rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal.

title: Hallucination in World Models is Predictable and Preventable
blurb: This paper argues that hallucination in generative world models is a data coverage problem, concentrating in low-coverage regions of the state-action space where lightweight signals can both detect and mitigate it. The authors introduce MMBench2, a 427-hour, 210-task dataset, identify three hallucination modes, and use their failure predictors as curiosity rewards to adapt a 350M-parameter model to unseen environments with as few as 50 real trajectories.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba must be punished for largest Claude cloning attack
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running the largest campaign yet to illicitly extract Claude's capabilities, citing more than 28.8 million exchanges generated through nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5.
