In a letter to senators, Anthropic said the operation targeted agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon tasks, and used obfuscation and proxy networks to evade detection after Trump moved to curb such distillation attacks.

title: OpenAI will delay GPT-5.6 after Trump administration request
blurb: OpenAI will release GPT-5.6 in a limited preview to a small group of enterprise customers, complying with a Trump administration request to stagger the launch over security concerns. The administration will approve customer access case-by-case during the preview, a softer arrangement than the export control directive that forced Anthropic to suspend access to its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models earlier this month.

title: The US government asks OpenAI to slow its next model’s release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its upcoming GPT-5.6 model, marking the first time the US government has preemptively requested an American AI lab restrict a launch. Sam Altman told staff the model would first go to a short list of trusted partners, with the government approving access customer by customer during the preview period over cybersecurity concerns.

title: The White House is asking OpenAI to slow roll the release of its new model over safety concerns
blurb: OpenAI plans to limit the release of its new model, GPT-5.6, to a select group of close partners after the Trump administration requested a restricted rollout over safety concerns. CEO Sam Altman reportedly told staff the government would approve access customer by customer during a preview period, with a broader public release possible a couple of weeks later if it goes well.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: Anthropic's Model Context Protocol moves to a new enterprise-ready spec on July 28, 2026, making MCP stateless at the protocol layer and giving legacy versions a 12-month deprecation window. Akamai's analysis found the redesign closes off session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts but opens new attack surfaces, including predictable tracking IDs that enable cross-tenant actions and MCP-specific HTTP headers that can leak API keys or PII.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup behind the Mojo programming language and the MAX inference platform. The deal gives Qualcomm Modular's software stack for running AI models efficiently across hardware, supporting its push to compete in data center and edge AI inference beyond its mobile chip business.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis AI models can be manipulated into revealing whose data was used to train them, exposing a privacy risk in clinical machine learning systems. The attack could let adversaries confirm whether a specific patient's records were part of a training set, undermining the anonymity protections that healthcare deployments depend on.

title: When are likely answers right? On Sequence Probability and Correctness in LLMs
blurb: This paper quantifies when a model's sequence probability, the conditional likelihood of a continuation given a prompt, actually predicts whether the answer is correct, testing the relationship across decoding methods, hyperparameters, datasets, and repeated responses. Higher probability tracks correctness across prompt-answer pairs in a fixed dataset, but raising it by tuning hyperparameters or methods does not reliably improve accuracy, and it fails to rank repeated responses to the same prompt.

title: ShareLock: A Stealthy Multi-Tool Threshold Poisoning Attack Against MCP
blurb: This paper introduces ShareLock, a multi-tool poisoning attack against MCP that uses Shamir's threshold scheme to split a malicious instruction into benign-looking secret shares spread across several tool descriptions. A covert trigger planted during a server update reconstructs the hidden instruction, evading description-based detectors while maintaining an average attack success rate above 90% across mainstream LLMs and two MCP clients.

title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
blurb: This paper studies how LLM coding agents are configured, finding in 10,008 GitHub repositories that agent config files propagate as unmanaged shared components, with 10.1% of tracked paths being exact duplicates across independent repos and under 1% declaring any permission boundaries. The authors propose Rel(AI)Build, a deterministic control plane that treats agent definitions as a managed supply chain with content-addressed lockfiles, tiered permissions enforced before LLM invocation, and prompt-drift detection via Jaccard similarity.

title: Anthropic's Mythos Model Found Vulnerabilities in Classified US Government Systems, Official Says
blurb: A U.S. official told the Associated Press that Anthropic's Mythos model identified vulnerabilities in highly sensitive classified government systems within hours during a joint testing exercise with U.S. intelligence agencies. The tests ran under Anthropic's Project Glasswing initiative, and the disclosure comes as the administration restricts foreign access to Mythos and the more limited Fable models over cybersecurity concerns.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
blurb: This paper studies prompt injection in LLM-based résumé screening, where candidates insert subtle self-promotional text that adds no real qualifications but nudges model rankings. The authors find injection reliably boosts rankings when résumés are similar and few candidates do it, but its effect collapses as more candidates inject, and with varied candidate quality it can occasionally let weaker applicants outrank stronger ones.

title: Where Do Models Find Happiness? Emotion Vectors in Open-Source LLMs
blurb: This paper extends prior work on emotion vectors, internal representations that encode emotion concepts, from Claude Sonnet 4.5 to two open-weight models, Apertus-8B-Instruct and Gemma-4-E4B-it. The authors recover valence geometry in both (peak PC1-valence correlations of r=0.76 and r=0.83), but find the representations emerge at opposite model depths, collapsing in late layers for Gemma while surfacing only at mid-depth for Apertus.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed with regulators to raise up to $29.43B through an American depositary receipt listing on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, scheduled to debut July 10th. All proceeds are earmarked for AI memory capacity, funding its first Yongin cluster fab, a Cheongju HBM packaging plant, and a record $7.9B order for roughly 30 ASML EUV scanners through 2027.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a merger with SPAC Churchill Capital Corp XI at a $2.5B valuation, becoming the first U.S. company dedicated solely to humanoid robots to reach Wall Street. The deal provides over $620M in cash, including roughly $200M from a Foxconn-led group, though filings withhold revenue and show the company remains unprofitable after burning about $100M in 2025.

title: Washington’s Foreign Ban on Anthropic’s Top Models May Backfire
blurb: The White House banned foreign access to Anthropic's top models, a restriction analysts say could redirect global demand toward China's open-source AI offerings. The move may undercut its own intent by accelerating adoption of Chinese alternatives like those from DeepSeek and Alibaba, giving rivals a foothold among developers cut off from US frontier systems.

title: Italy is investigating Microsoft 365's price hike, saying Microsoft failed to inform users that AI tools like Copilot were being integrated into the service (Giulia Segreti/Reuters)
blurb: Italy's antitrust authority opened an investigation into Microsoft over its Microsoft 365 price increase, alleging the company failed to disclose that AI tools like Copilot were being bundled into the service. Regulators are examining whether the integration and accompanying price hike were communicated clearly enough to users, a question that could shape how AI features get packaged into existing subscriptions.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming frontier model, according to a person familiar with the matter. The request follows Anthropic's decision nearly two weeks earlier to pull its most capable offerings from the market under regulatory pressure.

title: OpenAI staggers GPT-5.6 rollout for government vetting, eyes 2027 IPO
blurb: OpenAI will roll out GPT-5.6 to a small set of vetted partners rather than the public, after the Trump administration asked to review the model customer by customer before a wider release. The arrangement mirrors the government's handling of Anthropic's Mythos and comes as OpenAI leans toward a 2027 IPO rather than filing this year.

title: Error-Conditioned Neural Solvers
blurb: This paper introduces error-conditioned Neural Solvers (ENS), which feed the PDE residual field directly into the network as an input at each iteration so it can read the spatial structure of its own errors and learn an iterative correction policy. Across four PDE families, ENS reaches the highest prediction accuracy in most settings, with gains up to 10x on turbulent Kolmogorov flow while avoiding the compute cost of hybrid residual-minimization methods.

title: Chai: Agentic Discovery of Cryptographic Misuse Vulnerabilities
blurb: Chai is an AI system that discovers and validates cryptographic misuse vulnerabilities using naturally occurring signals, reworking differential testing to catalog flaws at the library level and propagate them across a dependency graph. Evaluated on X.509, JWT, and SAML libraries, it surfaced over 100 vulnerabilities, including a critical previously unknown bug in an SSL library running on billions of devices.

title: Hierarchical Muon: Tiled Newton-Schulz Updates for Efficient Muon Optimization
blurb: This paper introduces Hierarchical Muon (HiMuon), a tiled Newton-Schulz scheme that partitions each momentum-gradient matrix into T×T tiles, applies the same finite Newton-Schulz map to each tile independently, and reassembles them. The approach cuts the leading optimizer work from O(r²sK) to O(HWTK) and decomposes into independent small dense operations, keeping transformer training close to full-matrix Muon while enabling tile-size GPU kernels and cross-layer batching.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
blurb: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study of the "Web4" agent economy, where autonomous agents hold crypto wallets, trade on-chain, and pay for API calls via protocols like MCP, x402, and EIP-8004. Analyzing 99,448 identity registrations, over 317M transaction logs, 341 MCP projects, and 349 GitHub issues, the authors find an active machine-to-machine payment economy built on immature infrastructure, with payment interoperability the most persistent unresolved bottleneck.

title: Beyond Function Calling: Benchmarking Tool-Using Agents under Tool-Environment Unreliability
blurb: ToolBench-X is a benchmark that evaluates tool-using agents under recoverable reliability hazards, injecting five failure types including specification drift, execution failure, and cross-source conflict into otherwise clean tool environments. Experiments reveal a substantial reliability gap, as agents that handle reliable tools well often fail when recovery requires hazard diagnosis rather than higher tool-use volume or inference budget.

title: SpaceX inks $6.3B compute deal with Reflection AI
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal worth up to $6.3B with SpaceX, paying $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for access to Nvidia GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The open-weight lab, founded in 2024 by two former Google DeepMind researchers, framed it as one of the largest open AI infrastructure commitments to date and smaller than SpaceX's Anthropic and Google contracts.

title: MIRROR: Novelty-Constrained Memory-Guided MCTS Red-Teaming for Agentic RAG
blurb: MIRROR is a red-teaming framework for multimodal agentic RAG systems that runs memory-guided Monte Carlo tree search while conditioning attack generation on retrieved context, using a deterministic Novelty Gate to reject candidates that copy the retrieval set. It reaches 76% attack success on image poisoning versus 52% for baselines and 97% on orchestrator attacks at half the query cost, and ships ART-SafeBench with over 41,000 records across four attack surfaces.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout marks a shift from mobile, event-based deployments to fixed infrastructure, intensifying civil liberties concerns over mass biometric surveillance in one of the city's busiest commercial districts.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire inference software startup Modular for 19.2 million shares worth $3.92 billion, more than double Modular's last funding valuation. The chipmaker also previewed two data center chips, the Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator, and raised its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue guidance to $40 billion, sending shares up 14% in after-hours trading.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
blurb: This paper presents an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified Cedar policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, the autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement used in prior work.

title: Google tests voice dictation and Magic Pointer on Gemini desktop
blurb: Google is testing system-wide voice dictation and a Magic Pointer cursor-tracking feature in the macOS Gemini desktop app, alongside possible device-linking functionality. The additions would let Gemini capture spoken input and follow on-screen context across any application, extending the assistant beyond its current chat window into a system-level tool.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators affiliated with Alibaba and its Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5. The company described it as its largest known distillation attack, exceeding its February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax combined and targeting Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning.

title: Inherited Circuits, Learned Semantics: How Fine-Tuning Creates Evasion Vulnerabilities Invisible to Standard Evaluation
blurb: This paper shows that fine-tuning LLMs for security classification can introduce evasion vulnerabilities invisible to standard held-out evaluation, since models learn token-level indicator rules that break under behavior-preserving transformations like PowerShell alias substitution and case mutation. Studying Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct against base Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, the authors localize the classification circuit to an inherited late-attention route and propose a linear-probe monitoring method to flag semantic drift before deployment.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A developer built hackmyclaw.com, inviting anyone to email his OpenClaw assistant Fiu and try to make it leak a secrets.env file. After hitting the Hacker News front page, Fiu fielded over 6,000 emails from 2,000+ people using fake-future personas, compliance-audit pressure, and multilingual prompts, but the secrets never leaked. The exercise cost over $500 in API tokens and got the assistant's Gmail suspended for three days, while batch processing initially contaminated results until each email was given a fresh context.

title: Adaptive Evaluation of Out-of-Band Defenses Against Prompt Injection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper organizes out-of-band prompt injection defenses like CaMeL, FIDES, and Progent as instances of classical integrity protection, reference monitoring, and least privilege, then warns that all are validated only on static benchmarks. The authors reproduce Progent's adaptive-attack analysis on AgentDojo with a self-hosted Qwen2.5-7B, finding it cut mean attack success roughly sixfold to 4.2%.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC has reportedly told customers to prepare for price increases across all advanced nodes, extending hikes beyond 3nm to include 5nm and 7nm processes that together make up 74% of its wafer revenue. The increases reportedly fall in the 5% to 10% range and would raise wafer costs for major chip designers including Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom debut custom Jalapeño chip for AI inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom debuted Jalapeño, a custom inference-only chip for running OpenAI's large language models, with early testing showing significantly higher performance per watt than current state-of-the-art accelerators. The architecture reduces data movement between logic and off-chip memory, and OpenAI will deploy it in custom racks using Broadcom's Tomahawk 6 networking, which handles up to 1.6 terabits per second.

title: TabClean: Reusable LLM-Synthesized Programs for Tabular Data Cleaning
blurb: TabClean is a model-training-free system that compiles LLM reasoning into reusable, guarded Python cleaning programs, so recurring batches run as deterministic code instead of repeated LLM inference. Given a dirty table and a small annotated dev set, it profiles evidence, synthesizes transformations, and validates them with cell-level feedback, improving F1 over rule-based, learning-based, and LLM-based baselines on five of six benchmarks while cutting recurring runtime and API cost.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets the chatbot interact with everyone in a channel on a user's behalf. With preset guidance, it can monitor channel activity, send alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drop comments in conversations, and fix issues with code.

title: Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement
blurb: BINEVAL is a training-free evaluation framework that decomposes scoring criteria into atomic yes/no questions, then aggregates the verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores with question-level feedback. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS it matches or beats UniEval and G-Eval, avoids the ceiling effects of holistic LLM judges, and feeds its per-question feedback back into iterative prompt optimization.

title: How a Niche Technology Became a Choke Point for A.I.
blurb: Advanced chip packaging, the technology that stitches together multiple silicon dies to boost AI computing power, has concentrated production in Taiwan and deepened US dependence on TSMC. The article argues this packaging has become a critical choke point in the AI supply chain, with American efforts to build domestic capacity still years behind Taiwan's scale and expertise.

title: Paved with True Intents: Intent-Aware Training Improves LLM Safety Classification Across Training Regimes
blurb: This paper introduces AIMS, a human-annotated dataset of 1,724 difficult safety prompts each paired with an intent description and harm label, to test whether modeling user intent as an explicit signal improves LLM safety classifiers. Rewarding intent faithfulness with GRPO yielded the strongest average performance across five external safety benchmarks, and the intent-aware models formed the inference latency-F1 Pareto frontier.

title: Augmentation with Dilution: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Human Contributor Ecosystems After AI Coding Agent Adoption
blurb: This paper presents the first large-scale empirical study treating human contributors, not AI, as the dependent variable after AI coding agent adoption in open-source projects. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design across 11,097 GitHub repositories from 2023 to 2026, the authors find that agents don't reduce the absolute number of human contributors but cut their relative density, with newcomers' participation share dropping 3.7 points and review depth rising 5.3% as burden shifts from writing code to reviewing it.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm announced it would acquire infrastructure startup Modular, reportedly for around $3.92B, to strengthen its AI software stack and data center push. The deal targets the inference market and is expected to close in the second half of 2026, positioning Qualcomm to help enterprises run AI more efficiently as token costs climb.

title: Chinese Supercomputer Overtakes U.S. as World’s Fastest
blurb: China's LineShine system took the No. 1 spot on the latest Top500 supercomputer ranking released this week, overtaking U.S. machines as the world's fastest. The result marks a shift in the high-performance computing race that underpins AI training and large-scale scientific work, reversing the lead the U.S. had held atop the list.

title: [AINews] OpenAI reports median internal Codex output tokens grew 56x in Research, 32x in Customer Support, 27x in Engineering, and 13x in Legal since November 2025.
blurb: OpenAI's economic research team reported that median internal Codex output tokens have grown sharply across non-coding departments since November 2025, led by Research at 56x. Customer Support rose 32x, Engineering 27x, and Legal 13x over the same period, a shift notable given that OpenAI employees have always had unlimited model access yet underused it through late 2025.

title: Anthropic’s Claude is winning over paid consumers, a market owned by ChatGPT
blurb: Anthropic's Claude is gaining ground with paying consumers, a segment long dominated by ChatGPT, according to credit card transaction data from Indagari covering roughly 28 million U.S. consumers. Claude's paying consumers and revenue in that sample are up about 75% since January 2026, and DataCamp reports consumer demand for Claude courses now outpaces ChatGPT three to one, though ChatGPT still leads on total paying users.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is leaning toward delaying its IPO until next year, as advisers urge Sam Altman to slow down following volatility in SpaceX's stock. The company is weighing the move while grappling with financial challenges, and the cautious timeline reflects concerns about taking a high-burn AI business public into an uncertain market.

title: OpenAI will initially only release ChatGPT 5.6 to government-approved customers
blurb: OpenAI will stagger the release of ChatGPT 5.6, initially limiting access to customers approved by the US federal government on a case-by-case basis during a preview period, according to The Information. CEO Sam Altman told staff this is not the company's preferred long-term approach, with a broader release expected a couple of weeks after agencies finish approving individual customers.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of high-capability "covered models" to report major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days. Reportable events include models attempting to evade human oversight, resist shutdown, or enable cyberattacks and chemical, biological, or nuclear weapons development, with the Secretary of Commerce setting the capability thresholds that trigger compliance.

title: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until 2027; expectations of a windfall from OpenAI's debut helped support Softbank's stock (Aya Wagatsuma/Bloomberg)
blurb: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until 2027, removing a near-term catalyst investors had counted on. Expectations of a windfall from OpenAI's public debut had been a key support for SoftBank's stock, leaving the company exposed as the timeline slips further out.

title: CARVE: Content-Aware Recurrent with Value Efficiency for Chunk-Parallel Linear Attention
blurb: CARVE is a linear-attention architecture that fixes three coupled flaws in the delta-rule model GDN-2 by erasing memory only on the key axis, which the authors prove is necessary and sufficient to keep the WY-form chunk solver valid. At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B tokens, it cuts WikiText perplexity by 0.18 and leads every recurrent baseline on RULER retrieval at 0.4% throughput overhead, 13% lower peak memory, and 19% fewer parameters.

title: Helpful or Harmful? Evaluating LLM-Assisted Vulnerability Patching via a Human Study
blurb: This paper proposes a controlled human study evaluating whether LLM-assisted vulnerability patching outperforms manual debugging, using a balanced crossover design and hidden "Ghost Tests" that check patch integrity beyond visible functional requirements. The authors measure remediation speed, security validation pass rates, and developer perception to test whether LLM assistance accelerates fixes or produces superficial repairs that pass functionality checks while failing security.

title: How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?
blurb: This paper argues that most of the accuracy gap in time-series forecasting can be closed by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling models, using Ridge regression as a closed-form testbed. Searching over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation across eight benchmarks, the optimized linear models beat prior linear forecasters and exceed Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of eight datasets.

title: How Much Static Structure Do Code Agents Need? A Study of Deterministic Anchoring
blurb: This paper studies whether injecting lightweight static analysis, such as call graphs and inheritance topology as plain-text comments, can make LLM code agents navigate repositories more reliably. Using OpenAI's Codex as a baseline, the authors find that these deterministic anchors improve function-level localization by 2.2pp, shorten trajectories, and roughly halve run-to-run variance at the cost of about 10% more input tokens.

title: Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents
blurb: This paper introduces JERP (Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies), a method that updates both a long-term rule pool and an LLM agent's policy from the same interaction trajectories. After each episode, JERP optimizes the policy and revises the rules by comparing rollouts against reference successful trajectories, keeping the prompted rules aligned with the evolving policy and yielding consistent decision gains on AlfWorld and WebShop.

title: Show HN: OpenKnowledge – open source AI-first alternative to Obsidian/Notion
blurb: OpenKnowledge is an open source, local-first markdown editor and LLM wiki that pairs full WYSIWYG editing with collaborative AI editing through Claude, Codex, and Cursor, or any agent via MCP and CLI. It ships with out-of-the-box MCP, skills, and agentic search, plus no-code team sharing and auto-sync backed by git and GitHub, and is available as a macOS app or a web app and CLI on Linux, Windows, and Intel Mac.

title: Patronus AI lands $50M to build ‘digital worlds’ that stress-test AI agents
blurb: Patronus AI raised a $50M Series B led by Greenfield Partners to build simulated "digital world" environments that stress-test AI agents after training. The startup creates replicas of websites and internal systems where agents are evaluated using reinforcement learning, and its revenue grew 15-fold over the past year, bringing total funding to $70M.

title: Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text
blurb: This paper proposes GAversary, a black-box genetic algorithm that generates adversarial text attacks using only a model's logit outputs, with GloVe embeddings guiding word-replacement mutations to preserve semantic similarity. Across benchmark datasets it cut one target model's accuracy from 76.8% to 5.8%, far below the 27.6% from the BAE attack, at the cost of perturbing nearly twice as many words.

title: Memory maker SK hynix files for $29B US IPO amid AI demand
blurb: SK hynix, the world's largest supplier of HBM memory, filed to list on Nasdaq in an offering that could raise up to $29.4 billion, the second-largest US IPO on record after SpaceX. The company produces more than half of the world's HBM, the high-bandwidth memory that surrounds AI chips like Nvidia's Rubin, and posted a 77% net margin on $38 billion in Q1 revenue.

title: A Mechanistic Explanation of Prompt Injection (and why you should study roles)
blurb: This post presents a mechanistic theory of prompt injection, arguing the vulnerability comes down to how LLMs perceive roles defined by chat template tags. Because a model receives its entire context as one continuous token stream, its own reasoning, user instructions, and fetched webpage content all arrive through the same channel, with role tags as the only structure separating them. The authors use the theory to construct new attacks, explain prior mech interp findings, and predict when injections succeed.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun's new Paris-based startup AMI Labs raised $1.03B in seed funding from investors including Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, and HV Capital, with Alex LeBrun joining as CEO. The lab, which LeCun announced after leaving Meta in November, will focus on world models, AI systems built to understand and reflect how the physical world works.

title: Liquid AI's smallest model yet LFM2.5-230M beats models 4X its size at data extraction, can run 'anywhere'
blurb: Liquid AI released LFM2.5-230M, a 230-million-parameter model built on its LFM2 architecture for on-device agentic workflows across smartphones, laptops, and robotics. The model outperforms Alibaba's Qwen3.5-0.8B and Google's Gemma 3 1B on data extraction benchmarks, supports a 32K context window, and stays free for individuals and companies under $10M in annual revenue.

title: Notion Mail shuts down amid agent takeover
blurb: Notion will shut down its email product, Notion Mail, on September 22, redirecting users to its AI agents that manage inboxes automatically. The company said more than half of Notion Mail users already handle email without ever opening their inbox, and its email-based agents will keep running after the shutdown.

title: Analog chipmaker Onsemi buys Synaptics in $7B all-stock deal to push into physical AI
blurb: Onsemi announced it would acquire human-interface and IoT chipmaker Synaptics in an all-stock deal valued at around $7 billion. The acquisition is meant to advance Onsemi's push into "physical AI," pairing its analog and silicon carbide sensing chips with Synaptics' connectivity and software to move AI from cloud data centers onto edge devices like robots, drones, and autonomous vehicles.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Mirendil, a San Francisco startup founded by former Anthropic researchers Behnam Neyshabur and Harsh Mehta, raised $200M at a $1B valuation in one of the largest seed rounds in AI history. The round was co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins, with NVIDIA participating, to build AI systems that automate frontier AI research itself for universities and institutions outside the largest labs.

title: E-TTS: A New Embodied Test-Time Scaling Framework for Robotic Manipulation
blurb: E-TTS is a plug-and-play test-time scaling framework for robotic manipulation that unifies reasoning and action scaling through history-aware iterative refinement with vision-language verifiers. Across 4 benchmarks, 6 environments, and 4 base vision-language-action models, it improved performance by up to 33.14% in simulation and 26.62% in the real world without extra expert data or retraining.

title: TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference
blurb: TOPS is a training-free, model-agnostic visual token pruning module that formulates token selection around three information-theoretic principles: task relevance, information coverage, and semantic diversity. Across 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks it beats prior methods, removing 77.8% of visual tokens on LLaVA-NeXT while preserving 100% and 100.6% of performance on the 7B and 13B models.

title: RedVox: Safety and Fairness Gaps in Speech Models Across Languages
blurb: RedVox is a multilingual safety and fairness benchmark for speech models, built on real voices and covering unsafe and stereotypical requests across English, French, Italian, Spanish, and German. Evaluating eight state-of-the-art models, the authors find vulnerabilities persist under non-adversarial conditions, worsen in non-English languages, and are amplified when requests arrive as spoken input rather than text.

title: Forecasting With LLMs: Improved Generalization Through Feature Steering
blurb: This paper applies LLMs to forecasting tasks and uses sparse autoencoders to inspect whether the models rely on time-specific knowledge or generalizable patterns, identifying features tied to both time-aware and look-ahead-biased reasoning. Amplifying the time-awareness features substantially reduced look-ahead bias on out-of-domain forecasting prompts while preserving general reasoning, whereas steering the candidate look-ahead-bias features had no effect.

title: So You Want to Sell Inference
blurb: This post argues that reselling inference at cost is a zero-margin payment rail, and the only way to hold 30+ points of gross margin is to escape cost-plus pricing. Cost-plus markups compress toward zero as inference commoditizes and customers route to the raw API, while value-based pricing charges for outcomes like resolved tickets or completed tasks and stays decoupled from the inference line. The author notes distillation to proprietary small models is the most defensible way to widen margin.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers who use them, as token consumption from autonomous, long-running tasks climbs. The piece argues that current per-seat and subscription pricing masks the true expense, and that as agents take on more work, those costs will surface and keep rising.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported third-quarter revenue of $41.46B, more than quadrupling from $9.3B a year earlier and topping Wall Street's $35.84B target, as AI demand drains memory production capacity. Gross margin climbed to 84.9%, the highest among major US tech companies, and Micron guided to roughly $50B in revenue for the current quarter against the Street's $43.58B forecast.

title: Jailbreaking for the Average Jane: Choosing Optimal Jailbreaks via Bandit Algorithms for Automatically Enhanced Queries
blurb: This paper tests whether non-expert attackers can reliably jailbreak LLMs, introducing a multi-armed bandit method that learns the optimal jailbreak from a large set using a small number of noisy exploration queries. Against 15 state-of-the-art open-weight models it reached success rates as high as 97%, and adding complexity to malicious queries raised attack success by up to 26% on average.

title: Advancing Omnimodal Embodied Agents from Isolated Skills to Everyday Physical Autonomy
blurb: OmniAct is a framework for persistent embodied agents that unifies cyber and physical actions through a hierarchical architecture separating planning, memory, and verification. Across 40 real-world long-horizon tasks on two robotic platforms coordinating four IoT devices, it improves end-to-end success at all complexity levels, holds token consumption near-flat past 100k accumulated interactions, and lifts mid-scale open-weight models to proprietary-level performance.

title: VIGIL: Runtime Enforcement of Behavioral Specifications in AI Agent Skills
blurb: VIGIL is a runtime enforcement framework that checks an AI agent's actual execution trace against behavioral policies drawn from skill specifications, operator constraints, and cross-skill global rules. Its policy language compiles temporal dependencies, argument constraints, and value-flow conditions into SMT constraints over finite traces, detecting violations with over 95% recall and a false-positive rate below 10% on real LLM-agent runs.

title: [AINews] It's Meta-Harness Summer
blurb: Databricks CTO Matei Zaharia introduced Omnigent, an open-source, pluggable architecture for pulling any coding or knowledge-work agent into a standardized, secure, and scalable system. It joins a wave of "meta-harnesses" including Zed's ACP, Cloudflare's Flue, and Vercel's Eve, all converging on a common pattern as roughly a thousand AI-native shops independently rediscover the same orchestration layer.

title: Meta plans to replace 90% of content review staff with AI
blurb: Meta plans to replace roughly 90% of its content review staff with AI, according to Axios. The company said AI already handles 50% of content review and expects that share to grow by year's end, despite a recent Instagram breach that raised questions about its automated systems.

title: Learning to Fold: prizewinning solution at LeHome Challenge 2026 (1st place online, 2nd offline)
blurb: This paper describes the winning solution to the LeHome Challenge 2026, an ICRA bimanual garment-folding competition, where it placed 1st of 62 teams in simulation and 2nd in the real-world final. The system augments a vision-language-action policy with a reinforcement-learning loop where the same network predicts actions, success, and progress, driving advantage estimation, live failure detection, and candidate selection.

title: How agents are transforming work
blurb: OpenAI published a research paper examining how AI agents are reshaping work by handling longer, more complex tasks across a range of roles. The paper documents productivity gains as agents take on multi-step workflows, expanding the scope of what can be automated beyond single, narrow tasks.

title: Un-0: Generating Images with Coupled Oscillators
blurb: Un-0 is an image generator built on a simulated system of coupled oscillators, an example of physical computing where the dynamics of a physical substrate do the work instead of conventional GPU networks. It reaches FID 6.74 on ImageNet 64×64, matching early versions of leading conventional generators, and the team released weights, training, and ablation code as a step toward running AI at roughly 1,000x less energy.

title: Einstein World Models
blurb: This paper introduces Einstein World Models (EWMs), a blueprint for LLM reasoning systems that insert visual-temporal rollouts directly into the reasoning trace. An LLM calls a separate world-module to generate short scene rollouts, treating each not as the answer but as an inspectable hypothesis that supports later reasoning, extending tool-calling into visual thought experiments.

title: Safe Autoregressive Image Generation with Iterative Self-Improving Codebooks
blurb: This paper proposes iterative self-improving codebooks to make autoregressive image generation safer without human annotation, using the multimodal model's own judgment to flag unsafe outputs. The method builds harmful and safe image-text pairs to update the codebook, eliminating harmful mappings, then fine-tunes within the safe space and repeats until safety no longer improves.

title: OpenAI says 97.9% of its employees are now using Codex, up from ~40% in August 2025; non-developer usage of Codex has risen 137x for individual users (Thomas Claburn/The Register)
blurb: OpenAI said 97.9% of its employees now use Codex, up from roughly 40% in August 2025. Non-developer usage of the coding agent has grown 137x among individual users, signaling the tool's spread beyond engineering teams into broader internal workflows.

title: Pentagon Sees Bigger Role for AI in Setting Military Targets
blurb: The Pentagon quietly revised its targeting doctrine to allow AI a larger role in selecting military targets during wartime. The change opens the door for AI systems to inform or make critical battlefield decisions that were previously reserved for human commanders.

title: DeepSeek Looks to Double Workforce as Fresh Funding Fuels Growth
blurb: DeepSeek plans to roughly double its workforce after a fresh round of funding, with the Hangzhou-based company now hiring for 27 types of technical roles. Open positions include development engineers and AI product managers, all available to interns as the firm scales its research and product operations.

title: Ribbon: Scalable Approximation and Robust Uncertainty Quantification
blurb: Ribbon is a scalable approximation to Dirichlet-reweighted bootstrap uncertainty that replaces repeated model refitting with an influence-function linearization around a single fitted model, requiring only post-hoc linear algebra. Across synthetic regression, MNIST, and California Housing benchmarks, it delivers competitive predictive performance and improved calibration while recovering the robust sandwich covariance under model misspecification.

title: NSA Loses Anthropic Mythos Access After June Export-Control Order
blurb: The NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 cybersecurity model after the Trump administration's June export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend its most advanced models for all foreign nationals, including noncitizen employees, The New York Times reported. Officials clarified that earlier claims of Mythos breaking into classified systems in hours referred to a controlled red-team exercise, not an outside breach.

title: Despite the "DeepMind mafia" pulling billions into London AI startups, none of Demis Hassabis' former lieutenants is building a homegrown UK frontier AI model (Tim Bradshaw/Financial Times)
blurb: Former DeepMind employees have funneled billions into a wave of London AI startups, but none of Demis Hassabis' ex-lieutenants is building a homegrown UK frontier model. The so-called "DeepMind mafia" is instead concentrating on applied tools and vertical applications, leaving the capital-intensive race to train foundation models to US and Chinese labs.

title: The New York Times Amends Lawsuit Against OpenAI and Microsoft
blurb: The New York Times amended its copyright lawsuit against OpenAI and Microsoft, accusing Microsoft of encouraging OpenAI to train its AI systems on copyrighted articles. The new filing seeks to extend liability to Microsoft directly, arguing it was an active participant in the alleged infringement rather than a passive cloud and funding partner.

title: Ford execs say they made a mistake when they replaced human engineers with AI
blurb: Ford rehired roughly 350 veteran "gray beard" engineers over the past three years after concluding that replacing senior staff with AI was a mistake, with its VP of vehicle hardware engineering calling the move a major error. The returned engineers reprogrammed failing diagnostic and AI tools and ran pre-production troubleshooting, which Ford credits for jumping to the top mainstream brand in JD Power's latest quality survey.

title: Sail Research raises $80M to optimize long-horizon AI agents
blurb: Sail Research raised $80M at a $450M valuation in a Series A led by Sequoia, with investors including Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan and Alphabet chair John Hennessy. The startup runs long-horizon AI agents in Linux VMs called Sailboxes that can pause while waiting on external systems, and it claims a 90.72% score on BrowseComp-Plus at one-tenth the inference cost of rivals.

title: Empowering GUI Agents via Autonomous Experience Exploration and Hindsight Experience Utilization for Task Planning
blurb: This paper introduces PEEU, a method that has GUI agents autonomously explore web environments and use hindsight to synthesize aligned, high-level training data, addressing the weak planning of small open-source MLLMs. The 7B model reaches 30.6% accuracy on real-world benchmarks, outperforming the larger Qwen2.5-VL-32B, and the authors find that high-level task training drives stronger out-of-distribution generalization than mastering low-level atomic skills.

title: Beyond the Hard Budget: Sparsity Regularizers for More Interpretable Top-k Sparse Autoencoders
blurb: This paper introduces two sparsity regularizers for Top-k sparse autoencoders, the standard tool for interpreting vision foundation model representations: an ℓ1 penalty on off-support units and a scale-invariant ℓ1/ℓ2-ratio penalty, both applied before Top-k selection. Across two datasets, three vision models, and a range of k, both penalties improve feature monosemanticity at no cost to reconstruction quality, showing that hard architectural sparsity and soft regularization are complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

title: Generative Models on Analog Hardware with Dynamics
blurb: This paper introduces Analog Interaction Systems, a framework for running generative models directly on analog hardware like coupled oscillators and Ising machines, where fixed physics-defined dynamics normally limit expressivity. Using time-varying parameters, hidden physical states, and a Wasserstein GAN training procedure, the oscillator-based models hit FID scores of 27.6 on MNIST and 80.8 on Fashion-MNIST at 23uJ per image, a 100x energy improvement over digital baselines.

title: An Ex-Meta L8's Agentic Engineering Setup
blurb: A former L8 principal engineer at Meta, Microsoft, and Atlassian who led Atlassian's Rovo Dev walks through his complete agentic engineering setup after leaving big tech to build solo. He describes shifting from writing most code himself to directing a team of agents like an engineering manager, focusing on deciding what to build and judging quality while custom tooling handles the work in between.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: Five Eyes signals agencies for Australia, the US, the UK, New Zealand, and Canada issued a rare joint statement warning that frontier AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away. The agencies said the technology lowers barriers for bad actors and accelerates the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks, urging leaders to treat cyber risk as a core business responsibility rather than a technical issue.

title: The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale
blurb: This paper introduces FisherSketch, a streaming method for training-free source selection across LLM families that share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets, such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences. It reframes head Fisher alignment as a cosine between kernel mean embeddings, letting it run in a single pass with a 16 KB task signature where representation-similarity metrics like CKA fail to distinguish transferable tasks.

title: LMs as Task-Specific Knowledge Bases: An Interpretability Analysis
blurb: This paper tests whether language models behave like consistent knowledge bases and finds they don't, instead encoding facts in a task-specific way that fails to transfer across tasks during training. Parameter localization shows distinct subsets underlying the same fact for different tasks, and the authors argue this undermines the knowledge-base analogy with implications for the reliability and controllability of factual knowledge in LMs.

title: Adversarial Diffusion Across Modalities: A Fusion Survey of Attacks, Defenses, and Evaluation for Text, Vision, and Vision-Language Models
blurb: This survey unifies four previously disconnected tracks of adversarial AI research, diffusion-based attacks on LLMs, attacks on image classifiers, jailbreaks against vision-language models, and diffusion-based purification defenses, into a single taxonomy and evaluation framework. The authors catalog 50 papers across these areas, propose a six-class taxonomy of diffusion roles plus a five-dimension scoring framework covering attack success rate, transferability, query budget, perplexity, and defense evasion, and release the companion catalog and spreadsheet with the paper.

title: Anthropic's latest hiring spree reveals where it's building AI data centers next
blurb: Anthropic is hiring for 13 compute-department roles, eight of them in Australia and Japan, signaling an Asia-Pacific data center buildout to meet soaring demand for its products. The push follows spring US data center deals and a $65B May raise at a $965B valuation, with Australian listings citing "multi-hundred megawatt procurement efforts" across the region.

title: OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision
blurb: OpenRCA 2.0 is a 500-instance, cross-system benchmark for root-cause analysis that uses a step-wise labeling protocol called PAVE to annotate full causal propagation paths rather than just outcome labels. Across 11 frontier LLMs, agents recover the exact root-cause set in only 20.7% of cases and ground a correct service in a verified causal path in just 61.5%.

title: Notion killing Skiff-influenced email app since most users use AI agents instead
blurb: Notion will shut down Notion Mail across web, desktop, and iOS on September 22, ending what remained of its 2024 Skiff acquisition. The company said more than half of Notion Mail users already manage email without opening their inbox, handing workflows to AI agents, and that existing email history will stay in Gmail.

title: Parts of NSA lose Mythos 5 access after White House imposes limits
blurb: Parts of the NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 after the Trump administration imposed export controls on the company, citing national-security concerns. The restriction forced Anthropic to pull back its most advanced models, including Mythos 5 and Fable 5, and cut off NSA red teams whose access ran through Project Glasswing, the early-access program for Mythos Preview.

title: How the US AI build-out is pushing up prices for electricity, software, and more; in a survey, 81% of economists say it will add to inflation over the next year (Justin Lahart/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The US AI infrastructure build-out is raising prices for electricity, software, and other inputs as data center demand strains supply. In a Wall Street Journal survey, 81% of economists said the build-out will add to inflation over the next year.

title: Scaled Cognition nabs $100M to automate high stakes customer interactions
blurb: Scaled Cognition raised $100M in a Series A led by Khosla Ventures, valuing the customer-service AI startup at $750M. Its platform runs on a custom model called APT that retrieves existing records rather than generating new data to cut hallucination risk, and double-checks each task before confirming completion, making it suitable for sensitive actions like purchases on a customer's behalf.

title: Mirendil raises $200M to speed up scientific research with AI
blurb: Mirendil raised $200M at a $1B valuation in a seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Kleiner Perkins and Nvidia also contributing. The startup, run by SAM co-inventor Behnam Neyshabur and former Anthropic researcher Harsh Mehta, plans to build self-improving neural networks that automate frontier model development for scientists in chemistry, medicine, and robotics.

title: California launches tracker for AI-related job losses
blurb: California launched a public portal that tracks AI-related job losses across the state, built with the Employment Development Department and UC's California Policy Lab using unemployment claims data combined with AI exposure measures. Governor Gavin Newsom's office framed it as an "early warning system" to flag where interventions are needed, though researchers caution it can't confirm whether specific jobs were actually eliminated by AI.

title: ANTHROPIC recrute le patron IA d’ORANGE pour accélérer son développement en Europe
blurb: Anthropic hired Steve Jarrett, Orange's Chief AI Officer since 2019, to lead its enterprise expansion across European and African markets starting August 25. The Paris-based hire follows Anthropic's recent Milan office opening and plans to triple its international headcount ahead of an IPO, with Jarrett having driven over €300M in AI-generated value at Orange in 2025.

title: Run Codex in the cloud – DigitalOcean for Codex is now available
blurb: DigitalOcean released a plugin for OpenAI's Codex, now in Public Preview, that lets developers provision and connect persistent cloud development machines in their own DigitalOcean account through natural language. Codex spins up a Droplet preconfigured with the Codex CLI, common language tooling, and SSH access via OAuth, so agents can keep running long jobs while developers step away.

title: Automating Potential-based Reward Shaping with Vision Language Model Guidance
blurb: This paper introduces VLM-PBRS, a framework that learns the potential function for potential-based reward shaping directly from vision language model preferences over image pairs, removing the need for expert-designed reward terms while preserving the original optimal policies. To keep costs down it queries small, cheaper VLMs, and experiments in Meta-World and Franka Kitchen show their noisier preference labels still improve sample efficiency and resist reward hacking.

title: Improving General Role-Playing Agents via Psychology-Grounded Reasoning and Role-Aware Policy Optimization
blurb: This paper introduces Psy-CoT, a psychology-grounded chain-of-thought framework that splits a role-playing agent's pre-response reasoning into Interaction Perception, Psychological Empathy, and Logical Construction so the model reasons from a character profile rather than mimicking surface patterns. It also proposes Role-Aware Policy Optimization, which uses profile-token mutual information to weight gradients asymmetrically and curb reward hacking, beating GRPO across model scales on CoSER, CharacterBench, and CharacterEval.

title: MinGram: A Minimalist Unigram Tokenizer with High Compression and Competitive Morphological Alignment
blurb: MinGram is a Unigram tokenizer that keeps the editable token-list representation but replaces the heavy training pipeline with a BPE-derived seed vocabulary, Hard EM on a minimum-token path, and a single flat pruning step, removing the suffix array, forward-backward pass, and iterative prune loop. Across six languages it compresses better than both BPE and standard Unigram, and Unigram-family tokenizers including MinGram consistently beat BPE on bits-per-byte in controlled downstream language-model training.

title: Auditing Framing-Sensitive Behavioral Instability in Large Language Models for Mental Health Interactions
blurb: This paper audits how large language models respond to semantically equivalent mental health concerns presented through different contextual framings, finding that framing systematically shifts interpretive response tendencies across several instruction-tuned model families. Layer-wise probing showed behavior-associated information stayed decodable throughout transformer depth, and activation steering experiments suggest framing-linked representational directions can partially modulate downstream outputs.

title: ProvenAI: Provenance-Native Traces of Evidence in Generated Answers
blurb: ProvenAI is a framework for retrieval-augmented QA that splits transparency into three independently measured layers: answer correctness, citation fidelity against benchmark evidence, and per-document influence under leave-one-resource-out ablation. Tested on 7,405 HotpotQA examples, it hit 53.53% accuracy and 71.55% citation fidelity while surfacing a citation-influence gap, where a cleanly cited source barely shifts the output and seven uncited sources do.

title: The Spec Growth Engine: Spec-Anchored, Code-Coupled, Drift-Enforced Architecture for AI-Assisted Software Development
blurb: The Spec Growth Engine is a framework that addresses two failure modes in AI-assisted coding: context explosion, where agents degrade as they reason over an entire repository, and silent spec-code drift, where specifications fall out of sync with evolving code. It combines a machine-readable spec graph, a context assembler that scopes agents to an ownership path, hardest-first vertical-slice ordering, and a drift gate that makes spec-code divergence a blocking merge condition.

title: To Run or Not to Run: Analyzing the Cost-Effectiveness of Code Execution in LLM-Based Program Repair
blurb: This paper evaluates code execution in LLM-based program repair, analyzing 7,745 SWE-bench agent traces and 3,000 repair attempts across Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode under four execution paradigms. Restricting execution barely affected resolve rates on commercial agents with SOTA models, a non-significant 1.25-point gap, while saving substantial token and wall-clock cost, suggesting execution should be treated as a cost-benefit tradeoff rather than a default.

title: Agents That Know Too Much: A Data-Centric Survey of Privacy in LLM Agents
blurb: This survey reorganizes the scattered research on LLM agent privacy around the data an agent touches rather than the attacks it faces, covering query logs, intermediate results, memory writes, and inter-agent messages. The authors find that only information-flow control addresses both compositional and cross-session inference leakage, the two least-protected risks, and that no existing benchmark drives an agent across all its data surfaces under a single privacy policy.

title: Mixing Visual and Textual Code
blurb: Hybrid ClojureScript is the first hybrid programming language that lets developers embed interactive visual syntax directly within a program's text, mixing graphical and textual code on equal footing. A hybrid IDE renders these embedded instances as interactive mini-GUIs while other editors fall back to a textual representation, and the extensions compose with existing language features and preserve static reasoning.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuning natural language prompts creates "prompt debt," a slow-accumulating liability that caps what AI applications can become. Plain-English prompts make prototyping effortless but degrade into brittle thickets of edge cases and all-caps threats, slowing iteration, becoming illegible to teammates, and locking systems to a single aging model. Datadog data backs this up: GPT-4o remains the most-used model in observed traffic because hot fixes break when teams switch models.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: This post walks through shipping Claude Code telemetry to Amazon CloudWatch using the now-generally-available OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) endpoint, authenticated with a bearer token instead of collectors, sidecars, or IAM credential wiring. The setup yields per-developer cost attribution, team-level usage analytics, and operational alerting on token consumption, all queryable in PromQL, with companion guidance available for OpenAI Codex and GitHub Copilot.

title: Getty Images Announces Display Partnership with OpenAI
blurb: Getty Images announced a partnership with OpenAI to display its licensed editorial and creative imagery within ChatGPT, with attribution and links back to Getty. The deal positions Getty as a sourced visual provider inside the assistant, extending its licensing strategy with AI platforms while giving ChatGPT users access to professionally captioned photography.

title: Agentic infrastructure startup Seltz raises $12.5M to help AI agents search the web for answers
blurb: Seltz raised $12.5M in seed funding led by Speedinvest and B Capital to build search infrastructure optimized for AI agents rather than human users. The startup built its entire stack, including crawlers, index, retrieval models, and ranking systems, to handle long parallel queries that surface structured evidence from tables, images, and page-level content instead of returning lists of links.

title: ConcoLixir: Reactive LLM Discovery Oracles for Python Concolic Testing
blurb: ConcoLixir is a reactive LLM extension for Python concolic testing that uses the model as a discovery oracle, generating seeds, proposing concrete inputs after solver failures, and targeting uncovered code when coverage stalls. Across synthetic, real-world, and library targets it improved mean line coverage over an LLM-free baseline by 8.6, 15.1, and 17.0 percentage points, with the full evaluation costing $1.63 in API charges.

title: US Govt to individually approve who gets GPT 5.6
blurb: A widely shared LocalLLaMA thread claims the US government would individually vet and approve who receives access to OpenAI's GPT 5.6, framing frontier model distribution as a controlled export rather than an open release. The discussion centers on whether per-recipient approval is workable at scale and what it signals about tightening federal oversight of frontier model access.

title: Same Scrutiny, More Time: Eye Tracking Insights into Reviewing LLM-Labelled Code
blurb: This eye-tracking study used a Wizard-of-Oz experiment to measure how software engineers review code explicitly labeled as LLM-generated. Combining Bayesian and qualitative analysis, the authors found that review thoroughness did not change, but participants spent more time fixating on labeled code and shifted strategy toward checking logical correctness and using the prompt to guide the review.

title: DanceOPD: On-Policy Generative Field Distillation
blurb: DanceOPD is an on-policy generative field distillation framework that lets a single flow-matching model unify text-to-image, local editing, and global editing without the capabilities degrading one another. It routes each sample to one capability field, queries a low-noise student-induced state, and trains with a velocity MSE objective, also absorbing operator-defined fields like classifier-free guidance.

title: Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling
blurb: This paper proposes a model-assisted sampling framework that reframes mini-batch gradient estimation through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and using auxiliary gradient-prediction models to build lower-variance estimators. The method integrates with existing optimizers without changing their dynamics, showing gains in 71-86% of experiments and reaching better generalization in roughly half the training epochs when paired with AdamW.

title: IA : qu’est-ce que l’« adversarial distillation » que ANTHROPIC reproche à ALIBABA ?
blurb: Anthropic sent a letter, reported by Bloomberg, accusing Alibaba of running an "adversarial distillation" campaign against its models between April and June 2026. The dispute marks a shift in AI's geopolitical contest from semiconductors and data centers toward the extraction of capabilities from the models themselves, with distillation used to train competing systems on a rival's outputs.

title: Just how sure are you? Improving Verbalized Uncertainty Calibration in Medical VQA
blurb: This work introduces a training-based framework that finetunes multimodal LLMs to fix overconfident outputs in medical visual question answering, using a composite loss that combines a Brier calibration term, an anchor regularizer against confidence collapse, a contrastive image-text alignment term, and a KL stabilization term. Across three benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), it cuts calibration error by at least 60% and improves discrimination by 26% or more while preserving accuracy.

title: Why current LLM costs are not sustainable
blurb: The author argues frontier LLM pricing is unsustainable as performance plateaus and cheaper open-weight models like GLM-5.2 close the gap.

title: Detecting Misuse with the Claude Compliance API: The Threat is in the Content
blurb: This post details detection rules built on Anthropic's Claude Compliance API to catch prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data exfiltration in Claude Enterprise content.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI writing detector GPTZero for an undisclosed sum, folding its tools into the Superhuman Go assistant. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and detection features that flag fake citations, verify cited sources, and log a document's keystroke history.

title: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness
blurb: This post defines the agent harness as the orchestration layer between a foundation model and its environments, covering system prompts, tool wrappers, planner-executor loops, retry policies, and context compaction, with an agent being a harness plus a model. The author argues most harness code is temporary scaffolding for the current level of model capability, and teams treating it as a permanent product surface will spend a year ripping it out as next-generation models absorb those functions.

title: Heavy-Ball Q-Learning with Residual Weighting Correction
blurb: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method that adds momentum to standard Q-learning, proving its convergence and identifying conditions under which it provably converges faster. The analysis models Q-learning as a switched linear system and uses the joint spectral radius of the switching families to explain the acceleration, a viewpoint the authors extend to Q-learning with linear function approximation.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic spanning joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal across Micron's data center portfolio, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The two companies will analyze how HBM, DRAM, and SSD subsystems perform across AI workloads to improve energy efficiency and token economics as Anthropic scales compute to train and serve Claude.

title: HarmVideoBench: Benchmarking Harmful Video Understanding in Large Multimodal Models
blurb: HarmVideoBench is a diagnostic benchmark for harmful video understanding that pairs 1,379 videos with 4,137 multiple-choice questions across three hierarchical layers: Observable Evidence, Clip-Internal Meaning, and Beyond-Clip Reasoning. The authors evaluate 19 leading multimodal models and introduce BCR, a method that retrieves context only when needed, raising the macro average from 61.7% to 84.4%.

title: Smaller Models, Unexpected Costs: Trade-offs in LLM Quantization for Automated Program Repair
blurb: This paper empirically evaluates 13 LLM quantization configurations across six models on two automated program repair benchmarks, HumanEval-Java and Defects4J. While quantization cut memory footprints by up to 85%, it raised inference time and energy use due to poor hardware utilization, and base and quantized models repaired largely non-overlapping sets of bugs despite fixing comparable totals.

title: The Riddle Riddle: Testing Flexible Reasoning in Large Language Models and Humans
blurb: This paper introduces the "riddle riddle" paradigm, which rewrites popular riddles so their answers require only literal interpretation, testing whether models flexibly select reasoning strategies or pattern-match on surface form. Across nine state-of-the-art LLMs and 100 humans, the failures inverted: LLMs scored 84.9% on genuine riddles but 50.7% on riddle riddles, while humans showed the reverse, suggesting strong riddle performance reflects memory retrieval rather than genuine reasoning.

title: Qualcomm to Acquire Modular
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup building a unified software stack for running models across different chips. The deal pushes Qualcomm beyond its mobile and Snapdragon roots and into the inference software layer, where the real fight against Nvidia's CUDA lock-in is being waged. For founders, it is another sign that the hardware giants now see the portability layer between models and silicon as the part of the stack worth owning outright.

title: The US government asks OpenAI to slow its next model’s release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of GPT-5.6, releasing it first to a short list of trusted partners with the government approving access customer by customer during a preview window. The request came from the Office of the National Cyber Director and the Office of Science and Technology Policy, framing it as cybersecurity, and lands two weeks after a directive pulled Anthropic's most capable models from the market. Washington is now shaping the release schedules of frontier labs directly, moving from voluntary commitments to a hands-on say over who gets early access.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful model, less than two weeks after Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings under regulatory pressure. The signal for founders is that frontier capability is now gated by Washington as much as by compute or research, and the most advanced models may reach the market on a regulator's schedule. If your product roadmap assumes day-one access to each new frontier release, that assumption is now a policy risk you don't control.

title: Adobe Just Deferred a Big Annual Price Increase. It’s the First Big Crack in B2B Pricing Power Since 2022.
blurb: Adobe just deferred roughly $500M ARR of Creative Cloud price increases it had already planned for the back half of FY2026, breaking a decade-long habit of raising prices like clockwork. For four years the B2B playbook was identical: hike every 12 to 18 months, bolt on an AI SKU, push net revenue retention above 110%, and customers paid because they had nowhere to go. When one of software's most pricing-powerful companies voluntarily walks away from a planned hike, the signal isn't generosity. It's the first real crack in B2B pricing power since 2022.

title: SK hynix files to raise up to $29 billion in historic Nasdaq listing — all proceeds going to advanced AI memory fabs and EUV tool orders
blurb: SK hynix filed to raise up to $29.43B through a Nasdaq ADR listing on July 10th, one of the largest such sales ever, with every dollar earmarked for AI memory fabs and EUV scanner orders. None of it eases the current shortage: the Yongin Y1 fab and Cheongju packaging plant don't reach volume output until 2027, and chairman Chey Tae-won expects AI demand to keep supply tight through 2030. SK hynix already holds about 57% of the HBM market and 32% of global DRAM, and it just placed a record $7.9B order with ASML for roughly 30 scanners.

title: ‘Digit’ maker Agility Robotics to go public in $2.5B deal — here’s what the filings say about its finances
blurb: Agility Robotics is taking its Digit humanoid public through a $2.5B SPAC merger with Churchill Capital Corp XI, becoming the first US-listed company built solely around humanoid robots. The filings show why the timing matters more than the numbers: Agility doesn't disclose revenue, remains unprofitable, burned roughly $100M in cash last year, and saw operating expenses climb to about $111M from $71M. Its headline $300M in "committed" Digit v5 orders comes with fine print that it isn't current revenue and hinges on a single undisclosed customer. The SPAC reload is funding humanoid bets long before the unit economics are proven.

title: Jalapeño: OpenAI's new Chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom released Jalapeño, OpenAI's first in-house inference chip, built around its own LLM roadmap and already running GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark on engineering samples in the lab. Early testing shows performance per watt substantially better than current state-of-the-art, and the chip went from design to production in nine months, accelerated by OpenAI's own models. It is the clearest sign yet that OpenAI wants the full stack, from models to products to silicon, with deployment planned at gigawatt scale across multiple generations alongside data center partners.

title: Washington’s Foreign Ban on Anthropic’s Top Models May Backfire
blurb: The White House banned foreign access to Anthropic's top models, citing national security, but the likely effect is to push overseas buyers toward China's open-source alternatives. Cut off from frontier US models, developers in the affected markets default to what they can actually run, and China's open-weights labs are already positioned to absorb that demand. The lesson for founders building on US frontier APIs is that export policy is now a supply risk, and a customer base concentrated abroad can be regulated away from your stack overnight.

title: Founding a company in Germany: €9600, 152 days and I still can't send an invoice
blurb: A founder spent more than €9,600 and 152 days incorporating his second company in Germany, and he still can't send a single invoice. The notary, two courts, a law firm, a tax firm, and the software vendors all billed him on time, while the one VAT ID he needs for reverse-charge invoicing to foreign clients is still coming by post. Everyone in the chain can invoice him; he is the only one who can't invoice anyone.

title: The White House is asking OpenAI to slow roll the release of its new model over safety concerns
blurb: The White House is reportedly pressuring OpenAI to limit the release of GPT-5.6, sharing it only with a select group of partners that the government will approve customer by customer during a preview period. The ask follows an executive order directing AI companies to submit new models for federal testing before public release, and it pushes OpenAI toward the gated approach Anthropic already took with its Project Glasswing cyber model. For founders building on frontier APIs, model access is becoming a regulated pipeline, where the most capable systems may reach the broad market weeks late or not at all.

title: OpenAI staggers GPT-5.6 rollout for government vetting, eyes 2027 IPO
blurb: Washington is now gatekeeping frontier model launches, with OpenAI agreeing to roll GPT-5.6 out customer by customer for government vetting before any wide release, at the request of the Trump administration. The move follows the same template the administration used to pull Anthropic's Mythos offline under emergency export controls, and it shifts the timing of major releases from the labs to two federal agencies worried about cyber capabilities reaching adversaries. OpenAI is absorbing that scrutiny while leaning toward a 2027 IPO rather than filing this year, which would rank among the largest debuts ever.

title: Apple Shares Fall After Prices Increase for Macs, iPads
blurb: Apple raised prices across all Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro to offset a memory and storage chip shortage, and the stock fell on the news. The hikes are global and largely without precedent in the company's modern history, which is the more important signal. When a vendor with Apple's supply-chain leverage can't absorb component costs and has to pass them through, every hardware-dependent business should expect the same memory and storage squeeze to land on their own bill of materials.

title: Anthropic’s Claude is winning over paid consumers, a market owned by ChatGPT
blurb: Anthropic's Claude is gaining on ChatGPT among consumers who pay for AI, with paying-consumer revenue up about 75% since January 2026 in credit card data from Indagari, which tracks billions of transactions across 28 million U.S. consumers. The signal isn't just spend: on DataCamp, "Claude" is now the most-searched term ahead of "AI" itself, and self-directed consumer demand for Claude courses is outpacing ChatGPT three to one. ChatGPT still has far more paying users, but Claude is no longer just the developer tool it's typically pegged as.

title: Micron stock jumps 15% as soaring prices from memory crunch lead to quadrupling of revenue
blurb: Micron's fiscal Q3 revenue more than quadrupled to $41.46B from $9.3B a year earlier, and it guided to roughly $50B next quarter as AI demand keeps memory in short supply. Gross margin hit 84.9%, up from 39% a year ago, and the stock is up about 700% over the past year, pushing the market cap past $1 trillion. The CEO expects shortages to persist into 2028, and Micron has locked in 16 long-term agreements worth $22 billion that put half or more of revenue under multi-year contracts.

title: Qualcomm inks deal for AI startup Modular to bolster software stack, data center build-out
blurb: Qualcomm agreed to buy AI infrastructure startup Modular for a reported $3.92B to strengthen its software stack and push into the data center inference market. The buy is a bet on horizontal, developer-friendly platforms that run AI across different compute, positioning Qualcomm against Nvidia's grip on inference. The timing tracks a real operator pain point: enterprises are scrambling to cut AI spend as token costs eat into budgets, and the labs and chipmakers are racing to own the layer that makes inference cheaper to run.

title: Qualcomm’s $3.9 billion purchase of Modular aims to change the data center dynamic
blurb: Qualcomm bought Modular, the AI software company run by Chris Lattner, for $3.9B to build a silicon-agnostic compute layer that runs across CPUs, GPUs, NPUs, and custom ASICs. The bet is that enterprises locked into one accelerator vendor want flexibility, since CIOs are committing to data center architectures without knowing what AI hardware will look like in two years. Analysts doubt Qualcomm can pull real share from Nvidia, but they agree the pain point is genuine, as heterogeneous accelerators for different workloads are becoming the default rather than the exception.

title: Sources: SpaceX COO Gwynne Shotwell told investors during an IPO roadshow SpaceX may launch a Starlink mobile product and build its own terrestrial US network (Financial Times)
blurb: SpaceX is signaling a move down the stack, with COO Gwynne Shotwell telling IPO-roadshow investors it may launch a Starlink mobile product and build its own terrestrial US network.
