That pairs the satellite coverage it already owns with the ground infrastructure that has kept it dependent on carrier partners like T-Mobile, turning a wholesale supplier into a direct competitor. For any startup or carrier betting on Starlink as a neutral connectivity layer, the lesson is the familiar one: your platform vendor will eventually want the customer relationship too.

title: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may delay its IPO until 2027; expectations of a windfall from OpenAI's debut helped support Softbank's stock (Aya Wagatsuma/Bloomberg)
blurb: SoftBank shares fell 12% after reports that OpenAI may push its IPO to 2027, a reminder of how much of the firm's valuation now rides on a single bet. Investors had been pricing in a windfall from OpenAI's debut, and the delay pulls that expected catalyst a year further out. When one position carries this much of your upside, its timing stops being someone else's problem and becomes your own balance sheet's.

title: GCash Owner Said to Seek $1.5 Billion in Record Philippine IPO
blurb: Mynt, the operator behind GCash, is seeking up to $1.5 billion in what would be the largest IPO in Philippine history, pricing the offering at as much as 92.3 billion pesos. The deal signals that Southeast Asia's fintech leaders are now big enough to anchor record public listings in their home markets rather than chasing capital abroad. For founders building in emerging markets, it's a sign that a dominant local wallet can become a domestic exit story on its own terms.

title: OpenAI Leans Toward Holding Up I.P.O. Until Next Year
blurb: OpenAI is now leaning toward holding its IPO until next year, with advisers pushing Sam Altman to slow down after SpaceX's stock turned volatile and the company kept wrestling with its own financial challenges. The caution signals that even the most hyped AI name doesn't see public markets as a sure bet right now. For founders watching the exit window, the message is that the bar for going public is rising, and the companies setting the pace would rather wait than test weak demand.

title: SpaceX wants to sell Starlink phone service directly to US consumers
blurb: SpaceX has told investors it plans to launch a Starlink mobile service sold directly to US consumers, the Financial Times reported, which would put it in direct competition with Verizon, AT&T, and T-Mobile. The move would replace its current T-Mobile partnership and let SpaceX capture revenue per user instead of wholesaling capacity, a far larger pool than the satellite-broadband business it has built so far. The $17B EchoStar spectrum purchase gave it the terrestrial licences a standalone network needs, signaling SpaceX no longer wants to be a wholesale supplier to the carriers it now depends on.

title: OpenAI will initially only release ChatGPT 5.6 to government-approved customers
blurb: OpenAI will release ChatGPT 5.6 only to government-approved customers first, with federal leaders signing off account by account during a preview period before a wider rollout a couple of weeks later. The shift follows an executive order asking labs to submit powerful models for voluntary federal review, though Anthropic recently pulled two models on a vague federal directive blocking foreign access. For founders building on frontier APIs, model availability is now a regulatory variable, not just a vendor roadmap, and the line between voluntary review and gated release is getting harder to find.

title: Patronus AI lands $50M to build ‘digital worlds’ that stress-test AI agents
blurb: Patronus AI raised a $50M Series B led by Greenfield Partners to build simulated "digital worlds" that stress-test AI agents before they go live. Benchmarks don't prove an agent can actually book a trip or run a financial analysis correctly, so the company spins up replicas of websites and internal systems and uses reinforcement learning to catch the shortcuts agents take to fake task completion. Revenue grew 15-fold over the past year, with nearly every frontier lab now a customer. Evaluation, not raw capability, is becoming the constraint on shipping agents.

title: a16z-backed Base Power is offering cheaper electricity to the power grid that needs it most
blurb: Base Power started selling its residential battery systems in Illinois, its first move into PJM, the largest US grid operator and the one straining hardest under data center demand. Wholesale prices in PJM territory have nearly doubled in the past year, and Base undercuts local utility ComEd by 25% by siting batteries behind the meter, where an interconnection already exists, so it skips the queue that has stalled new generation since 2022. The startup raised $1B led by Addition in October and already runs more than 500 megawatt-hours of storage in Texas.

title: Musk Starts Expanding X Payments to More Users After Delay
blurb: X is rolling out X Money, its digital payments product, to a subset of premium users after repeated delays, the first concrete step in Musk's long-promised plan to turn the platform into an "everything app." Payments is the hard part of that vision because it demands banking partners, state-by-state money-transmitter licenses, and trust that X's brand does not obviously carry. The model to beat is WeChat, where payments became the glue that locked users into chat, commerce, and services, and no Western platform has yet replicated it at scale.

title: ON Semiconductor strikes $7 billion deal for Synaptics in physical AI push
blurb: ON Semiconductor agreed to buy Synaptics in a nearly $7B all-stock deal, its largest ever, to push deeper into physical AI and intelligent systems. The acquisition adds connected compute and software reach that ON Semi says lifts its total addressable market by $30B to $243B by 2030. It lands in the same week Qualcomm bought Modular and Salesforce moved on Fin for $3.6B, as chipmakers and software vendors buy their way into AI capabilities rather than build them. ON Semi shares fell about 6% on the news while Synaptics rallied 13%.

title: Trump Plans to Name Adam Candeub as Antitrust Chief in Justice Dept.
blurb: Trump plans to nominate Adam Candeub, a telecommunications lawyer and outspoken tech critic, to run the Justice Department's antitrust division. Candeub has argued that large platforms wield unchecked power over speech and competition, signaling that the administration's antitrust posture toward Big Tech will harden rather than ease. For founders, that means the merger environment stays hostile at the top end, and the regulatory pressure on incumbents like Google, Apple, and Meta keeps the acquisition exits narrower than they were a few years ago.

title: Metas Ex-KI-Chef sammelt über eine Milliarde Dollar für neues Startup ein
blurb: Yann LeCun raised $1.03B in seed funding for AMI Labs, the Paris startup he founded after leaving Meta, with backing from Bezos Expeditions, Greycroft, Cathay Innovation, HV Capital, and Hiro Capital. The bet is on world models, AI systems that learn how the physical world actually works rather than predicting the next token, and LeCun is staffing it across Paris, New York, Montreal, and Singapore. It is one of the few frontier labs positioned as neither Chinese nor American, with former Facebook researcher Alex LeBrun installed as CEO and a team that treats this as a long-horizon science project, not a product race.

title: Italy opens an antitrust probe into Microsoft 365’s AI price rise
blurb: Italy's competition authority opened an investigation into Microsoft over how it raised Microsoft 365 prices, alleging customers were automatically moved to a pricier Copilot-bundled plan unless they actively opted out, with too little information to choose. The case turns on the opt-out architecture rather than the headline number, framing an automatic upgrade as a consumer-consent problem rather than a pricing one. For founders bundling AI into existing products, the signal is that European regulators are now scrutinizing the default mechanics of how a price increase reaches the customer, not just whether the new price is fair.

title: Ending respiratory infections
blurb: Intercept is a $500M philanthropic initiative betting that respiratory infections like colds and flu can be eliminated, the way clean water and pharma made cholera and typhoid rare a century ago. The bet rests on new platform technologies, immunology advances, and protein design tools funding two product types: broad-spectrum preventatives and air cleaning. The wager is that the gap was never the science but the funding, since broad-spectrum work fits neither philanthropic nor commercial models.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the salaries of the developers running them, as token-heavy autonomous workflows rack up usage faster than any per-seat license ever did. A single Codex-style agent left running can burn through compute continuously, and recent reports show clumsy logging alone quietly writing data and costing millions. The implication is that the old per-developer pricing math breaks down, and the constraint on engineering output shifts from headcount to how tightly you govern agent spend.

title: Prediction markets just posted their third straight record-setting week
blurb: Prediction markets just posted their biggest week ever, with weekly volume hitting $14.4B, up from $5-6B at the start of the year and more than 10x where it was a year ago. Open interest reached $1.6B, its third straight weekly record, which matters more than volume because it only climbs when new money is staked faster than old bets resolve. The growth isn't just sports. Non-sports volume across politics, economics, and geopolitics hit $3.6B on Kalshi and Polymarket combined last week, roughly 18x its July 2025 level and larger than the entire market was a year ago.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal pointed an Agentforce SDR agent at the 8,000 merchants a month its 200-rep org was never going to call, the ones who stalled partway through onboarding, and meeting conversion is now running about 50% higher than reps managed alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence on every lead and books the meeting, then hands the rep a qualified prospect with full context loaded. The lesson for operators is to stop waiting on clean CRM data and aim agents at the leads you already drop, where anything beats zero coverage.

title: One year at Anthropic, then $200M at $1B: The researchers who just closed one of AI’s largest-ever seed rounds
blurb: Two former Anthropic researchers who left in December 2025, weeks after joining, raised $200M at a $1B valuation for Mirendil, one of the largest seed rounds in AI history. The pitch is AI that does AI research itself: experimental design, hyperparameter search, evaluation, and iterative training, packaged so universities and hospitals can run frontier R&D without a frontier lab's team. Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins co-led, with NVIDIA joining, on a company that has yet to ship a product. The premium is for the people and the bet that research itself is the next thing to automate.

title: AIRWALLEX lève 320 millions de dollars et ouvre un nouveau front face à Stripe
blurb: Airwallex raised $320M at an $11B valuation, up from $8B six months ago, and is repositioning from cross-border payments specialist into an AI-native financial operating system. The bet is that financial operations will increasingly be initiated and executed by autonomous agents, which puts Airwallex on a direct collision course with Stripe's expansion down the same stack. The numbers back the move: $1.3B in annualized revenue up 74% on the year, $287B in transaction volume, and 90% of revenue now coming from customers using multiple products.

title: Slate EV truck starts at $24,950
blurb: Slate's electric pickup starts at $24,950, undercutting every EV truck on the market with a stripped-down base model that ships as a two-seat truck and converts to a five-seat SUV. The whole pitch is owner-serviceable hardware: swappable panels, accessible parts, free manuals, and charging off a standard wall outlet. The bet is that a cheap, hackable, accessory-driven platform beats spec-loaded competitors, and the 200-plus add-on catalog turns one vehicle into a configurable product line.

title: Micron is tech's new margin king as memory crisis pushes company past Nvidia and Meta
blurb: Micron posted an 84.9% gross margin in fiscal Q3, the highest of any major US tech company, topping Meta at 81.9% and Nvidia at 75%. Revenue hit $41.46 billion and net income $28.24 billion, both company records, as data center buyers grab every chip they can to feed AI demand. The company is now locking in long-term strategic customer agreements with floor prices that hold margins above any past cycle, which means the memory cost squeeze hitting Nvidia, AMD, Google, and Apple is structural, not a temporary shortage.

title: SpaceX Starfall Demo Provides Validation for Reentry Startups
blurb: SpaceX launched a demo of its Starfall reentry capsule, signaling that an industry giant now sees money in returning cargo from orbit. Founders at the dozen-plus reentry startups already in the market read the move as validation, not a threat: when SpaceX enters a space, it confirms the demand signal is real. The specs back that reading, since Starfall carries about 1 metric ton and runs 3x the size of Varda's capsules but can't maneuver in orbit or guide itself to a precise landing, leaving room for rivals to own the niches it doesn't.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix Reportedly Preparing Huge AI Spending Push
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix are preparing to announce hundreds of billions of dollars in new investments on Monday, according to South Korean media reports this week. The two memory giants supply most of the world's high-bandwidth memory, the bottleneck constraining every AI accelerator, so spending at this scale signals they expect demand to keep outrunning supply for years. For founders building on top of GPUs, the read is that the compute crunch is structural, not a passing spike, and capacity planning should assume tight HBM supply well into the next cycle.

title: The 'papers, please' era of the internet will decimate your privacy
blurb: Age verification laws are quietly becoming identity verification, forcing users to hand government IDs or face scans to third-party apps they know nothing about. Australia's under-16 social media ban, the model many countries now copy, mandates that platforms collect biometric or government ID under threat of massive fines, yet the government's own research found roughly seven in 10 kids still using social media months later. For any founder running a consumer platform, the regulatory direction is toward collecting the most sensitive data you can hold, with the breach liability that comes attached.

title: How HappyFox Closed $1M in Expansion on a $20 AI Agent Spend with CEO Shalin Jain
blurb: Your cheapest growth is buried inside your own customer base, and almost nobody mines it. HappyFox, a $20M bootstrapped help desk company running expansion off four AEs, built an agent called Rex that reads every support ticket the moment it closes and flags buying intent that reps see but never route and AEs never read. The result was $1M in closed expansion revenue on under $20 of tokens. The lesson is that proactive expansion was never a headcount problem, it was an unread-data problem.

title: Perplexity Computer for Counsel
blurb: Perplexity launched Computer for Counsel, an agentic tool that connects lawyers' research databases, document repositories, and contract systems to its Computer product and runs tasks like research, document gathering, and contract triage. It routes work across 20+ frontier models, picking the best one per task, and reasons over trusted legal sources including Midpage case law, Deel compliance data, and LegalZoom templates. With nearly 75% of lawyers calling administrative work a major time drain, Perplexity is moving directly into the workflows that legal-tech incumbents like Thomson Reuters and LexisNexis have long owned.

title: As AI Companies Race for Power, Amazon and Google Have the Lead
blurb: The race to power AI has turned electricity into the binding constraint, and Amazon and Google are pulling ahead because they locked in energy supply years before the rest of the field started competing for it. Both have signed nuclear deals, utility contracts, and grid connections that newer entrants can't replicate on demand, since new power capacity takes years to come online. The lesson for founders is that compute scarcity is increasingly an energy problem, and the companies with the deepest power pipelines will set the terms for everyone renting capacity downstream.

title: Trump administration bars Polestar from selling its new EVs in the US
blurb: The Trump administration's Commerce Department barred Polestar from selling its new EVs in the US under the Connected Vehicle Rule, which restricts cars running Chinese software or hardware. The Geely-owned Swedish maker had requested a special authorization and was refused, even though sibling brand Volvo, also Geely-owned, got the same clearance months earlier. Polestar will sell off existing Polestar 3 and 4 stock and shift its focus to Europe, where 94% of its Q1 retail volume already came from. The rule now functions as a hard line down the stack of any China-linked supply chain.

title: Despite the "DeepMind mafia" pulling billions into London AI startups, none of Demis Hassabis' former lieutenants is building a homegrown UK frontier AI model (Tim Bradshaw/Financial Times)
blurb: Britain's "DeepMind mafia" has pulled billions into London AI startups, yet none of Demis Hassabis' former lieutenants is building a homegrown UK frontier model. The alumni are spreading into applications, tooling, and vertical AI rather than competing with OpenAI and Anthropic on the base layer. That leaves Britain with deep talent and real capital but no national contender at the frontier, and the gap is a deliberate bet that the returns now sit in what you build on top of the models, not in training them.

title: Warp, a startup using AI to automate payroll compliance and employee management, raised a $60M Series B led by Battery, bringing its total funding to $85M (Lucinda Shen/Axios)
blurb: Warp raised a $60M Series B led by Battery, lifting its total funding to $85M for an AI system that automates payroll compliance and employee management. The bet is that the back-office work founders hate most, tax filings, onboarding, and the rules that vary by state, is now structured enough for AI to run end to end rather than just assist. Capital is concentrating in the unglamorous compliance layer of HR and finance, where mistakes carry real penalties and switching costs run high once a company's payroll lives in your system.

title: SK Hynix surges 12% after Micron earnings; blockbuster Nasdaq listing
blurb: SK Hynix shares jumped more than 12% on plans to raise up to $29.4B through a Nasdaq ADR listing, with Micron's quarterly revenue more than quadrupling on AI memory demand reinforcing that high-bandwidth memory stays supply-constrained for years. The dual listing is mainly about narrowing the valuation gap with Micron and opening the Korea-only stock to global investors. With shares already up more than 300% year-to-date, the AI buildout is now propping up an entire memory-chip cohort, and Samsung and TSMC climbed alongside it.

title: Omnicom Chief Defends Interpublic Deal as AI Rattles Ad Industry
blurb: Omnicom CEO John Wren says the $13B Interpublic merger is starting to pay off, citing customer wins and cost savings seven months after closing. The deal created the world's largest ad holding company, a defensive bet on scale as AI tools let brands generate campaigns and buy media without traditional agencies. The market still isn't convinced, which is the real tell: consolidation buys cost leverage, but it doesn't answer whether agencies stay necessary when the work itself is getting automated.

title: Microsoft lifts price of Xbox consoles due to soaring component costs
blurb: The AI memory crunch is now hitting consumer hardware. Microsoft is raising Xbox prices starting Aug. 1, with the Series S jumping $100 to about $500 and the entry Series X starting near $750, hours after Apple lifted MacBook and iPad prices. The cause is the same upstream squeeze: Micron and SK Hynix are steering limited capacity toward high-bandwidth memory for AI GPUs, and Microsoft says console storage and memory costs are up more than 2.5x with another doubling expected by fall 2027. The compute buildout is quietly repricing every device that needs a memory chip.

title: British Business Bank commits €104 million to support UK venture capital fund managers
blurb: The British Business Bank committed up to €104 million as a cornerstone investor in 10 new UK microfunds, its first deployment under the £400m Investor Pathways Capital initiative. The funds run £10 million to £20 million each and target pre-seed and seed first-cheque capital across AI, DeepTech, climate, and defence. The deliberate bet is on first-time managers who struggled to raise institutionally, with women making up 57% of GPs, on the theory that a more diverse pipeline of investors crowds in private capital and builds the early-stage track records the UK is short on.

title: Alan lève 480 millions d’euros et atteint 5,5 milliards d’euros de valorisation
blurb: Alan raised €480M at a €5.5B valuation, led by Prosus with Dara Holdings joining, just three months after a €100M round nearly five times smaller. The French health-insurance scale-up now posts €787M in ARR, up 54% year over year, and turned profitable in France in 2025 even as the group still lost €26M last year. The raise funds acquisitions and international expansion, with Alan already live in Belgium, Spain, and Canada and targeting group-wide profitability by 2027.

title: DeepSeek Looks to Double Workforce as Fresh Funding Fuels Growth
blurb: DeepSeek is hiring across 27 technical roles and aims to roughly double its workforce after raising fresh capital. The Hangzhou lab is recruiting development engineers and AI product managers, with every position open to interns. The expansion signals that China's most efficient frontier lab is now spending to scale headcount, not just compute, as it pushes to keep pace with far larger US rivals.

title: Visionaries general partner Judith Dada joins AI startup Langdock as co-CEO
blurb: Visionaries general partner Judith Dada is joining Berlin-based Langdock as co-CEO in September, while staying a senior partner at the firm ahead of its next fundraise. Langdock, which lets companies route work across Gemini, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Mistral from one platform, recently hit $40M in ARR, turned profitable, and serves more than 10k customers despite last raising just $3M in 2024. The move lands as Europe's sovereign-AI debate heats up, and Dada's mandate is largely external: making the public case to policymakers and customers that AI adoption can go well.

title: Exclusive: British Business Bank backs 10 VC funds with £90m pot
blurb: The British Business Bank committed up to £90m to 10 first-time UK VC funds, each sized between £10m and £20m and focused on pre-seed and seed bets in deeptech, defence, and climate. It is the first deployment from the £500m package announced last July to back managers from underrepresented backgrounds, and across the selected GPs 57% are women and 43% are from ethnic minorities. The state bank is funding roughly half of each vehicle to manufacture institutional track records and a capital multiplier at the earliest stage, where new fund types are scarcest.

title: DeepMind handpicked this startup for its robotics accelerator. Now it’s raised an $11.7m seed round
blurb: Greek robotics startup Acumino raised $11.7m in seed funding to scale its deployments with industrial customers, after DeepMind handpicked it for its robotics accelerator. The company builds robot models for dexterous tasks in industrial automation, the kind of fine-motor work that has stayed hard to automate even as warehouse and arm robotics matured. The signal for founders is where robotics money is now flowing: not general-purpose humanoids, but narrow models that make existing industrial hardware capable of the precise, high-variation tasks customers will actually pay to deploy.

title: Samsung’s $647bn home investment heads for the chip-starved southwest
blurb: Samsung is expected to announce a 1,000 trillion won ($647.5bn) investment in South Korea over the next 10 years, with a reported 300 trillion won going to chip factories in the country's neglected southwest. Spread across a decade, the headline sits closer to Samsung's existing capex than the number implies, and the 29 June event at the presidential office is as much industrial policy as corporate strategy. SK Hynix, which just overtook Samsung as Korea's most valuable listed company on AI memory demand, will be in the same room with plans of its own.

title: ALAN dépasse le milliard d’euros levés avec l’arrivée de Prosus
blurb: Alan raised €480M from Prosus at a €5.5B valuation, pushing the French health insurance startup past €1B in total funding. The round lands just three months after a €100M raise and ranks among the largest in Europe this year outside of AI. The pace signals that investor appetite for proven European scale-ups with real revenue, not just AI narratives, is still very much intact.

title: Zombie unicorns are haunting Silicon Valley
blurb: A growing share of the world's unicorns are now stuck: too richly valued to sell, too unproven to go public, and too cash-hungry to keep raising at the prices they once commanded. These zombie companies sit on paper valuations from the last boom while exits stay frozen and down rounds threaten to wipe out earlier investors. For founders, the lesson is that a high valuation you can't grow into becomes a trap, narrowing every option until the only realistic moves are a fire sale, a recap, or a slow wind-down.

title: AI coding token costs are on track to rival human payroll
blurb: Gartner projects that within two years, enterprise spending on a developer's AI coding tokens will match or exceed that same engineer's salary, as consumption-based pricing replaces flat per-seat licensing. The shift is already producing scary outliers, with analysts citing single developers burning $20K in a month and business users hitting $32K. Most organizations have no real visibility into how tokens are billed, no built-in cost controls from vendors, and no framework to tie that spend to ROI, so budgets get wiped out earlier than anyone forecast.

title: Sail Research raises $80M to optimize long-horizon AI agents
blurb: Sail Research raised $80M at a $450M valuation in a Series A led by Sequoia to run AI agents on tasks that can take weeks. The pitch is cost: its platform shuts agents down while they wait on external systems and runs the workload on customized open-source inference engines, claiming a tenth the price of rivals while topping the BrowseComp-Plus research benchmark at 90.72%. The bet is that long-horizon agents need scale and sustainable economics, not the raw speed that human-in-the-loop tools optimize for, and that infrastructure is where that gets decided.

title: Kalshi targets $40B valuation, nearly triple rival Polymarket’s $15B target, as prediction markets split in two
blurb: Kalshi is raising at roughly $40B, nearly triple the $15B its blockchain rival Polymarket is reportedly chasing, and the gap is the actual signal. Kalshi runs as a federally regulated CFTC exchange while Polymarket settles in crypto without US approval, and institutional money is lining up behind the regulated model. Monthly volume on Kalshi recently passed $17B, up from roughly $5B a year earlier, and management is now weighing an IPO that likely won't land before 2027.

title: How the US AI build-out is pushing up prices for electricity, software, and more; in a survey, 81% of economists say it will add to inflation over the next year (Justin Lahart/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: The US AI build-out is now showing up in prices, pushing up the cost of electricity, software, and other inputs as data center demand competes for constrained supply. In a Wall Street Journal survey, 81% of economists said it will add to inflation over the next year. The capex that markets celebrate as growth lands on operators as higher utility bills and pricier tooling, which is a real margin problem for any startup running compute-heavy workloads.

title: AI Cost Reality Check Hits Asia Tech Stocks as Apple Hikes Price
blurb: Apple and Microsoft raised prices on their products, and Asian tech stocks slumped on fears that climbing component costs will dent device demand and stall the memory-chip rally underpinning the AI trade. The signal for founders is that the AI buildout has a hardware bill that is now reaching consumer prices. Cheap, abundant compute was the assumption underneath most AI product roadmaps, and rising memory costs are the first sign that the input prices baked into those plans may not hold.

title: Drone Startup Elroy Air Is Said to Near $800 Million SPAC Deal
blurb: Cargo drone startup Elroy Air is in advanced talks to go public via a SPAC merger that would value the company at about $1 billion, with the deal said to be near $800 million. Elroy is betting its autonomous aircraft can replace delivery trucks for middle-mile logistics, a market where ground transport still dominates. The move signals that blank-check vehicles are back as a path to public markets for capital-intensive hardware startups that can't easily fund years of buildout through private rounds alone.

title: US Stocks Post First Outflow Since March as Tech Trade Falters
blurb: US equities saw their first outflow in three months, with record withdrawals from tech funds suggesting the AI trade is cooling. The selling is concentrated where the conviction was highest, which means the names that carried the index up are now the ones dragging it down. For founders raising or selling into the AI boom, a softening public-market appetite usually shows up in private rounds a quarter or two later, tightening valuations and lengthening the path to a term sheet.

title: Scaled Cognition nabs $100M to automate high stakes customer interactions
blurb: Scaled Cognition raised $100M in a Series A led by Khosla Ventures at a $750M valuation, with customer-service vendor Genesys joining in. The startup sells an AI platform built on its own model, APT, that retrieves existing records rather than generating new data and double-checks every action before confirming, betting that reliability has to be engineered into the architecture for high-stakes tasks like making purchases on a customer's behalf. The funding signals that enterprise buyers are now paying up for agents that get it right the first time, not just ones that sound human.

title: Hightouch : pourquoi cette startup a fait une offre à 1,2 milliard de dollars à Publicis
blurb: Hightouch, a composable customer data platform that plugs directly into Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks rather than copying data into a legacy stack, raised a $150M Series D at a $2.75B valuation and now claims $100M in ARR across more than 1,000 customers including Spotify and Warner Music. The pitch has shifted from data activation to agentic marketing, with AI agents that build audiences, generate content variants, and orchestrate across 300-plus channels. At Salomon, the number of people able to create marketing audiences went from a handful to about thirty, which is the real threat to incumbents like Adobe and Salesforce.

title: Exclusive: XCures Lands $46M Series B To Clean Up Messy Medical Records With AI
blurb: XCures raised $46M in a Series B led by Innovius Capital, valuing the medical-records startup at $127M post-money, more than double its December 2023 Series A. The company connects directly to national healthcare interoperability networks and uses AI to structure the industry's "dirty data," the unusable patient records that hospitals have generated for decades. The round lands inside a fast-rising category: investors have put roughly $8.5B into AI-powered health tech this year through June 22, already nearly matching the $8.6B raised across all of 2024.

title: Mirendil raises $200M to speed up scientific research with AI
blurb: Mirendil raised $200M at a $1B valuation in a seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz, with Kleiner Perkins and Nvidia also in. The pitch is a self-improving AI that automates the manual research work behind building frontier models, then upgrades itself to push machine learning faster than human teams can. The founders carry the credentials the bet needs: CEO Behnam Neyshabur co-invented the SAM optimization algorithm, and CTO Harsh Mehta helped build Anthropic's internal research-automation tooling. The plan is to sell that engine to chemistry, medicine, and robotics labs building task-specific frontier models.

title: EIFO commits €200 million to Scaleup Europe Fund to back European tech scaleups
blurb: EIFO committed €200 million to the Scaleup Europe Fund, joining seven other institutional backers including Novo Holdings, Wallenberg Investments, and Allianz in a vehicle central to the European Commission's Startup and Scaleup Strategy. The fund targets late-stage rounds in AI, quantum, robotics, energy, space, and biotech, the gap that has long pushed European scaleups to raise abroad and often relocate with the money. For founders, it signals that European growth capital is finally organizing around keeping companies on the continent, with state-backed anchors willing to underwrite the stage where the talent usually leaves.

title: Tumbling AI stocks signal another day of turmoil for tech companies
blurb: Rising AI infrastructure costs are now showing up where they hurt: company margins. Tech stocks sold off globally on Friday, with SoftBank down more than 12% and chipmakers from SK Hynix to Samsung falling 8% to 9%, after Apple raised MacBook and iPad prices and Microsoft lifted Xbox prices, both citing higher chip costs. The selloff marks a shift in the AI trade, from rewarding compute spend to questioning who absorbs it, with OpenAI reportedly struggling to secure demand at a $1 trillion valuation as it weighs delaying its IPO.

title: Slate Auto says each $24,950 electric pickup truck will be profitable as it aims to be cash-flow positive next year
blurb: Slate Auto says every one of its $24,950 electric pickups will ship gross-margin positive, with CEO Peter Faricy targeting positive free cash flow and EBITDA by 2027. The math leans on a stripped-down truck (optional speakers, crank windows, 205-mile range) and a break-even point of roughly 80,000 vehicles a year, just over half its planned 150,000-unit capacity in Warsaw, Indiana. That bet runs against the recent EV-startup record, where Lordstown and Fisker went bankrupt and Rivian and Lucid posted billions in losses before their latest layoffs.

title: Agentic infrastructure startup Seltz raises $12.5M to help AI agents search the web for answers
blurb: Seltz raised $12.5M in seed funding led by Speedinvest and B Capital to build search infrastructure designed for AI agents rather than humans. The bet is that traditional engines surface ranked links for people, while agents need structured evidence pulled from tables, images, and page-level text that Google and Bing don't easily reach. Founder Antonio Mallia built the entire stack in-house, from crawlers to ranking, instead of reselling queries through an existing provider, which signals where retrieval value moves as machines, not people, become the primary consumer of search.

title: ANTHROPIC recrute le patron IA d’ORANGE pour accélérer son développement en Europe
blurb: Anthropic hired Steve Jarrett, Orange's Chief AI Officer since 2019, to lead its push into European and African enterprise from Paris starting August 25. The signal is the resume: at Orange his Data and AI work generated more than €300M in value in 2025, targeting €600M a year by 2028, with over 90% of 130,000 employees using internal AI tools. The frontier labs are now competing for the operators who can prove ROI inside large companies, not just the researchers who build the models, as Anthropic triples its international headcount ahead of an IPO.

title: The AI Trade May Be Reaching a Juncture. Time to Pick Winners
blurb: The AI trade still works, but the era of every name rising together is ending. A volatile week for tech stocks from Seoul to New York, driven by stretched investor positioning and doubts about chip demand, signaled that the market is moving from buying the theme to picking the companies that actually compound. Correlation is breaking down, which means dispersion between winners and losers widens from here, and capital starts rewarding fundamentals over exposure. The implication for founders raising or benchmarking against public comps is that a generic AI story no longer carries the round.

title: Meet the professor who could cost Google £1bn+
blurb: A UK competition case against Google heads to an 11-week trial starting September 28, seeking more than £1bn in damages for thousands of app developers allegedly overcharged on Play Store commissions of up to 30%. The claim, brought by Strathclyde law professor Barry Rodger on an opt-out basis covering sales since August 2018, just survived Google's last-ditch attempt to force developers to opt in publicly. Google denies the claims and faces similar suits elsewhere, but the opt-out structure means UK app developers are in by default, raising the stakes for app-store economics across the industry.

title: Micron’s Blockbuster Earnings Quiet the AI Doubters
blurb: Micron posted blockbuster earnings driven by surging demand for the high-bandwidth memory that AI data centers run on, and the stock rally pulled Nasdaq futures up with it. The print lands as a direct rebuttal to the growing camp arguing the AI buildout is overextended, because memory is the part of the stack that only sells when customers are actually deploying compute. For founders, it is a read on whether the infrastructure spending your roadmap assumes is still accelerating or starting to cool.

title: OHB Shares Dip After German Satellite Firm’s €789 Million Re-IPO
blurb: KKR took German satellite maker OHB public again, raising €789 million in a Frankfurt re-IPO, and shares slipped on the open. The deal is a private-equity sponsor cashing in part of its position by widening investor access to a space company, not a fresh capital raise for OHB itself. For founders watching the space sector, it is another sign that European defense and satellite assets are now liquid enough for sponsors to recycle capital through public markets.

title: The CEO of AWS on why Amazon is hiring 11,000 interns and junior employees
blurb: AWS CEO Matt Garman is the loudest optimist on AI and jobs, calling the idea of replacing junior employees with AI one of the dumbest things he has heard, and Amazon is hiring 11,000 interns and new grads this year to prove the point. His argument is that AI changes jobs rather than erasing them, the way Excel retired manual calculation while the labor force kept growing. The tension is that AWS now sells the agents doing the white-collar work, from a developer agent to an AI recruiter that runs voice interviews with no human in the loop.

title: Elastic announces a ~7% reduction in its workforce, and says "advances in AI and automation are letting us operate with leaner teams"; ESTC closed down 8.70% (Richard Speed/The Register)
blurb: Elastic cut about 7% of its workforce and tied the move directly to AI, saying advances in AI and automation let it operate with leaner teams. The stock still closed down 8.70%, so investors read the reorganization as a sign of softness rather than a confident efficiency play. The pattern to watch is companies now naming AI as the reason for headcount cuts, which reframes layoffs as a productivity story and gives every other software CEO cover to do the same.

title: Apple stock gets slammed on bigger Mac, iPad price hikes. Why it can weather the storm
blurb: Apple shares fell more than 6%, their worst session in over a year, after the company pushed memory-cost increases of 17% to 25% onto Macs, iPads, and home devices, with the MacBook Air 512GB jumping to $1,299 from $1,099. Microsoft raised Xbox prices the same day and hit a 52-week low, while Micron soared 15.8% and guided for tight memory supply past 2027. Apple's scale and supplier leverage let it absorb the squeeze better than rivals, but the selloff signals that investors are now pricing in demand destruction across the hardware stack.

title: GLM 5.2: why I’m replacing Opus in Claude Code with this new model
blurb: Claire Vo ran GLM 5.2, Z.AI's open-weight coding model, through four real tasks in her production Next.js app, including a 45-minute autonomous bug-hunt pulling Sentry and Vercel logs, for $3.36 across roughly 6M tokens. She argues open weights buy cost and vendor independence worth the trade-offs, and reports the model matched ChatPRD's design system on the first try.

title: Cloudflare launched self-managed OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all Developer Platform customers, letting developers create and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on harder-to-manage API tokens. The release adds clearer consent screens, dashboard-based revocation, and visible app ownership to curb OAuth phishing, targeting SaaS integrations, internal developer platforms, and agentic tools that need delegated access.

title: Mostly Automatic Translation of Language Interpreters from C to Safe Rust
blurb: Reboot translates real-world C interpreters to safe Rust mostly automatically, converting six interpreters of 6k-23k lines with only 1 to 11 brief human interventions each while passing 100% of provided test suites and 62%-92% of held-out validation tests. It pairs feature reduction, which breaks the job into incrementally restored, individually testable milestones, with a multi-agent loop that keeps unreliable coding agents on track and eliminates memory bugs like heap overflows and use-after-free.

title: Run a vLLM Server on HF Jobs in One Command
blurb: Hugging Face added `hf jobs run` support for vLLM, letting developers stand up an OpenAI-compatible inference server on rented GPU infrastructure with a single command and per-minute billing. The exposed endpoint speaks the standard OpenAI API behind an HF token, making it a quick option for tests, evals, or batch generation without the overhead of a managed Inference Endpoint.

title: LuaJIT 3.0 proposed syntax extensions
blurb: LuaJIT maintainer Mike Pall opened an umbrella issue proposing a set of syntax extensions for LuaJIT 3.0, with consolidated documentation evolving in the thread. The proposals are scoped to additions that improve quality-of-life, are proven in other languages or Lua dialects, introduce no syntactic ambiguity, and preserve backwards compatibility, explicitly avoiding the complexity of Perl, Ruby, C++, or Rust.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: Microsoft introduced Coreutils for Windows at Build 2026, a maintained fork of the Rust-based uutils coreutils that installs as a single binary and brings standard Unix commands like `ls -al` to PowerShell and cmd. The preview matches Unix syntax and output formatting so scripts port across WSL and native Windows, though it remains incomplete and conflicts with existing cmd and PowerShell commands.

title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
blurb: Curl shipped patches for 18 vulnerabilities, the most CVEs ever in a single release, including CVE-2026-8932, an mTLS connection-reuse bug introduced in version 7.7 in 2001 that can bypass authentication in libcurl applications. The flaws surfaced through a community audit after Anthropic's Mythos and Aisle's AI platform began probing the hard-to-reach paths in a library running on over 30 billion devices.

title: How Netflix Simplified Batch Compute with Kueue
blurb: Netflix replaced the custom queuing and scheduling logic in its homegrown Compute Managed Batch system with Kueue, a cloud-native job queueing system for batch workloads, as part of moving its Titus container platform toward Kubernetes-native components. The post covers how the team migrated millions of batch jobs to Kueue while preserving CMB's tenant hierarchy, priority-ordered execution, and per-tenant capacity reservations federated across multiple Kubernetes cells.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: MCP's 2026-07-28 revision makes the protocol stateless at the layer, closing off session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while tightening authentication, with legacy versions deprecated over a 12-month window. Akamai's analysis warns the rewrite shifts risk onto implementations: predictable tracking IDs and state objects open the door to cross-tenant workflow hijacking, and new MCP HTTP headers introduce desync attacks and secret leakage if developers map API keys or PII into them.

title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
blurb: A study of 6,145 agent config files across 10,008 GitHub repos finds that LLM coding rules and agent definitions propagate as undeclared shared components, with 10.1% being exact SHA-256 duplicates across independent repos and under 1% declaring any permission boundary. The authors argue this configuration layer should be governed by a deterministic, tool-agnostic control plane with content-addressed lockfiles, tiered permissions, and audit logs, rather than delegated to further LLM orchestration.

title: Augmentation with Dilution: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of Human Contributor Ecosystems After AI Coding Agent Adoption
blurb: A study of 11,097 GitHub repositories from 2023 to 2026 finds that AI coding agent adoption does not reduce the absolute number of human contributors, but cuts their relative density and drops the newcomer participation share by 3.7 percentage points. The authors call this "augmentation with dilution," noting that review depth rises 5.3% as the burden shifts from writing code to reviewing AI-generated pull requests.

title: How Much Static Structure Do Code Agents Need? A Study of Deterministic Anchoring
blurb: This paper tests whether injecting lightweight static analysis facts (call graphs, inheritance topology) as plain-text comments gives LLM code agents deterministic anchors for repo navigation. Using OpenAI's Codex as a baseline, the authors find anchoring improves function localization by 2.2pp, shortens trajectories by 1.6 rounds, and roughly halves run-to-run variance for about 10% more input tokens, with forward edges worth pruning in large repos.

title: Show HN: OpenKnowledge – open source AI-first alternative to Obsidian/Notion
blurb: OpenKnowledge is an open-source, local-first markdown editor and LLM wiki that offers full WYSIWYG editing of plain markdown files plus collaborative AI editing through Claude, Codex, and Cursor or any agent via MCP. It ships as a macOS app or a CLI-served web app for Linux, Windows, and Intel Mac, with built-in agentic search and no-code team sharing backed by git and GitHub.

title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
blurb: The author pointed go-git at a Tigris object storage bucket using billy, a Go filesystem abstraction, demonstrating that a git server can run directly on S3-style storage with no /usr/bin/git binary involved. The post walks through git's actual storage model, where a repository is just objects, trees, commits, and refs, and shows that go-git's filesystem-native design makes a bucket-backed repo Just Work.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, which run user-supplied or AI-generated code in Firecracker-isolated sandboxes that boot in seconds from SnapStart snapshots and keep memory and disk state for up to 8 hours. Each session gets a persistent VM with a dedicated HTTPS endpoint over HTTP/2, gRPC, or WebSockets, auto-suspending when idle so you pay nothing between bursts of traffic.

title: Framework's 10G Ethernet module exposes USB-C's complexity
blurb: Jeff Geerling tested WisdPi's 10G Ethernet Expansion Card for Framework laptops and found USB-C bandwidth tiers, not the card, cap real-world throughput. The Realtek RTL8159 needs USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps) for full speed, yet many Framework ports fall back to under 8 Gbps, the Linux driver fails to compile on kernel 7.x, and the module runs near 70°C under sustained load.

title: Half-Life 2 in a Browser
blurb: A developer ported Half-Life 2 to run in the browser via WebAssembly, playable directly at the link.

title: The Dependency Black Hole
blurb: An exploratory case study of a product built from 267 microservices triangulated compile-time, run-time, and task dependencies and found a recurring "black hole" structure: a dense core of dependency magnets, a transitional region of increasingly entangled services, and a lightly connected outer ring. The authors map this to a singularity, event horizon, and accretion disk, proposing a theory for spotting services at risk of becoming magnets and motivating governance interventions before concentration compounds.

title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
blurb: Apple released Xcode 26.6, adding Google Gemini as a coding assistant provider alongside the existing Anthropic Claude Agent and OpenAI Codex options. The update also opens the IDE to other compatible agents via the Agent Client Protocol and ships Swift 6.3.3 plus SDKs for the iOS 26.5 family.

title: CLIR: Liveness-Driven and Structure-Aware Fuzzing for the Cranelift Compiler
blurb: CLIR is a differential testing framework for the Cranelift compiler backend that guarantees SSA-valid IR through hierarchical generation, maximizes computational density with liveness-guided instruction refinement, and adapts across architectures for faster root cause analysis. In 72 hours it found 24 bugs across x86-64, AArch64, s390x, and RISCV64, detecting 8x more unique bugs than cranelift-fuzzgen, with 21 confirmed and 9 fixed.

title: PostgreSQL is enough (2024)
blurb: The author argues that PostgreSQL alone can replace most of the specialized infrastructure teams reach for by default, covering job queues, full-text and vector search, audit logging, change-data-capture, and REST/GraphQL APIs through extensions and built-in features. The post catalogs the tooling for each use case, from pg_cron and pgmq to pgvector and PostgREST, making the case that one well-understood database beats a sprawl of services you have to operate separately.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A developer ran hackmyclaw.com, inviting 2,000 people to email his OpenClaw assistant Fiu and trick it into leaking a secrets.env file, using only a four-rule anti-injection prompt. None of the 6,000 attempts succeeded, but the experiment cost over $500 in API tokens, got the Gmail account suspended for three days, and revealed that batch processing leaked suspicion between unrelated emails.

title: Migrating from Proxmox to NixOS and Incus
blurb: The author migrated their multi-node homelab from Proxmox to NixOS running Incus, replacing a GUI-first hypervisor with declarative configuration. The post argues that Proxmox's clickable paradigm invites state drift, where UI debugging tweaks silently desync from infrastructure-as-code, and that NixOS's atomic, reproducible config eliminates that gap and makes the setup safer to hand to AI agents.

title: Parallel Parentheses Matching
blurb: The post explains how to match nested parentheses in parallel, replacing the usual sequential stack scan with a depth computation derived from a prefix sum over open and close characters. The author shows how treating each parenthesis's nesting depth as an associative scan lets the work split across cores, with matched pairs recovered by grouping positions that share a depth within the same span.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that installs from the Google Antigravity MCP Store and gives coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source code across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph exposed through two MCP tools, query_graph and get_graph_schema, and in internal tests cut agent hallucinations by up to 45x while using up to 4.5x fewer tokens.

title: Client-Side Load Balancing at a Million Requests Per Second
blurb: Zalando replaced the shared edge ingress router on its Product Read API with an in-process client-side load balancer handling a million requests per second of internal fan-out traffic. The post details the layers added on top, including N-ring fade-in, occupancy-based bounded load, and AZ-aware routing with a latency health factor, and explains how owning the routing decision cut cost and isolated whether latency spikes came from their code or the infrastructure.

title: To Run or Not to Run: Analyzing the Cost-Effectiveness of Code Execution in LLM-Based Program Repair
blurb: This paper analyzes code execution in LLM-based program repair across 7,745 SWE-bench agent traces and 3,000 repair attempts on Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode, finding agents average 8.8 test runs per task and apply execution indiscriminately. Prohibiting execution on commercial agents with SOTA models cut token and wall-clock cost while moving resolve rate just 1.25 points, suggesting execution should be a cost-benefit decision rather than a default.

title: The Spec Growth Engine: Spec-Anchored, Code-Coupled, Drift-Enforced Architecture for AI-Assisted Software Development
blurb: This paper introduces the Spec Growth Engine, a framework that fights two failure modes of AI coding agents: context explosion and silent spec-code drift. It uses a machine-readable spec graph, a context assembler that scopes the agent to an ownership path, a hardest-first vertical-slice growth protocol, and a drift gate that makes spec-code divergence a blocking merge condition.

title: The Problem is Prompt Debt
blurb: The author argues that hand-tuned natural language prompts accrue "prompt debt," a slow-building liability that caps what AI systems can become. As teams pile edge-case instructions and all-caps threats onto a single prompt, iteration grinds to a halt, the prompt becomes illegible to colleagues, and the application locks to one aging model because hot fixes break on any newer one.

title: Analyzing Claude Code usage with CloudWatch and OpenTelemetry
blurb: AWS's guide shows how to ship Claude Code telemetry to CloudWatch over the now-GA OTLP endpoint using bearer token auth, sending token consumption and per-developer cost metrics straight from developer laptops with no collector, sidecar, or IAM credential wiring. Once connected, the metrics are queryable in PromQL for team-level usage analytics and cost attribution, with the trade-off that bearer tokens are long-term credentials best avoided for in-AWS workloads where SigV4 fits.

title: Run Codex in the cloud – DigitalOcean for Codex is now available
blurb: DigitalOcean released a Codex plugin in Public Preview that provisions a persistent, pre-configured Droplet for OpenAI's Codex agent directly from natural-language prompts, with no manual SSH or dependency setup. The Droplet ships with the Codex CLI, codex-universal language tooling, and OAuth-based account linking, so long-running agent work keeps executing after you step away and can be steered from the ChatGPT mobile app.

title: GitHub shouldn't be a dependency for publishing Rust on crates.io
blurb: The author argues that crates.io's reliance on GitHub for authentication makes Microsoft a single point of failure for publishing Rust packages, leaving the ecosystem exposed to outages, account lockouts, and policy changes outside its control. The piece calls for independent authentication options so registry access doesn't hinge on one company's infrastructure or terms of service.

title: Same Scrutiny, More Time: Eye Tracking Insights into Reviewing LLM-Labelled Code
blurb: A Wizard-of-Oz eye-tracking study found that labeling code as LLM-generated did not change how thoroughly engineers reviewed it, but it did make them fixate longer and shift strategy toward checking logical correctness and consulting the original prompt. The authors flag a gap between reviewers' stated intentions and actual behavior, arguing companies should revisit AI policies and treat the prompt as a reviewable software artifact.

title: Mixing Visual and Textual Code
blurb: Hybrid ClojureScript embeds interactive visual syntax directly inside program text, letting developers express geometric and domain-specific ideas with a mix of textual and graphical constructs. The visual syntax is a composable language extension that preserves static reasoning, rendering as editable mini-GUIs in an enhanced IDE and falling back to a plain textual representation everywhere else.

title: ConcoLixir: Reactive LLM Discovery Oracles for Python Concolic Testing
blurb: ConcoLixir adds a reactive LLM "discovery oracle" to Python concolic testing, generating seeds, proposing inputs after solver failures, and targeting uncovered code when coverage stalls. Every candidate still runs concolically with only observed coverage and path constraints guiding exploration, lifting mean line coverage by 8.6 to 17.0 points near semantic barriers and library boundaries for $1.63 in API charges.

title: Stop Building Chatbots. Build Agents That Open PRs
blurb: The unit of useful agent work is a reviewable artifact like a PR, not an evaporating chat reply.

title: TabClean: Reusable LLM-Synthesized Programs for Tabular Data Cleaning
blurb: TabClean compiles LLM reasoning into reusable guarded cleaning programs, profiling a dirty table and a small annotated dev set to synthesize deterministic Python transformations that fire only when their dirty pattern, evidence support, and scope constraints all hold. The point is cost: instead of calling an LLM per row or per recurring batch, you pay once to compile a program and run it deterministically thereafter. Across six benchmarks it beats rule-based, learning-based, and LLM-based cleaners on F1 for five datasets while cutting recurring runtime and API spend.

title: Mistral OCR 4
blurb: Mistral released OCR 4, an OCR model that returns bounding boxes, typed-block classification, and inline confidence scores alongside extracted text, with support for 170 languages and single-container self-hosted deployment. The structured output feeds RAG and enterprise search pipelines with citation-ready inputs, and Mistral reports a 72% average win rate against leading OCR systems plus a top 85.20 score on OlmOCRBench.

title: PostgreSQL is enough (2024)
blurb: The author argues that Postgres alone can replace most of the specialized tooling teams reach for by default, cataloging extensions and patterns that cover queues (pgmq), cron (pg_cron), vector search (pgvector), full-text search, analytics columnstores (ParadeDB, Hydra), CDC (Debezium, PeerDB), and REST/GraphQL APIs straight from the database. The trade-off is operational concentration, but the payoff is one system to run, back up, and reason about instead of a sprawling stack glued together with reverse ETL.

title: How Good Can Linear Models Be for Time-Series Forecasting?
blurb: This paper argues that most of the accuracy gap between simple and large time-series models can be closed by tuning preprocessing rather than scaling architectures. Using Ridge regression as a transparent testbed, the authors search over context length, local normalization, regularization, and augmentation across eight benchmarks, and beat Transformer, MLP, and CNN baselines on six of them. The findings overturn a common convention: optimal lookback is series-specific and often shrinks as forecast horizon grows, and normalizing over a trailing fraction of the context beats normalizing the whole window.

title: BtrLog: Low-Latency Logging for Cloud Database Systems
blurb: BtrLog is a reusable cloud logging service that gives single-writer databases low-latency durable WAL appends without a proprietary, engine-coupled backend. It replicates log records across a quorum of SSD-backed log nodes in one network round trip, then asynchronously archives them to object storage as large segments to keep storage cost down. In evaluation it beat EBS on write latency and raised end-to-end transaction throughput when wired into a DBMS.

title: Privacy-Aware Infrastructure in the AI-Native Era: An Asset Classification Case Study
blurb: Meta's engineering team detailed how it classifies data assets for privacy enforcement, where an ambiguous field like "age" could mean a person's age or a cache TTL. The architecture uses LLMs narrowly to interpret novel or ambiguous assets, then distills their judgments into versioned, human-reviewed deterministic rules that handle routine production enforcement. The goal is to shrink the LLM's production role over time, keeping enforcement low-latency, replayable, and auditable while humans adjudicate reference labels and approve rule promotions.

title: Query Cost Model Calibration in Confidential Virtual Machines
blurb: This paper tackles why analytical queries run slow inside confidential VMs like AMD SEV-SNP: the query optimizer still uses cost assumptions borrowed from non-encrypted KVM guests, which no longer hold once memory encryption and RMP translation enter the picture. The authors add a lightweight CVM-aware calibration that models data movement and translation overhead using physical proxies the optimizer already has, recovering up to 48% of the lost performance and beating the KVM baseline on some workloads.

title: EcoTable: Cost-effective Table Integration in Data Lakes for Natural Language Queries
blurb: EcoTable proposes a natural language-based data integration framework that, given a set of plain-English queries, automatically joins CSV and Parquet tables in a data lake into a form that answers them, skipping the predefined target schema that traditional ETL requires. It cuts LLM invocation costs 5x by using a lightweight model to score join likelihood and framing path discovery as a Steiner tree search, reserving expensive LLM calls for transformation code. Across four benchmarks with 200-plus queries, it beat baselines by more than 30% on accuracy.

title: Krea 2 Technical Report
blurb: Krea released Krea 2, a series of image foundation models built for creative exploration rather than the single polished default that production diffusion tools tend to converge toward. The system pairs a custom diffusion transformer with a multi-stage pipeline of pretraining, SFT, preference optimization, and RL, plus components like grouped-query attention and a Qwen3-VL text encoder to widen its range across styles, moods, and compositions.

title: Does the 2026 World Cup font have a problem?
blurb: FWC26, the bespoke ultra-condensed typeface by Alistair McCready at Monolith, gives the 2026 World Cup a cohesive identity by cramming heavy geometric letters into tight spaces across jerseys, signage, and stadium displays. Typographers including Oliver Schöndorfer argue the same density breaks down on broadcast scoreboards and small stats labels, where the tight forms turn illegible and call for a dedicated low-resolution variant.

title: Ikea turns everyday objects into ingenious World Cup guessing game
blurb: Ikea Canada and Dentsu Creative launched Assemble the World, a social-first campaign that rebuilds 18 World Cup nations' flags from everyday Ikea products, like a VÅGSJÖN blanket as Canada's maple leaf and a PEPPRIG duster as Sweden's yellow stripes. Followers guess the products behind each flag, turning a brand tie-in into an interactive shopping prompt that also runs as OOH near stores through the summer.

title: Meta's first in-house AI glasses designs include a Kylie Jenner collaboration
blurb: Meta released the Meta Glasses line, its first AI smart glasses designed in-house rather than under the Ray-Ban label, spanning three frames including a Kylie Jenner collaboration tuned to her personal style and voice. Built with EssilorLuxottica and running the new Muse Spark model, the glasses drop the Ray-Ban Display's in-lens screen in favor of lighter, more ergonomic frames with dual 100-degree cameras and 20-language translation.

title: Dramatic atriums define Bangkok shopping centre by Linehouse
blurb: Linehouse designed the interiors of Bangkok's 40,000-square-metre Central Park shopping centre around three atriums clad in silver, copper, and bronze, metals read as auspicious in Thai culture. Each void carries a distinct character, from the calm Silver Void at street level to the community-hub Copper Void, whose cladding shifts from dusty pink to reddish copper under overhead light against stepped, illuminated escalators.

title: BMW Unveils Striking M Concept Neue Klasse
blurb: BMW revealed the M Concept Neue Klasse, its design study for an electric performance M-series, introducing yellow M Lights, three-dimensional Track Lights, and a trimaran-style front apron that doubles as structural support for the splitter. Industry reports peg it as a near-production design targeting 2027 or 2028, signaling how BMW M intends to carry its visual identity into an EV lineup built on the cleaner Neue Klasse language.

title: Why a stylist and her crew built a magazine instead of just moaning about AI
blurb: Fashion stylist Anna May and a small crew of photographers, makeup artists, and an art director responded to AI's spread through editorial imagery by self-funding MISC, a print magazine now on its second issue. The piece is a case for making something physical and human instead of just complaining, built on favors and unpaid time rather than a business plan.

title: Your design system runs on one person’s judgment-AI is about to prove it
blurb: The author argues that every mature design system develops a single human gatekeeper who reviews each contribution for tokens, naming, API sanity, and accessibility, making that person an unacknowledged single point of failure. As AI-generated contributions flood review queues, the post contends manual gatekeeping becomes untenable, and the only fix is dispersing ownership without lowering the quality bar.

title: What's actually in the world's first AI art museum?
blurb: Dataland, billed as the world's first AI art museum, opened beside The Broad and MOCA in Los Angeles with an inaugural exhibition, Machine Dreams: Rainforest, built by founders Refik Anadol and Efsun Erkılıç. The 25,000-square-foot space renders real-time environments from a Large Nature Model across 84 synchronized projectors and a 200-channel soundscape, with optional biosensor and scent wearables that tailor each visit.

title: Golf cart, small car and moon buggy meet in electric vehicle Amble One
blurb: Former Apple designer Julian Hoenig and Forpeople founder Michael Tropper launched Amble One, a doorless, street-legal electric buggy from Portugal-based startup Amble built on an EV-style skateboard battery platform rather than a golf-cart frame. With a 65km/h top speed and 100km range, it targets hotel estates first but pitches a broader short-range vehicle category for school runs, errands, and neighborhood trips.

title: Rethinking Figma in an AI world
blurb: A UX Collective essay argues that Config 2026 marks Figma's shift from defending the design canvas to expanding it into code layers, Figma Motion, shader tools, and agent-driven workflows, as AI pulls product work toward code-native environments. The author's sharper point is that this pressures Figma's seat model, since engineers generating or inspecting UI inside coding tools weaken the case for the passive review seats Figma has long sold.

title: Call It a Day lighting by Tongqi Lu Design
blurb: Shanghai studio Tongqi Lu Design released Call It a Day, a lighting collection of table, floor, and pendant lamps wrapped in translucent silicone shades that users switch on and off through gestures like a bop or a tug rather than a switch. The collection swaps conventional controls for tactile interaction, with the silicone surface stretching and relaxing under the hand to reframe a lamp as a soft, low-effort end-of-day ritual.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant disclosed active zero-day exploitation of CVE-2026-20245, a privilege-escalation flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager that lets an attacker with a compromised admin account reach root via a malicious CSV upload that bypasses file-upload filtering. The actor, observed at a service provider in early 2026, established rogue SSH peering, altered default account passwords, and ran anti-forensic cleanup scripts to purge indicators, so defenders should patch, rotate appliance credentials, and audit peering configurations.

title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added four actively exploited flaws to its KEV catalog, including three max-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS bugs (CVE-2026-34908, -34909, -34910) that Bishop Fox chained for unauthenticated RCE with elevated privileges, plus a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection (CVE-2025-67038). BOD 26-04 gives federal agencies three days to patch; apply Ubiquiti's May updates and Lantronix firmware 2.2.0.0R1, and run Bishop Fox's detection script to find vulnerable instances.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration in which untrusted pull requests trigger privileged GitHub Actions workflows, enabling command injection, credential theft, and supply-chain compromise exploitable by any unauthenticated user with a free account. A scan of roughly 30,000 high-impact repositories found 300+ fully exploitable, including projects at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare; audit workflows that run on `pull_request_target` and strip write permissions and secrets from untrusted PR triggers.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is prepending JavaScript block comments containing fake nuclear and biological weapons policy text to spyware payloads, aiming to derail LLM-based triage that feeds file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data. The trick can trigger refusal, context pollution, or premature classification in naive LLM-first scanners, but it does not evade YARA rules, entropy checks, AST parsing, or behavioral detection.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Mandiant found an unknown threat actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN flaw CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS 7.8) as a zero-day for at least two months before disclosure, uploading a malicious CSV to gain root on a communications provider's device. The attack chained earlier authentication-bypass zero-days (CVE-2026-20127, CVE-2026-20182) and used anti-forensic config restoration to evade detection, so apply Cisco's patches and audit SD-WAN peering connections and admin credentials.

title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
blurb: Operation Endgame disrupted the Amadey botnet and StealC infostealer, with Microsoft, Europol, and partners taking down 326 servers and 142 domains tied to the malware-as-a-service operations, alongside the SocGholish loader. The action recovered roughly 27 million credentials stolen from over 385,000 systems; defenders should treat any credentials handled on affected hosts as compromised and force rotation.

title: Anthropic Claims Alibaba Ran ‘Brazen’ Campaign to Access Its Claude AI Model
blurb: Anthropic alleged in a letter obtained by the Wall Street Journal that Alibaba and its AI unit ran "the largest known distillation attack" against Claude to date. The accusation escalates IP-theft disputes around model distillation, where a rival systematically queries a frontier model to replicate its outputs and train a competing system.

title: The hits keep on coming for Cisco vulnerabilities
blurb: The Register catalogs a continuing run of disclosures across Cisco's product line, from edge appliances to management software, with several flaws drawing active exploitation interest. Teams running Cisco gear on the perimeter should track the vendor's advisory feed closely, prioritize patches for internet-facing devices, and treat any unpatched exposed appliance as a likely target rather than a deferred maintenance item.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager (CVSS 8.6) that chains to unauthenticated root RCE, with attacks starting less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published PoC and a full exploit chain. Cisco patched the bug on June 3; the WebDialer service is off by default, so prioritize patching and disabling WebDialer on any internet-exposed CUCM or Unified CM SME deployments.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Four vulnerabilities dubbed DifyTap in the open-source LLMOps platform Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, enable cross-tenant data theft in multi-tenant deployments, including the critical CVE-2026-41948 (CVSS 9.4) in the plugin daemon and CVE-2026-41947 (CVSS 9.1) in tracing that creates a persistent message-exfiltration channel. Dify 1.14.2 patches all four flaws; operators running multi-tenant instances should update immediately and apply WAF rules to block exploitation.

title: Mystery hackers use novel SharkLoader dropper against governments, software devs
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a global campaign using the novel SharkLoader dropper to compromise government organizations and software developers across multiple countries. The attackers breach internet-facing apps via public PoC exploits for known flaws in SharePoint, Exchange, FortiOS, Cisco IOS XE, and BIG-IP, then deploy a Cobalt Strike beacon to dump Windows and Active Directory credentials while disabling security logging.

title: In a first, a court takedown goes after two cybercrime tools at once
blurb: Microsoft and law enforcement executed a single court-authorized takedown against both Amadey, a malware-delivery botnet, and StealC, a Russia-linked infostealer sold as malware-as-a-service, disrupting over 200 command-and-control servers under the RICO Act. The two tools were tied to more than 140,000 infected machines in the first week of May, so defenders should hunt for Amadey and StealC indicators and rotate any credentials exposed to infostealer activity.

title: Unlocking the Cloudflare app ecosystem with OAuth for all
blurb: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all customers, letting any developer register and manage their own OAuth clients for scoped, delegated access to the Cloudflare API instead of relying on long-lived API tokens. The release pairs the broader access with a hardened consent flow, dashboard-based revocation, and visible app ownership to mitigate OAuth phishing as agentic tools drive demand for delegated access.

title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
blurb: Researchers linked a new in-memory backdoor named Mistic (aka MLTBackdoor) to initial-access broker KongTuke, deployed since April 2026 alongside the Python-based ModeloRAT against insurance, education, IT, and professional-services orgs via ClickFix lures. Per Symantec and Carbon Black, Mistic side-loads through trusted Microsoft tooling (MpExtMs.exe), runs payloads in memory with no disk artifacts, loads Beacon Object Files, and self-deletes via a kill switch for long-term, low-visibility access.

title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
blurb: Citizen Lab published evidence on June 25 that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone 12 in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it stopped selling to Russia. MobileLockdown USB-pairing records matching a known Cellebrite host fingerprint, plus an official MVD forensic report naming UFED Physical Analyzer and UFED 4PC, show investigators pulling WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber data to search for named opposition figures.

title: Protocol Prying: Systematic Vulnerability Research in the Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share Proximity Transfer Protocols
blurb: Researchers published the first cross-platform reverse-engineering and protocol-aware fuzzing study of Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share, finding six pre-auth vulnerabilities (V1-V6) across the proximity transfer stacks used by over five billion devices. The flaws include three zero-click AirDrop DoS issues in macOS/iOS, a Samsung Quick Share D2D encryption bypass, and a heap use-after-free in Google Quick Share for Windows; Apple, Samsung, and Google acknowledged the reports.

title: Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group attributed STOCKSTAY, a previously undocumented multi-component .NET backdoor, to Russian state actor Turla, which has deployed it against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to Italian foreign policy. The implant shares significant code with Turla's long-running Kazuar implant, communicates over secure WebSocket C2, and masquerades as benign tools like PDF viewers and calculators to enable file theft, screen capture, and command execution.

title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
blurb: Russia-linked APT Turla (UAC-0194, Venomous Bear) has been deploying StockStay, a .NET espionage backdoor disguised as PDF viewers and calculator utilities, against Ukrainian government and military targets since 2022, according to Google's Threat Intelligence Group. The implant uses a secure WebSocket C&C channel and shares code with Turla's Kazuar implant, supporting screen capture, file exfiltration, registry modification, and arbitrary command execution.

title: Anthropic says Alibaba must be punished for largest Claude cloning attack
blurb: Anthropic told senators that operators affiliated with Alibaba and its Qwen lab ran the largest distillation campaign it has measured, generating 28.8 million exchanges through roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5 to extract Claude's agentic reasoning, software engineering, and long-horizon capabilities. The operators evaded detection using obfuscation techniques and proxy networks, part of what Anthropic calls a growing circumvention economy fueling repeated model-distillation attacks against US frontier labs.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
