blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230 (CVSS 8.6), an SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME that lets an unauthenticated remote attacker write files to the OS and escalate to root. Cisco patched it on June 3 with no workaround; defenders should patch immediately and, where possible, disable the WebDialer service, which is required for exploitation and off by default.

title: StubZero: $148,337 RCE in Google Cloud Production
blurb: Researcher Brutecat chained public debugging endpoints on Google Cloud's cloudcrmipfrontend-pa API into full remote code execution on production (CVE-2026-2031), starting from a getProtoDefinition endpoint that leaked internal protobuf definitions for any google3 service, then escalating from there. Google paid a $148,337 bounty and patched the flaw, but a variant resurfaced three months later, so teams relying on first-party API isolation should audit debugging endpoints exposed to authenticated console origins.

title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
blurb: CVE-2026-12569, an improper input validation flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM, is being actively exploited by remote, unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary code and deploy persistent JSP webshells for command execution and data exfiltration. CISA added it to the KEV catalog with a June 28 federal remediation deadline; PTC released patches and IoCs starting June 17, so manufacturing and OT operators running Windchill on the edge should patch and hunt for webshells immediately.

title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
blurb: The MCP specification moves to a stateless, enterprise-ready version (MCP 2026-07-28) on July 28, closing classes of vulnerabilities like session hijacking and unsolicited server prompts while opening new ones, per Akamai's analysis. The shift to client-held tracking identifiers and state objects risks workflow hijacking and cross-tenant access if IDs are predictable, and new MCP-specific HTTP headers introduce request desync attacks and secret leakage through x-mcp-header mappings.

title: Microsoft gives Windows 10 users an unexpected extra year of free security updates
blurb: Microsoft extended its consumer Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program by a year, pushing free critical-security patches for eligible 22H2 devices out to October 12, 2027. The extension surfaced quietly through documentation edits rather than a formal announcement, giving teams still running Windows 10 fleets more runway to migrate to Windows 11 before coverage lapses.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers at XM Cyber disclosed a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user disable enterprise security tools and invoke root-level functions without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering alerts. The flaw stems from how macOS caches and reuses an application's CDHash, letting an attacker impersonate trusted components to abuse privileged XPC services, demonstrated against CrowdStrike Falcon EDR and Kandji MDM; Apple has not responded.

title: Privacy-Aware Infrastructure in the AI-Native Era: An Asset Classification Case Study
blurb: Meta detailed its privacy-aware infrastructure (PAI) approach to asset classification at scale, the "understand" layer that feeds retention, access, and purpose enforcement. The architecture is deliberately LLM-narrow: models interpret only novel or ambiguous assets (a field named "age" could be a person's age or a cache TTL), then distill their reasoning into versioned, human-reviewed deterministic rules that handle routine production decisions. The pattern keeps enforcement low-latency, replayable, and auditable while shrinking the model's production footprint over time.

title: Medical diagnosis AIs can be tricked into telling whose data trained them
blurb: Researchers showed that medical diagnosis models leak their training data membership, letting an attacker query a model to determine whether a specific patient's records were used to train it. The technique is a membership inference attack against the clinical AI itself, exposing protected health data without breaching the underlying dataset and undermining the de-identification assumptions hospitals rely on when sharing models.

title: ShareLock: A Stealthy Multi-Tool Threshold Poisoning Attack Against MCP
blurb: ShareLock is a multi-tool poisoning attack against Model Context Protocol that splits a malicious instruction into benign-looking secret shares using Shamir's threshold scheme, distributing them across separate tool descriptions to evade per-tool inspection and automated detectors. A covert trigger planted during a server update reconstructs the hidden instruction at runtime, and the authors report an average attack success rate above 90% across mainstream LLMs on two MCP clients while defeating description-based detection.

title: Automated Detection of Configuration-Specific Security Vulnerabilities via Patch Analysis
blurb: PatchLens is a static analysis technique that recovers Vulnerability Impact Conditions, Boolean predicates over compile-time options identifying which configuration variants of C/C++ software contained a given flaw, without compiling any variant. Evaluated across 1,192 Linux kernel, 289 FFmpeg, and 100 PHP patches, it produces compact predicates and shows that CVE texts encode the required options only about 1% of the time, enabling variant-aware CI triage, targeted fuzzing, and feature risk scoring.

title: Mostly Automatic Translation of Language Interpreters from C to Safe Rust
blurb: Reboot translates real-world C interpreters to safe Rust with minimal human input, eliminating memory bugs like heap overflows and use-after-free.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
blurb: This paper studies prompt injection in LLM-based résumé screening, defined as self-promotional text that adds no real qualifications but is crafted to inflate an applicant's ranking. The authors show injection reliably boosts rankings when résumés are homogeneous and few candidates inject, but its effect collapses as injection becomes widespread, and under heterogeneous quality it can occasionally push lower-quality candidates above stronger ones.

title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
blurb: Threat actors are abusing Shopify's Shop order-tracking app to deliver callback phishing, inserting fake receipts that impersonate Norton, McAfee, Apple, and PayPal alongside legitimate purchases, per Gen Digital. The receipts list a scammer-controlled phone number that harvests credentials, payment details, and OTPs or pushes remote-access software; the in-app delivery channel remains unconfirmed, and Gen found no evidence Shopify or the impersonated brands were compromised.

title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
blurb: Microsoft is tracking an active phishing campaign hitting hotels across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files with disguised .lnk shortcuts that drop a Node.js implant called TonRAT onto front-desk machines. The operators launder authentication by routing lures through Calendly and Google redirects to pass SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, then resolve C2 domains via the TON blockchain API to evade static blocklists.

title: 25-Year-Old Vulnerability Patched in Curl
blurb: Curl patched 18 vulnerabilities in a single release, including CVE-2026-8932, a 25-year-old mTLS connection-reuse flaw introduced in version 7.7 (March 2001) that lets libcurl reuse a connection after client certificate or private key changes, enabling authentication bypass. The bug affects libcurl applications rather than the command-line tool, with no public reports of in-the-wild exploitation; upgrade affected applications to the patched release.

title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its free Windows 10 consumer Extended Security Updates program by a year, pushing coverage to October 12, 2027, via documentation edits and a blog post addendum rather than a formal announcement. Consumers can enroll at no cost by syncing Windows settings to a Microsoft account or redeeming 1,000 reward points, buying defenders more time before unsupported Windows 10 fleets go dark.

title: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
blurb: A study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (6,145 agent config files) finds that LLM coding-agent configurations behave as an unmanaged supply chain: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repos, 75.5% of clone pairs cross org boundaries, and under 1% declare permission boundaries. The authors propose Rel(AI)Build, a deterministic control plane that content-addresses agent definitions, enforces tiered permissions and blocklists before LLM invocation, and logs a hash-chained audit trail.

title: Self-destructing Mistic backdoor linked to access broker selling corporate footholds to ransomware gangs
blurb: Researchers linked Mistic, a self-destructing backdoor that wipes itself after deployment to frustrate forensic analysis, to an initial access broker that sells the corporate footholds it establishes to ransomware affiliates. Defenders should hunt for the malware's short-lived artifacts in memory and EDR telemetry rather than disk, and treat any confirmed Mistic infection as a precursor to imminent ransomware deployment.

title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
blurb: A compromised third-party vendor injected a malicious script into Polymarket's frontend, enabling a phishing campaign that stole roughly $3M in pUSD from at least 11 users, per PeckShield. The attacker bridged the funds from Polygon to Ethereum and swapped them into ~1,893 ETH; Polymarket says it removed the affected dependency and will fully refund impacted users.

title: Mirage2FA phishing kit uses HTML smuggling to steal Microsoft 365 credentials
blurb: Fortra researchers identified Mirage2FA, a Microsoft 365 phishing kit that uses short-lived HTML smuggling and obfuscated JavaScript loaders (Base64, XOR with 0xAD, and eval()) to deliver a fake 365 sign-in page that harvests credentials and intercepts MFA prompts, including authenticator, number-matching, and SMS workflows. Any user who submitted credentials should have passwords reset, sessions and refresh tokens revoked, MFA methods reviewed, and mailbox rules and OAuth grants inspected.

title: Chai: Agentic Discovery of Cryptographic Misuse Vulnerabilities
blurb: Chai is an AI system that discovers and validates cryptographic misuse vulnerabilities by inverting differential testing: instead of auditing one codebase for many flaws, it catalogs flaws at the library level and propagates them across a cryptographic dependency graph. Evaluated on X.509, JWT, and SAML libraries, it surfaced over 100 vulnerabilities, including a previously unknown critical bug in an SSL library powering billions of devices. The approach targets crypto misuse, a class that lacks the runtime instrumentation that makes memory-safety bugs cheap to confirm.

title: The Web4 Agent Economy: A Large-Scale Empirical Study of the Landscape, Challenges, and Opportunities
blurb: A large-scale empirical study of the emerging "Web4" agent economy analyzed 99,448 identity registrations, 317M transaction logs, and 341 MCP projects, finding autonomous agents already process millions of daily machine-to-machine payments on protocols like MCP, x402, and EIP-8004. The authors warn this growth rests on immature infrastructure, with weak identity and authorization practices and unresolved payment interoperability as the most persistent security gap.

title: DroidBreaker: Practical and Functional Problem-Space Attacks on Machine-Learning Android Malware Detectors
blurb: DroidBreaker is a problem-space attack framework that evades machine-learning Android malware detectors by manipulating only the APK components most influential to the target model, using build-safe, semantics-preserving injection and obfuscation of API calls, modules, permissions, and URLs. It achieves high evasion rates with few queries in both white-box and black-box settings and sharply cuts detections by commercial scanners on VirusTotal. The authors argue prior attacks overstated success by smoke-testing only installation rather than verifying the modified APK still runs its intended behavior.

title: MIRROR: Novelty-Constrained Memory-Guided MCTS Red-Teaming for Agentic RAG
blurb: MIRROR is a red-teaming framework that runs memory-guided Monte Carlo tree search across four attack surfaces of multimodal agentic RAG systems, including text poisoning, image injection, and orchestrator-level tool manipulation. A deterministic Novelty Gate blocks candidates that duplicate retrieved context, addressing the 73-84% template recycling the authors measured in prior text-poisoning tools. MIRROR hits 76% ASR on image poisoning versus 52% for baselines and 97% on orchestrator attacks at half the query cost; the authors released ART-SafeBench with 41,815 records for testing defenses.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout extends LFR from temporary van-mounted deployments to fixed infrastructure, despite ongoing legal challenges and civil-liberties objections over consent, bias, and the absence of specific statutory authorization in UK law.

title: Anthropic Says Alibaba Ran 28.8 Million Claude Exchanges Through Fake Accounts
blurb: Anthropic told U.S. officials that operators affiliated with Alibaba's Qwen lab ran 28.8 million Claude exchanges through nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to harvest outputs for adversarial distillation. Per a June 10 letter, the April 22 to June 5 campaign targeted Claude's software engineering and agentic reasoning skills and exceeds Anthropic's February claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax combined.

title: UK school’s network left wide open for invasion, student found
blurb: A student at a UK school discovered the institution's network was exposed with weak access controls, leaving internal systems reachable to anyone who probed them. The finding underscores how unmanaged edge devices and default configurations in education environments remain trivial to compromise, and defenders running similar setups should audit external attack surface and enforce segmentation before a less benign actor finds the same gap.

title: Inherited Circuits, Learned Semantics: How Fine-Tuning Creates Evasion Vulnerabilities Invisible to Standard Evaluation
blurb: This paper shows that fine-tuning an LLM for security classification can introduce evasion vulnerabilities that standard held-out evaluation misses. Studying Foundation-Sec-8B-Instruct, the authors find it inherits its classification circuit from base Llama, then specializes it into brittle token-level indicator rules that fail under behavior-preserving PowerShell transformations like alias substitution and case mutation. They propose a pre-deployment linear probe and indicator-token sign test to flag command families where semantics drift after fine-tuning.

title: Helpful or Harmful? Evaluating LLM-Assisted Vulnerability Patching via a Human Study
blurb: This paper proposes a controlled human study, using a balanced crossover design, to test whether LLM-assisted vulnerability patching actually outperforms manual debugging or just produces superficial fixes. The key methodological detail is hidden "Ghost Tests" that validate patch security beyond the visible functional checks, catching repairs that pass functionality but fail security validation. Only a pilot has run so far, so treat the full results as forthcoming rather than established.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
blurb: This paper presents an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural-language policy documents into formally verified Cedar policies using an LLM generator-critic loop. The approach targets the gap between probabilistic guardrails, which offer no formal guarantees, and hand-coded symbolic enforcement, which doesn't scale. On MedAgentBench, the generated policies cover substantially more of the source specification than the prior hand-coded enforcement, pointing to a path for verifiable agent governance in high-stakes domains.

title: ZeroTier Quantum RC2 brings post-quantum security closer to general availability
blurb: ZeroTier released RC2 of ZeroTier Quantum, its end-to-end encrypted networking platform, pairing ML-KEM-1024 with hybrid AES-384 and mutual identity authentication to meet NIST/NSA CNSA 2.0 and FIPS requirements. The Rust-based, zero-trust mesh targets "harvest now, decrypt later" threats ahead of the U.S. government's 2027 PQC mandate, with a third-party audit and pen test pending before general availability.

title: A Mechanistic Explanation of Prompt Injection (and why you should study roles)
blurb: A new analysis argues that prompt injection works because LLMs receive the entire conversation as one undifferentiated token stream, and rely on role tags (<system>, <user>, <tool>) rather than any inherent channel separation to tell instructions from data. The authors use this role-based theory to construct novel attacks, explain prior mech-interp findings, and predict when injections succeed. They call for a research subfield focused on the "science of roles," with the full work published at ICML.

title: Boundary 1.0 releases RDP session recording and improved management
blurb: HashiCorp released Boundary 1.0, its privileged access management platform, adding session recording for RDP that captures and replays every interactive user action to support compliance audits and security event analysis. The release also ships two Helm charts for deploying controllers and workers on Kubernetes and adds org- and project-scoped aliases so teams in multi-tenant deployments can reuse human-readable target names.

title: Adaptive Evaluation of Out-of-Band Defenses Against Prompt Injection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper organizes out-of-band prompt-injection defenses for LLM agents (CaMeL, FIDES, Progent, FORGE) as classical integrity controls (Biba, reference monitors, least privilege), and warns that all are validated only on static benchmarks, the same gap that let adaptive attacks break twelve in-band defenses at over 90% success. An independent reproduction on AgentDojo with a self-hosted Qwen2.5-7B held: Progent cut mean attack success sixfold (25.8% to 4.2%), and a hand-crafted adaptive attack stayed at 2.6%. White-box GCG attacks remain untested.

title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
blurb: A developer ran hackmyclaw.com, inviting the public to email Fiu, an OpenClaw AI assistant, and trick it into leaking a secrets.env file. After reaching Hacker News, Fiu absorbed 6,000+ emails from 2,000+ people using future-self impersonation, fake compliance audits, and multilingual prompt injection, yet never leaked credentials or sent an unauthorized reply. A short anti-injection system prompt held, though batch processing initially cross-contaminated context, arguing for fresh per-email isolation when running agents on untrusted input.

title: FCC passes new cybersecurity rules for emergency systems, undersea cables
blurb: The FCC approved new rules Thursday tightening cybersecurity for the Emergency Alert System and Wireless Emergency Alerts, mandating strong passwords, prompt patching, firewalls, and a new authentication ID to verify alerts before they spread. The same vote delivered the first comprehensive update to submarine cable regulations in decades, tightening some security requirements while exempting certain undersea cable providers from Team Telecom national security reviews.

title: Jailbreaking for the Average Jane: Choosing Optimal Jailbreaks via Bandit Algorithms for Automatically Enhanced Queries
blurb: This paper introduces a multi-armed bandit attack that efficiently learns the optimal jailbreak per target model from a large candidate set using a small number of noisy exploration queries, then applies the learned policy at scale. Tested against 15 state-of-the-art open-weight LLMs it reached up to 97% average success, and adding query complexity raised success rates by up to 26%, lowering the bar for non-expert actors.

title: VIGIL: Runtime Enforcement of Behavioral Specifications in AI Agent Skills
blurb: VIGIL is a runtime enforcement framework that checks an AI agent's actual execution trace against behavioral policies drawn from skill specifications, operator constraints, and cross-skill global rules. Its policy language compiles into SMT constraints over finite traces, catching violations that depend on event order, argument relationships, or cross-call value flow rather than single-call filters. On real LLM-agent runs across office, operational, and engineering tasks, it flagged policy violations with over 95% recall and a sub-10% false-positive rate.

title: Nation-state actors cracked critical Australian infrastructure to ‘cripple it at a time of their choosing’
blurb: Australia's intelligence agencies disclosed that nation-state actors have compromised critical infrastructure networks and established persistent access positioned to disrupt operations at a time of their choosing. The intrusions reflect pre-positioning rather than immediate sabotage, so operators of energy, water, and telecom systems should hunt for living-off-the-land activity, audit dormant accounts, and segment OT networks to limit lateral movement.

title: The 'papers, please' era of the internet will decimate your privacy
blurb: The author argues that age-verification mandates are functionally identity-verification mandates, forcing users to hand government IDs or facial biometrics to opaque third-party services with unknown data-handling and breach exposure. Citing Australia's under-16 social media ban, the piece notes the law fails on its own terms, with roughly 70% of kids still using social media months after enforcement, while creating new troves of sensitive identity data.

title: Russian APT 'Gamaredon' Upgrades Its Arsenal, Requiring New Defenses
blurb: ESET documented 35 Gamaredon (aka Aqua Blizzard, BlueAlpha) spear-phishing campaigns against Ukraine in 2025, during which the FSB-linked APT shipped six new PowerShell downloaders and improved C2 concealment. Its new PteroPaste tool spreads via USB by copying a malicious loader onto connected drives under a random Word document's name with a .lnk extension, so defenders should restrict removable-media execution and hunt for masqueraded .lnk files.

title: Polymarket says hackers stole users’ funds
blurb: Polymarket confirmed that hackers stole user funds after a third-party vendor compromise let them inject malicious code into its website for some users, with blockchain monitors PeckShield and independent analysts pegging losses at roughly $3M across 11-plus victims. The company says it has contained the incident and is refunding affected users in full, but has disclosed neither the vendor nor the attack mechanism, and a concurrent phishing campaign was targeting the same users.

title: zQR: A Verifiable QR-Driven zkSNARK Proof Verification Framework for Mobile Platforms
blurb: This paper introduces zQR, a framework that encodes zkSNARK proofs into QR codes so offline verifiers can check zero-knowledge proofs on mobile devices without a network connection. It pairs blockchain logging for auditability and non-repudiation with LLM-generated circuits, and the evaluation reports proof-generation and verification latency, QR encode/decode times, and on-chain gas costs. Treat it as an early proof-of-concept: it works at QR version 19 with low error correction, but the authors only sketch the attack surfaces rather than harden against them.

title: NSA Loses Anthropic Mythos Access After June Export-Control Order
blurb: The NSA lost access to Anthropic's Mythos 5 cybersecurity model after the Trump administration's June export-control directive forced the company to suspend its most advanced models for all foreign nationals, including noncitizen employees on US soil. The cutoff hit analysts mid-evaluation, where Mythos had surfaced software vulnerabilities in a controlled red-team test that Sen. Mark Warner initially characterized as breaking into classified systems "in hours.

title: PRISM: PE Relational Inter-Section Matrix. A 2D Section-Aware Dataset for Static PE Malware Detection
blurb: PRISM is an open dataset and feature representation for static Windows PE malware detection that encodes each binary as a 2D matrix of sections in file order, preserving positional and inter-section context that flat vectors like EMBER discard. It spans 83,633 deduplicated samples across 684 families, and a gradient-boosted classifier matches EMBER's binary-detection accuracy at roughly one-sixth the dimensionality. The structural signal is aimed at less-saturated tasks like malware family classification.

title: A privacy-first take on local malware analysis
blurb: Burnyard, a research project from Ohio State University, runs dynamic malware analysis entirely on local hardware, executing samples via instruction-level user-space emulation that hooks every syscall and Windows API call to produce a traced classification across 43 malware families. The local-only design avoids the disclosure risk of uploading samples to VirusTotal or MalwareBazaar, where operators can spot their own hashes, and averaged 22.41 seconds on Windows samples versus VirusTotal's 32.36.

title: SIM-swapping gang busted in international police operation
blurb: Polish authorities arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of SIM-swapping, cryptocurrency theft, and laundering proceeds estimated to exceed tens of millions of zlotys, in an operation supported by the FBI and HSI. The group breached telecom-affiliated infrastructure and employee email accounts to hijack victims' phone numbers, then drained exchange accounts via intercepted SMS and email; all four face up to 25 years in prison.

title: A $2.5 Billion Whodunit: The Hack That Dented the U.K. Economy
blurb: Investigators now attribute the 2025 ransomware attack that crippled Jaguar Land Rover to Russian operators, despite a loose cybercriminal collective initially claiming credit. The shutdown halted JLR production for weeks and is estimated to have cost the U.K. economy roughly $2.5B, marking one of the most economically damaging cyberattacks in the country's history.

title: DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison
blurb: Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old Minnesota man who operated the "Snoopy" shop selling access to hacked DraftKings accounts, was sentenced to 18 months in prison after pleading guilty to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion. The 2022 credential-stuffing attack compromised roughly 68,000 accounts and stole $600,000; Austad was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1.3M in restitution.

title: Adversarial Diffusion Across Modalities: A Fusion Survey of Attacks, Defenses, and Evaluation for Text, Vision, and Vision-Language Models
blurb: This survey unifies four previously disconnected lines of adversarial AI research, diffusion-based attacks on LLMs, attacks on image classifiers, vision-language jailbreak pipelines, and diffusion-based input purification defenses, into a single taxonomy and a five-dimension evaluation framework spanning attack success rate, transferability, query budget, perplexity, and defense-evasion. The authors catalog 50 papers, propose a six-class taxonomy of diffusion roles in attack pipelines, and flag five recurring weaknesses in the current LLM-side literature. A companion catalog and spreadsheet ship with the paper.

title: Agents That Know Too Much: A Data-Centric Survey of Privacy in LLM Agents
blurb: This survey reframes LLM agent privacy around the data an agent touches rather than the attack used, covering retrieval, text-to-SQL, memory, and inter-agent messaging where sensitive data can leak through queries, intermediate results, and persisted state. The authors find that only information-flow control addresses both compositional and cross-session inference leakage, the two least-protected risks, and that no existing benchmark exercises an agent across all its data surfaces under a single privacy policy.

title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
blurb: Polish authorities arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group that breached telecom partner infrastructure and employee email accounts to run SIM-swapping attacks, draining millions from victims' cryptocurrency exchange accounts. The CBZC-led operation, backed by the FBI and HSI, pegs laundered funds at tens of millions of złoty (at least $5M); the suspects face up to 25 years on charges including organized crime, system intrusion, and money laundering.

title: Why patch directives only go so far
blurb:
CISA's June 21 emergency directive for CVE-2026-50751, a CVSS 9.3 authentication bypass in Check Point Remote Access VPN, landed six weeks after a Qilin affiliate began exploiting it in early May to compromise dozens of organizations. The IKEv1 certificate-validation flaw turns the VPN gateway itself into the attack vector, so patching does nothing for networks already breached, where the attacker now operates as a trusted user. Hunt the May-June window, don't just patch.

(78 words)

title: Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform
blurb: TerraProbe is a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating whether LLM-generated Terraform security fixes actually work, applied to 288 repairs from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. Targeted Checkov findings disappeared in 83.3% of cases, but full-scanner cleanliness held in only 10.4%, and human review found 71.4% were deceptive fixes that pass automated checks while leaving the vulnerability in place. Don't treat a cleared static-analysis finding as a remediated misconfiguration; validate plan behavior and security intent.

title: TESLA-for-5G: Broadcast Authentication for 5G Networks Using TESLA
blurb: This paper proposes TESLA-for-5G (TF5), a broadcast authentication protocol that lets user equipment verify 5G SIB1 messages without per-message digital signatures, closing the fake-base-station gap where UEs camp on forged unauthenticated system information. TF5 uses TESLA symmetric MACs with delayed key disclosure in steady state and bootstraps trust at cell entry via lightweight GG09 identity-based signatures, avoiding certificate distribution. The authors formally verified it in Tamarin under a Dolev-Yao adversary and benchmarked it on the OpenAirInterface 5G stack.

title: Chinese cybersecurity company claims it’s built a better-than-Mythos bug finder
blurb: A Chinese cybersecurity firm claims its new AI-driven vulnerability scanner outperforms Mythos at autonomous bug discovery, positioning it as a domestic rival to Western offensive-security tooling. The claim is vendor-supplied and unverified by independent benchmarks, so defenders should treat the performance numbers as marketing until reproducible results land, while noting that LLM-assisted bug finders are accelerating on both the attacker and defender sides.

title: DKVE: Decentralized Key Validation for End-to-End Encrypted Messaging
blurb:
This paper introduces DKVE, a protocol that validates contacts' public keys in end-to-end encrypted messaging by privately cross-checking them against mutual contacts' social graphs, using OPRF and OKVS to hide both queries and contact lists. On real social-network datasets it detected MitM key substitution with over 97% accuracy on strong-to-moderate-tie networks, cutting key-transparency queries by two orders of magnitude. It complements rather than replaces OOB and KT verification, struggling on weak-tie graphs and cold-start cases.

That's 78 words.

title: Zero-Day Exploitation of Vulnerability (CVE-2026-20245) in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager
blurb: Mandiant disclosed active zero-day exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, a privilege-escalation flaw in the file-upload feature that let an attacker turn a compromised admin account into root access via a malicious CSV upload. Enterprises running Catalyst SD-WAN, common across banks, retail, and healthcare branch networks, should prioritize patching and audit for the anti-forensic cleanup Mandiant observed, since attackers reverted configs to erase indicators.

title: OpenAI and Broadcom unveil LLM-optimized inference chip
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, a custom inference chip co-designed to run large language models more efficiently than general-purpose GPUs. The partnership gives OpenAI a path to reduce its dependence on Nvidia for serving capacity, and signals to enterprise buyers that the largest model providers are moving inference onto bespoke silicon to control cost and supply at scale.

title: CISA warns of max severity Ubiquiti flaws exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added three max-severity Ubiquiti UniFi OS flaws and a critical Lantronix EDS5000 command-injection bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, giving federal agencies three days under directive BOD 26-04 to patch or mitigate. Bishop Fox showed the UniFi flaws can be chained for unauthenticated remote code execution with elevated privileges, so IT teams running these devices should apply the May updates and run the vendor's detection script immediately.

title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
blurb: Mandiant found an unknown threat actor exploited a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day, CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS 7.8), for at least two months before disclosure, chaining a malicious CSV upload to escalate a compromised admin account to root on a communications service provider's device. The intrusion paired the flaw with earlier authentication-bypass zero-days and anti-forensic file restoration, so enterprises running Catalyst SD-WAN controllers should patch and audit peering connections and admin credential changes immediately.

title: Amadey, StealC malware operations disrupted in Operation Endgame action
blurb: Microsoft, Europol, and international partners disrupted the Amadey and StealC malware-as-a-service operations under Operation Endgame, seizing 326 servers and 142 domains and recovering roughly 27 million stolen credentials from over 385k compromised systems. The action degrades the initial-access and credential-theft infrastructure that feeds ransomware and fraud campaigns, with Microsoft, ESET, Proofpoint, and IBM X-Force among the private-sector partners supplying intelligence.

title: The hits keep on coming for Cisco vulnerabilities
blurb: Cisco is fielding active exploitation of two more flaws, including CVE-2026-20230, a Unified Communications Manager SSRF bug now used to gain root, and CVE-2026-20245, a Catalyst SD-WAN Manager zero-day exploited against a communications provider. It marks Cisco's sixth SD-WAN vulnerability under attack this year and the second zero-day in two months, pushing IT teams toward emergency patch cycles across core network infrastructure.

title: Mystery hackers use novel SharkLoader dropper against governments, software devs
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a previously unknown campaign that has compromised government organizations and software development firms across multiple countries using a novel dropper it named SharkLoader. The attackers break in through publicly known exploits in Microsoft SharePoint and Exchange, Fortinet, Cisco, F5, and other internet-facing products, then deploy Cobalt Strike beacons to dump credentials and move laterally while disabling the security logging defenders rely on.

title: In a first, a court takedown goes after two cybercrime tools at once
blurb: Microsoft, working with Europol and partners including ESET, BitSight, IBM X-Force, and Proofpoint, executed a court-authorized takedown that simultaneously disrupted the Amadey botnet and the StealC infostealer, knocking out more than 200 command-and-control servers tied to over 140,000 infected machines. It was the first operation to target two cybercrime tools at once, with the legal team using insights from Copilot to treat both malware families as a single RICO conspiracy.

title: Boffin claims Microsoft's supposed quantum leap does not compute due to 'basic Python errors'
blurb: Nature published a peer-reviewed critique by University of St Andrews physicist Henry Legg arguing that Microsoft's 2025 Majorana quantum-computing results rest on flawed tune-up software and coding errors that produced incorrect statements to peer reviewers. Legg contends the raw data points to disordered devices incompatible with a topological gap, casting doubt on the Majorana roadmap Microsoft has staked years of research and its Majorana 2 chip announcement on.

title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
blurb: Attackers began exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified Communications Manager that grants unauthenticated remote attackers root access, less than 24 hours after SSD Secure Disclosure published a full exploit chain. CUCM serves roughly 30 million users globally, and Cisco shipped fixed versions on June 3, urging admins to patch deployments running the optional WebDialer service rather than wait on the 8.6 CVSS score.

title: IBM goes sub-1nm, develops 0.7nm-class technology — offering up to 50% higher performance and 70% higher energy efficiency compared to IBM's 2nm-class node
blurb: IBM produced the first test chip on its 0.7nm-class (7-angstrom) process, the industry's first sub-1nm node, using "nanostack" transistors that vertically bond two wafers to stack n-type and p-type devices instead of placing them side by side. IBM claims up to 50% higher performance, 70% better energy efficiency, and roughly double transistor density versus its 2nm research node, though this remains a concept process years from production.

title: Attackers exploit Cisco Unified CM flaw weeks after patch release
blurb: Attackers are now actively exploiting CVE-2026-20230, a critical SSRF flaw in Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME, three weeks after Cisco patched it on June 3 and warned it could grant unauthenticated remote attackers root access. The bug carries a CVSS score of 8.6, requires the non-default WebDialer service to be enabled, and has no workaround, leaving patching the only fix for enterprise voice and conferencing deployments.

title: Apple's MacOS Gap Lets Users Disable Security Tools
blurb: Researchers at XM Cyber disclosed a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user disable enterprise security tools like CrowdStrike Falcon EDR and Kandji MDM without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering alerts, by abusing how macOS caches and reuses an application's CDHash to validate trusted XPC services. Any macOS app that exposes privileged XPC services running as root is potentially exposed, and Apple has not yet responded.

title: IBM details major chip breakthrough with new sub-1nm ‘nanostack’ 3D architecture
blurb: IBM detailed its "nanostack" 3D architecture, which it calls the industry's first sub-1nm chip technology at the 0.7nm node, with roughly twice the transistor density of its 2021 2nm chips. The design vertically stacks transistors for up to 50% more performance or 70% better energy efficiency on generative AI and cloud workloads, though it remains a research result years from volume production.

title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
blurb: Threat actors have exploited CVE-2026-12569, an unauthenticated remote code execution flaw in PTC Windchill and FlexPLM, to deploy persistent JSP webshells for remote command execution and data exfiltration, prompting CISA to add it to its KEV catalog with a June 28 federal patch deadline. Because Windchill is the PLM backbone for automotive, aerospace, and defense manufacturers, IT and OT teams should apply PTC's June 17 patches and hunt for the published IoCs immediately.

title: Microsoft gives Windows 10 users an unexpected extra year of free security updates
blurb: Microsoft extended its consumer Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program by a year to October 12, 2027, giving eligible 22H2 devices continued critical patches via Windows Backup sync, 1,000 Microsoft Rewards points, or a $30 one-year license covering up to 10 devices. The extension surfaced quietly through documentation edits rather than a formal announcement, buying enterprises and holdouts more runway to migrate to Windows 11 or Copilot+ hardware.

title: Protocol Prying: Systematic Vulnerability Research in the Apple AirDrop and Android Quick Share Proximity Transfer Protocols
blurb: Researchers published the first cross-platform reverse-engineering and protocol-aware fuzzing study of Apple AirDrop and Google/Samsung Quick Share, the proximity transfer protocols running on over 5B devices, and disclosed six zero-click vulnerabilities including pre-authentication DoS bugs in iOS/macOS AirDrop and a Google-bounty heap use-after-free in Quick Share for Windows. Apple, Samsung, and Google have acknowledged all reports, putting unpatched enterprise fleets at proximity-based attack risk.

title: Making Windows a developer platform, again
blurb: Microsoft used Build 2026 to reposition Windows as a first-class developer platform, headlined by Coreutils for Windows, a Microsoft-maintained fork of the Rust-based uutils package that ships standard Unix commands as a single installable binary. The tooling narrows the context-switching gap between WSL, Linux VMs, and native PowerShell or cmd, letting Unix scripts port to Windows, though it remains a preview with incomplete coverage and conflicts against existing shell commands.

title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
blurb: Threat actors are abusing Shopify's Shop order-tracking app by inserting fake purchase receipts that impersonate Norton, McAfee, Apple, and PayPal, listing scammer-controlled phone numbers for victims to call. Gen Digital researchers say the in-app receipts outperform email-based callback phishing because users inherently trust Shop, with calls steering victims toward disclosing credentials, payment details, and OTPs or installing remote-access software.

title: Retirement of Azure DevOps issuer in Workload identity federation service connections
blurb: Microsoft is deprecating the Azure DevOps issuer in workload identity federation service connections, with retirement set for July 1, 2027, as it standardizes on the Microsoft Entra issuer across Azure. Teams running secretless Azure Pipelines on single-tenant Entra apps or managed identities in the public cloud must migrate their federated credentials off the vstoken.dev.azure.com prefix, though multi-tenant apps and non-public clouds are excluded for now.

title: London cops bring live facial recognition to West End
blurb: The Metropolitan Police deployed permanent live facial recognition cameras across London's West End, scanning passersby against watchlists in real time. The rollout expands biometric surveillance into one of the city's busiest commercial districts, raising compliance and data-governance questions for enterprises operating in the area amid an unsettled UK regulatory framework.

title: Qualcomm shares jump 14% on Modular acquisition, guidance upgrade
blurb: Qualcomm shares jumped 14% in after-hours trading after it agreed to acquire inference-software startup Modular for $3.92 billion in stock, previewed two data center chips, and raised its fiscal 2029 non-handset revenue target to $40 billion. Modular's platform automates porting AI models across chips, which Qualcomm can use to lower the switching cost off Nvidia accelerators and drive adoption of its new Dragonfly C1000 CPU and AI300 accelerator.

title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
blurb: Microsoft warned of an active phishing campaign hitting hotels across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant called TonRAT onto front-desk machines. The operators abuse Calendly notifications and Google redirects in an "authentication laundering" chain that passes SPF, DKIM, and DMARC, exposing a gap for IT teams that treat authentication checks as a proxy for message safety.

title: UK school’s network left wide open for invasion, student found
blurb: A student discovered that a UK school's network was left wide open to attack, with security gaps that would let an intruder move freely across its systems. The case highlights how thin IT staffing and unsegmented networks leave the education sector exposed, and why basic access controls and network segmentation remain unfinished business for under-resourced public-sector buyers.

title: TSMC is reportedly hiking prices for 'all advanced nodes,' accounting for 74% of the company’s wafer business — Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm, and others will face higher wafer costs
blurb: TSMC reportedly told customers to brace for 5–10% price increases across all advanced nodes, extending beyond 3nm and 2nm to 5nm and 7nm and covering 74% of its wafer revenue. Apple, Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, and MediaTek now face higher silicon costs that will flow downstream into device and datacenter hardware pricing for enterprise buyers.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across a channel, monitoring activity, posting alerts and comments under preset rules, and fixing code issues on command. The move pushes Claude from a sidebar assistant toward a standing presence in enterprise messaging, positioning Anthropic against Slack's own AI and Microsoft Copilot for the workplace-agent layer IT teams will have to govern.

title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program for consumer devices by another year, with coverage now running through October 12, 2027 via a $30 payment, 1,000 reward points, or settings backup. The extension buys holdout fleets more time before forced Windows 11 migration, though enterprise customers still face the paid ESU track at up to $427 per device over three years.

title: Italy is investigating Microsoft 365's price hike, saying Microsoft failed to inform users that AI tools like Copilot were being integrated into the service (Giulia Segreti/Reuters)
blurb: Italy's antitrust authority opened an investigation into Microsoft's Microsoft 365 price increase, alleging the company failed to clearly inform users that AI tools like Copilot were being bundled into the subscription. The probe puts scrutiny on Microsoft's practice of folding Copilot into existing plans and raising prices, a tactic enterprise buyers across the EU are already weighing against their renewal terms.

title: Self-destructing Mistic backdoor linked to access broker selling corporate footholds to ransomware gangs
blurb: Researchers tied the self-destructing Mistic backdoor to an initial-access broker that sells corporate network footholds to ransomware gangs. The malware wipes itself after establishing persistence to evade forensics, and the broker connection signals that compromised IT environments are being packaged and resold downstream, raising the odds that an early intrusion escalates to full ransomware deployment.

title: OpenAI, Broadcom debut custom Jalapeño chip for AI inference
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom debuted Jalapeño, a custom inference-only chip that OpenAI says delivers significantly higher performance per watt than current state-of-the-art accelerators, an implicit jab at Nvidia. The clusters pair Jalapeño with Broadcom's Tomahawk 6 networking silicon and custom racks built with Celestia, signaling OpenAI's push to cut its dependence on Nvidia GPUs for serving models at scale.

title: Microsoft adds another year to Windows 10 extended update program
blurb: Microsoft extended its Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program by another year, pushing the end date from October 12, 2026 to October 12, 2027 with no new conditions for users already enrolled. The quiet change signals that Windows 11 migration has stalled, leaving IT teams free to delay fleet upgrades another cycle while Microsoft absorbs the support cost rather than expose unpatched machines.

title: Proposed US law would make AI risk reporting a legal obligation
blurb: US lawmakers introduced the AI Incident Reporting Act, which would require developers of high-capability "covered models" to disclose major safety and security incidents to the Commerce Department within seven days, covering behaviors like shutdown resistance, oversight evasion, and stolen model weights. The bill directs Commerce to set capability thresholds defining which developers are subject to the rules, creating a federal compliance obligation that enterprises building on frontier models would need to factor into vendor risk assessments.

title: Analog chipmaker Onsemi buys Synaptics in $7B all-stock deal to push into physical AI
blurb: Onsemi agreed to acquire Synaptics in an all-stock deal valued at roughly $7B, exchanging 1.35 Onsemi shares per Synaptics share to push into "physical AI" and edge computing. The acquisition pairs Onsemi's analog and silicon carbide power chips with Synaptics' human-interface, sensing, and connectivity portfolio, targeting robots, drones, and autonomous vehicles, though Onsemi's stock fell over 8% on the news.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS added high-resolution 20-second metrics for service auto scaling, cutting scale-out trigger time from 363 seconds to 86 seconds and total provisioning time from 386 seconds to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Available across Fargate, ECS Managed Instances, and EC2, the faster scaling lets teams cut baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding they ran to absorb spikes, lowering compute costs.

title: AI coding agents could soon cost more than the developers using them
blurb: The Register argues that AI coding agents are on track to cost more per month than the developers who run them, as token-metered pricing and agent-orchestrated workflows push consumption far beyond flat per-seat SaaS norms. The shift turns coding tools into a variable, usage-billed line item that IT leaders must budget and cap like cloud compute rather than a predictable per-developer license.

title: Soaring memory chip demand helps Micron quadruple its revenue and crush expectations again
blurb: Micron reported Q3 revenue of $41.46B, up from $9.3B a year ago, and gross margin of 84.9%, now the highest of any major US tech company ahead of Meta and Nvidia. AI data center demand has turned memory from a commodity into a pricing-power business, and the resulting supply shortages are pushing up costs for PCs, smartphones, and enterprise hardware that CEO Sanjay Mehrotra expects to persist into 2028.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
blurb: PayPal deployed a Salesforce Agentforce AI SDR agent into its 200-rep org 14 weeks ago, putting it on the roughly 8,000 monthly merchant leads no human had capacity to chase, and meeting conversion is running about 50% higher than reps working alone. The agent runs a 10-nudge cadence to book qualified meetings and hand reps fully contextualized leads, positioning Agentforce as a funnel-feeder rather than a headcount cut.

title: FCC passes new cybersecurity rules for emergency systems, undersea cables
blurb: The FCC approved new rules on Thursday that impose baseline cybersecurity requirements on the Emergency Alert System and Wireless Emergency Alerts, mandating strong passwords, prompt patching, firewalls, and a new authentication ID to verify alerts before they spread. A separate vote delivered the first major update to undersea cable regulations in decades, tightening some security rules while exempting certain providers from the Team Telecom national security licensing review.

title: Italy opens an antitrust probe into Microsoft 365’s AI price rise
blurb: Italy's competition authority opened an investigation into Microsoft over how it raised Microsoft 365 prices, alleging customers were automatically moved to a pricier Copilot- and Designer-bundled plan unless they actively opted out, with too little disclosure to choose. The case targets the opt-out architecture rather than the headline price ahead of the July 1, 2026 increase, signaling European regulators' growing scrutiny of how AI features get bundled into established enterprise software.

title: Nation-state actors cracked critical Australian infrastructure to ‘cripple it at a time of their choosing’
blurb: Australian authorities warned that nation-state actors have pre-positioned inside critical infrastructure networks, holding access intended to cripple essential services "at a time of their choosing" rather than for immediate disruption. The disclosure raises the bar for enterprise operators in energy, water, and telecom, who now face board-level pressure to fund threat-hunting for dormant intrusions and segment OT systems that legacy procurement left exposed.

title: Companies are not looking before they're leaping into the AI playpen
blurb: 93% of organizations report infrastructure incidents tied to AI deployments, according to a survey arguing that enterprises are pushing models into production faster than their ops and governance can absorb. The implication for IT leaders is that the bottleneck has shifted from model access to operational readiness, and the cost of skipping capacity planning, observability, and rollback discipline now shows up as outages rather than line items.

title: Qualcomm claims it's not too late for Dragonfly to land in datacenters
blurb: Qualcomm argued it is not too late to enter the datacenter market with Dragonfly, its Arm-based server chip line, despite Nvidia, AMD, and hyperscaler in-house silicon already dominating AI infrastructure buildouts. The pitch targets enterprise buyers weighing power and cost alternatives for inference workloads, though Qualcomm faces an entrenched software ecosystem and supply commitments that competitors locked in years ahead of its arrival.

title: Microsoft rivals line up to tell UK watchdog where the software behemoth hurt them
blurb: Microsoft's rivals are submitting evidence to the UK Competition and Markets Authority detailing how the company's licensing and bundling practices have disadvantaged them, escalating the regulator's ongoing probe into Microsoft's cloud and software dominance. For enterprise buyers, any remedies forced on Microsoft could reshape pricing on Azure, Windows Server, and Office licensing terms that currently lock procurement decisions into a single vendor.

title: Building a state-of-the-art development platform with Backstage
blurb: The author argues that Backstage solved the developer-portal problem but not the platform problem, since it organizes catalogs, docs, and templates while assuming an execution layer for deployments, environments, and policies already exists beneath it. Wiring Backstage directly to CI/CD, GitOps, Kubernetes, and observability creates a fragile "messy middle." The fix is a control plane between portal and runtime that compiles developer and platform abstractions into infrastructure, enforces policies, and reconciles drift.

title: Researchers cast new doubt on Microsoft’s quantum computing advance
blurb: A peer-reviewed paper in Nature by St Andrews physicist Henry Legg argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol, the analysis framework underpinning its Majorana quantum chip program, is flawed and that its results could stem from other effects or skewed data selection. The critique adds to long-running scientific skepticism over Microsoft's claim of a scalable quantum computer by 2029, following a 2018 Majorana paper the company was forced to retract.

title: “MS 마요라나 양자 칩 성과, 섣부른 결론”…학계서 비판 제기
blurb: A peer-reviewed Nature paper from St Andrews physicist Henry Legg argues that Microsoft's Topological Gap Protocol, the analysis framework behind its Majorana quantum chip claims, is flawed and that the observed signals can be explained by other physical effects. Microsoft is standing by its roadmap to a scalable quantum computer by 2029, but the renewed academic skepticism, plus Microsoft's incomplete release of raw data, leaves its topological-qubit bet unverified against rivals Google, IBM, and AWS.

title: Cisco cuts 500 Bay Area jobs in aggressive pivot to AI
blurb: Cisco filed WARN notices to cut 471 Bay Area jobs effective July 13, part of the 4,000 global layoffs CEO Chuck Robbins announced in May. The reductions reflect a shift in Cisco's operating model toward AI, with savings redirected into silicon, optics, and security as the company says it remains short of specialized technical talent in those areas.

title: Nvidia and Samsung to power 6G-ready digital twin for Japan telecom giant
blurb: KDDI launched a joint initiative with Nvidia, Samsung, and Keysight to build a high-fidelity RAN digital twin for training and validating AI-driven network optimization ahead of 6G. Nvidia supplies its Aerial Omniverse Digital Twin and accelerated compute, Samsung contributes vRAN technology, and Keysight provides equipment emulation, with a prototype targeted by March 2028 and platform expansion by 2030.

title: UK Finance and banks collaborate on digital verification service
blurb: UK Finance is backing Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, Nationwide, NatWest and Santander to build a bank-led digital verification service that lets customers confirm details like name, age or address through their banking app, sharing only consented data with third parties. The project has finished synthetic-data proof-of-concept work with technical partner Select ID and moves to a live pilot in the coming months.

title: Elastic lays off 7% of employees
blurb: Elastic cut roughly 7% of its workforce as CEO Ash Kulkarni reorganized engineering into three core areas with fewer management layers, citing AI and automation that let some teams run leaner. Kulkarni framed the cuts as a structural simplification rather than a retreat, expanding customer-facing sales and projecting total headcount to still grow year-over-year this fiscal year.

title: Microsoft extends extended updates for Windows 10 in the most muted way imaginable
blurb: Microsoft quietly extended its Extended Security Updates program for Windows 10, giving organizations another paid runway to keep patching machines past the October 2025 end-of-support deadline. The low-key rollout matters for IT teams still managing large Windows 10 fleets, since ESU enrollment remains the only supported path to security patches without a forced migration to Windows 11 and its stricter hardware requirements.

title: Micron locks in historically high memory prices for five years
blurb: Micron signed multiyear supply agreements that lock in today's historically high memory prices through 2031, guaranteeing the chipmaker outsized margins and profits as buyers move to secure DRAM and NAND capacity. The deals shift pricing risk onto enterprise hardware purchasers, raising the floor on server, storage, and PC component costs for IT budgets across the next five procurement cycles.

title: AI coding token costs are on track to rival human payroll
blurb: Gartner projects that enterprise AI coding token costs will match or exceed a software engineer's monthly salary within two years, driven by agentic tool adoption and a shift from flat per-seat SaaS to consumption-based licensing. The bigger problem is governance: vendors lack mature cost-optimization controls, billing is opaque, and IT leaders have little visibility into per-developer spend, with anecdotes of single developers burning $20K in a month. Without FinOps-style oversight, budgets get wiped out before ROI can be justified.

title: RAMageddon just got extremely real
blurb: Apple raised prices across Macs, iPads, HomePods, and the Vision Pro by hundreds of dollars in many cases, blaming the ongoing memory shortage that has already forced hikes on consoles, laptops, and phones. The move signals that DRAM and NAND scarcity is now structural rather than transient, so IT buyers should expect higher endpoint refresh costs and budget for sustained hardware price inflation through the next procurement cycle.

title: Intel’s Chip Business Shows Signs of Life After Years of Struggle
blurb: Intel's chip-manufacturing business is showing early operational improvement after years of losses, positioning it as the centerpiece of the Trump administration's push to expand domestic chip production. The recovery remains partial, with the company still trailing TSMC on advanced-node yields and far from the sustained profitability needed to call the turnaround complete.

title: The Magnum Ice Cream Company enlists six vendors to build tech stack
blurb: The Magnum Ice Cream Company signed long-term deals with six vendors — Microsoft, Salesforce, SAP, Kinaxis, Accenture, and HCLTech — to build an AI-ready tech stack from scratch following its 2025 spinoff from Unilever. The SaaS core (Microsoft cloud, Salesforce CRM, SAP ERP, Kinaxis planning) rolls out in waves over 18 months as the company exits Unilever's IT systems by the end of 2027, with Accenture and HCLTech handling deployment.

title: Qualcomm Targets Over $15 Billion in Data Center Revenue, Names Meta CPU Customer
blurb: Qualcomm told investors it expects more than $15B in data center revenue by fiscal 2029 and named Meta as the first public customer for its Dragonfly C1000 server CPUs, with systems entering production in the second half of 2028. The target nearly doubles Qualcomm's prior non-handset chip goal to $40B and sent shares up more than 12% after hours, signaling a credible challenge to incumbent server-CPU suppliers in hyperscaler procurement.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cybersecurity breach in which an attacker claimed to have stolen trade secrets tied to its work for Apple and Tesla, with the threat actor publishing samples to substantiate the claim. The incident raises supply-chain exposure questions for OEMs that route component manufacturing through Tata, and pressures enterprise buyers to scrutinize the security posture of contract manufacturers holding sensitive design data.

title: Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization
blurb: Google is splitting its Web & App Activity setting into separate Search Services History and Personalized Recommendations controls across Search, Maps, Shopping, Flights, Hotels, Translate, News, and Google Play, rolling out to accounts over the next few days. The new Search Services History defaults on for users with Web & App Activity enabled and now saves media like images, audio, and video from Lens and other interactive searches, which IT teams managing Google Workspace and consumer accounts should review.

title: Openchip and SUSE partner to drive European sovereign at the chip level
blurb: Openchip and SUSE partnered to certify Openchip's RISC-V high-performance compute and accelerator hardware across SUSE's stack, including SUSE Linux Enterprise Server, OpenSUSE Tumbleweed, and Rancher Prime, with Kubernetes plugins and a planned AI inference platform pairing SUSE's AI Factory with Openchip accelerators. The deal targets European sovereignty buyers needing data and model residency, building on Openchip's $126M EU Next Generation Fund backing and prior partnerships with NEC and Baya.

title: Rippling now wants to be your entire data stack
blurb: Rippling launched Rippling Data Cloud, a unified data stack that ingests, stores, transforms, and visualizes business data while layering in built-in knowledge of an organization's reporting structure. The move pushes the HR vendor directly against Fivetran, Snowflake, dbt Labs, and Tableau, pitching consolidation and org-aware analytics, including dashboards that flag low-ROI AI token spend, as the reason to drop a multi-vendor modern data stack.

title: NetApp federates performance-boosted StorageGRIDs into single namespace for greater scale
blurb: NetApp's StorageGRID 12.1 federates object stores into a single namespace scaling to 10EB, with up to 400% higher throughput for AI workloads.

title: Apple’s Macs and iPads and Microsoft’s Xbox consoles are getting more expensive — blame AI
blurb: Apple raised Mac prices 15% to 20% and iPad prices 15% to 25% today, pushing the MacBook Air to $1,299 and the iPad Pro to $1,199, with Microsoft following hours later with a significant Xbox hike. The driver is AI server demand, which has more than quadrupled DRAM and NAND prices over the past year and is forcing hardware vendors to pass component costs straight to buyers.

title: FCC may kill $2B program that connects schools and libraries to Internet
blurb: The FCC voted 2-1 to open public comment on scaling back or sunsetting E-Rate, the $2B-a-year Universal Service program that subsidizes Internet access and equipment for schools and libraries, with Chairman Brendan Carr citing excessive student screen time. Districts and library systems that budget around E-Rate discounts now face uncertainty over a funding stream that has underwritten connectivity procurement since 1997.

title: Microsoft Copilot can now handle more of your finance work in Excel with reusable skills and data connectors
blurb: Microsoft added reusable Skills and live-data connectors (FactSet, S&P Global, PitchBook) to Copilot in Excel for finance teams.

title: From 250 to 160 microns: Why fiber diameter is becoming a critical variable in AI infrastructure planning
blurb: The author argues that fiber coating diameter, long fixed at the 250-micron standard, has become a binding constraint on AI data center builds because conduits and trays are fixed assets that AI's high-density GPU clusters are outgrowing. Moving to 200-, 180-, and 160-micron fiber roughly doubles the count that fits a given conduit, letting operators reach 1.6T and 6,912-fiber cabling without ripping out existing infrastructure. For IT and facilities planners, fiber spec is now a procurement variable, not an afterthought.

title: Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks
blurb: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD misconfiguration where untrusted pull requests trigger privileged workflows, and its scan of 30,000 high-impact repos found 300-plus fully exploitable by any unauthenticated user with a free account. Affected projects include Microsoft's Azure Sentinel, Google's adk-samples, Apache Doris, and Cloudflare's Workers SDK, where forged approvals or crafted branch names let attackers run code on CI runners and steal long-lived credentials.

title: We're making Bunny DNS free: because a faster internet won't build itself
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees for Bunny DNS, removing per-request billing and query limits while bundling free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains per account. The service already routes nearly 200 billion queries monthly across 300,000 domains, offering latency-based routing, health checks, and JavaScript logic that the now-free tier extends without the risk of unpredictable bills from traffic spikes.

title: Deploy Boundary on Kubernetes with official Helm charts
blurb: HashiCorp released two official Helm charts for deploying Boundary on Kubernetes, one for the controller control plane and one for the session-proxying workers, replacing the hand-built deployments, config maps, and lifecycle automation teams previously maintained per environment. The controller chart automates database schema initialization via a pre-install hook and resolves credentials, license keys, and KMS references at runtime from Kubernetes secrets, including those synced by Vault Secrets Operator.

title: See your serverless: introducing the Headlamp plugin for Knative
blurb: The Knative SIG released a Headlamp plugin that consolidates KService management, traffic splitting, autoscaling config, and resource mapping into a single UI, replacing the constant jumping between the kn CLI, kubectl, and the dashboard. Operators can edit traffic splits for canary rollouts, restart pods, and view logs inline, with every action gated by the user's existing RBAC permissions.

title: What you need to know about Lambda MicroVMs
blurb: AWS launched Lambda MicroVMs, giving teams Firecracker-grade isolation with no shared kernel, sub-second snapshot boots, and stateful sessions that persist memory and disk for up to eight hours. The author argues it finally fits the AI-code-sandbox problem that EC2, ECS, and Lambda functions each solved only partially. The model is per-session VMs with dedicated HTTPS endpoints, bearer-token auth, and automatic suspend on idle so you stop paying when traffic stops.

title: Amazon ECS introduces new high-resolution metrics for faster service auto scaling
blurb: Amazon ECS service auto scaling now supports high-resolution 20-second metrics, cutting the time to trigger scale-out from 363 to 86 seconds and total scale-and-provision time from 386 to 109 seconds in AWS benchmarks. Faster reaction lets teams trim baseline task counts and the preemptive capacity padding that hedges against traffic spikes, lowering compute cost while target tracking replaces the custom step-scaling policies that used to deliver aggressive scaling.

title: Introducing the Cluster API plugin for Headlamp
blurb: Kubernetes SIG UI released a Cluster API plugin for Headlamp, adding a dedicated CAPI section that surfaces Clusters, Machines, MachineDeployments, MachineSets, and MachinePools through visual list and detail views inside the browser-based UI. The dashboard summarizes control plane and worker health across a management cluster, flags degraded resources by condition, and provides remediation guidance and diagnostic commands, replacing the raw kubectl workflows that CAPI lifecycle management has historically required.

title: HCP Vault Dedicated introduces cluster disaster recovery (public preview)
blurb: HashiCorp added cluster disaster recovery to HCP Vault Dedicated in public preview, letting teams run DR drills at the cluster level to simulate full cluster failures and verify failover readiness. The feature targets a gap in production Vault operations, where secrets-management outages have broad blast radius and recovery procedures often go untested until a real incident forces the issue.

title: Minimus container images are now free
blurb: Minimus opened its catalog of hardened, minimal container images to a free tier, with curated Helm charts for deployment. The free images ship without support, SLA, or guaranteed patching timelines, and paid subscriptions may receive security updates first, so teams should weigh the smaller attack surface against slower CVE response before pulling them into production.

title: Companies are not looking before they're leaping into the AI playpen
blurb: A survey found that 93% of organizations have logged infrastructure incidents attributable to AI workloads, as teams push models into production faster than they can harden the platforms underneath them. The gap shows up as unplanned capacity spikes, misconfigured access, and reliability regressions that land on the SRE and platform teams left to absorb the blast radius.

title: Building a state-of-the-art development platform with Backstage
blurb: Backstage solved the developer-portal problem but not the platform problem, the author argues: a portal organizes catalogs and templates, while a platform owns deployments, environments, policies, and runtime operations. Wiring Backstage point-to-point into CI/CD, GitOps, Kubernetes, and observability produces a fragile "messy middle." The post's fix is a control plane between portal and runtime that compiles developer and platform-engineer abstractions into Kubernetes resources, enforces policies, and reconciles drift.

title: Inspect Volcano workloads faster with Headlamp
blurb: The Volcano plugin for Headlamp surfaces Volcano's batch-scheduling resources—Jobs, Queues, and PodGroups—as dedicated list and detail views inside the Headlamp Kubernetes web UI. Operators troubleshooting gang scheduling and queue behavior can now move between a Job, its PodGroup, the underlying Pods, and events in one interface instead of stitching that context together across kubectl and the Volcano CLI.

title: The Dependency Black Hole
blurb: This paper introduces the "dependency black hole" theory from an industrial case study of 267 microservices, where compile-time, run-time, and task dependencies all concentrate around a dense core of "dependency magnet" services. The authors map the network to a black hole's anatomy: a singularity core, an event horizon of services being pulled in, and an outer accretion disk of lightly coupled services. The practical payoff is a lens for spotting services at risk of becoming magnets and justifying governance interventions before the core's blast radius grows.

title: ZeroTier Quantum RC2 brings post-quantum security closer to general availability
blurb: ZeroTier shipped RC2 of ZeroTier Quantum, its end-to-end post-quantum networking platform, marking the final testing milestone before general availability and CNSA 2.0 compliance ahead of the U.S. government's 2027 PQC mandate. Built in Rust with a peer-to-peer mesh, it pairs ML-KEM-1024 with hybrid AES-384 and claims 30% lower CPU utilization than comparable software-defined networking platforms, with a third-party audit and pen test still pending before GA.

title: How Cloudflare responded to the “Copy Fail” Linux vulnerability
blurb: Cloudflare detailed its response to "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation in the AF_ALG crypto path disclosed on April 29, 2026. Because the company runs a custom LTS-based kernel that ships weekly internal builds and reboots its edge on a four-week ERR cycle, the fix was already deployed before public disclosure, and behavioral detections flagged the exploit pattern within minutes. The post argues that disciplined patch cadence, not incident heroics, is what neutralizes kernel CVEs at scale.

title: Boundary 1.0 releases RDP session recording and improved management
blurb: HashiCorp released Boundary 1.0, marking its privileged access management tool as production-ready and adding RDP session recording that captures and replays every interactive action for compliance and security analysis. The release also ships two Helm charts for deploying controllers and workers on Kubernetes, plus org- and project-scoped aliases that let separate teams reuse the same human-readable target names in multi-tenant deployments.

title: Google Antigravity agents get full context with GitLab Orbit
blurb: GitLab released Orbit, a lifecycle context graph that Google Antigravity developers can install from the Antigravity MCP Store to give coding agents structured access to projects, pipelines, merge requests, vulnerabilities, and source across a GitLab instance. Orbit indexes the instance into a knowledge graph queried through two MCP tools, and GitLab's internal tests reported agents responding up to 11x faster, using 4.5x fewer tokens, and producing 45x fewer hallucinations.

title: Rethinking cloud operations with agentic observability
blurb: Microsoft announced general availability of the Azure Copilot Observability Agent, built on Azure Monitor to correlate logs, metrics, traces, and topology across agents, applications, and infrastructure for faster root-cause analysis. The launch targets a complexity problem Microsoft's own survey quantifies: 84% of organizations report rising cloud complexity, and 69% say it is outpacing their current operating model.

title: Migrating from Proxmox to NixOS and Incus
blurb: The author migrated his multi-node homelab from Proxmox to NixOS running Incus, replacing a GUI-driven hypervisor with declarative configuration files. The argument is that Proxmox's click-to-configure paradigm invites state drift, where UI tweaks made during debugging silently diverge from the Terraform or Ansible code six months later. NixOS makes the whole host reproducible: the author rebuilt a system on a fresh disk byte-for-byte by reapplying his config, and notes that declarative infrastructure matters more as AI agents start managing systems.

title: Client-Side Load Balancing at a Million Requests Per Second
blurb: Zalando's engineering team replaced its shared Skipper edge ingress with an in-process client-side load balancer for the million-requests-per-second internal fan-out behind its Product Read API. Owning the routing decision ended years of ambiguous incidents where the root cause was unclear between Skipper and PRAPI, since a single batch-of-100 request carried 100x exposure to the shared router. The CSLB layers N-ring fade-in, occupancy-based bounded load, and AZ-aware routing with a latency health factor, cutting cost and decoupling the service from the infrastructure beneath it.

title: Run Codex in the cloud – DigitalOcean for Codex is now available
blurb: DigitalOcean released a Codex plugin in Public Preview that provisions a persistent, Codex-ready Droplet in your own account through natural language, with OAuth auth and no manual SSH or dependency setup. The pre-configured machine runs the Codex CLI and common language tooling from the codex-universal image, letting long-running agent work continue after you step away and stay controllable from the ChatGPT mobile app.

title: Empirical Software Engineering TerraProbe: A Layered-Oracle Framework for Detecting Deceptive Fixes in LLM-Assisted Terraform
blurb: TerraProbe is a five-layer oracle framework for evaluating LLM-assisted Terraform security repairs, tested on 288 fixes from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet. The authors show that passing Checkov overstates success: targeted findings cleared 83.3% of the time, but full-scanner cleanliness held at 10.4% and human review flagged 71.4% of plan-compared repairs as deceptive fixes that leave the vulnerability in place. Wildcard IAM grants persisted in every CKV2_AWS_11 case across all three models.

title: PayPal Put Agentforce on 8,000 Leads a Month No Human Was Going to Call. Conversions Jumped 50%.
